explanation: Architectural views represent different perspectives for different stakeholders.

text: What is the 4+1 View Model?
options: A comprehensive software testing model and plan by the organization at every stage
A relational database normalization model type across all phases in a systematic way
An architecture description using Logical, Process, Development, Physical views plus Scenarios
Five physical computer display monitors together as a standard approach
explanation: The 4+1 View Model uses Logical, Process, Development, Physical views, unified by Scenarios.

text: What is a message broker in architecture?
options: A general-purpose programming language and tool within the system boundary
A specific type of database storage system tool according to best practices
An intermediary program that translates messages between different systems
A person who physically delivers messages by hand for the project goals
explanation: A message broker enables communication between decoupled systems.

text: What is software maintenance?

text: What is version control?

To install electrical wiring in office buildings
To manufacture physical computer hardware units
explanation: Software engineers design, develop, test, and maintain software systems.

text: What does 'scalability' mean for a software system?
options: Requirement to rewrite code for every new feature
Tendency to decrease in performance under any load
Ability to handle growing amounts of work efficiently
Capability to run on a single device without issues
explanation: Scalability is the ability of a system to handle increasing workload without degradation.

text: Which of the following best describes a software prototype?
options: The final production release of the application
An early working model of the proposed system
A hardware device used during system testing
A legal contract between client and developers
explanation: A prototype is an early, incomplete version of a system used to explore requirements.

text: What is the difference between verification and validation?
options: Verification writes documentation; validation writes test code
Verification deploys software; validation monitors production
Verification checks correctness; validation checks usefulness
Verification tests performance; validation tests user interface
explanation: Verification ensures the product is built correctly; validation ensures the right product is built.

text: Which factor most contributes to the software crisis?
options: Decreasing cost of modern hardware and peripherals
Growing availability of trained software professionals
Increasing system complexity and changing requirements
Widespread adoption of standardized coding practices
explanation: The software crisis is primarily driven by growing complexity and evolving requirements.

text: What is the significance of the NATO 1968 conference?
options: It launched the initial version of the world wide web
It formally introduced software engineering as a discipline
It created the first commercial operating system product
It established the first programming language standard
explanation: The 1968 NATO conference is credited with coining and formalizing 'software engineering.'

text: How does software engineering differ from programming?
options: It focuses only on writing source code in languages
It deals exclusively with database design and queries
It encompasses the entire development lifecycle process
It concerns itself primarily with hardware maintenance
explanation: Software engineering covers requirements, design, testing, and maintenance beyond just coding.

text: What is the purpose of a feasibility study in software projects?
options: To write the final source code for the application
To conduct user acceptance testing after development
To deploy the software to the production environment
To determine if the project is viable and worthwhile
explanation: A feasibility study evaluates technical, economic, and operational viability of a proposed project.

text: Which software characteristic ensures it works across platforms?
options: Obscurity of the source code implementation
Dependency on a single hardware configuration
Portability across different operating environments
Complexity of the underlying algorithm design
explanation: Portability allows software to operate in different computing environments.

text: What is meant by 'technical debt' in software engineering?
options: The monetary budget allocated for purchasing hardware
Revenue generated from software licensing agreements
Total salary expenses for the development team members
Accumulated cost of shortcuts taken during development
explanation: Technical debt is the implied cost of rework caused by choosing quick solutions over better approaches.

text: Why are software engineering ethics considered important?
options: They guide responsible behavior in professional practice
They reduce the physical size of deployed software
They increase the execution speed of the application
They eliminate the need for comprehensive testing
explanation: Ethics ensure software engineers act responsibly and consider societal impacts of their work.

text: What is the relationship between software and hardware engineering?
options: Both disciplines use identical processes and methodologies
Software engineering is a direct subset of hardware engineering
Hardware engineering completely depends on software practices
Software engineering builds on but differs from hardware methods
explanation: Software engineering borrows some principles from hardware engineering but has distinct methods.

text: What is the primary benefit of using CASE tools?
options: They automate and support software development activities
They convert hardware specifications into software code
They eliminate all possible defects from the source code
They replace the need for any human software developers
explanation: CASE tools automate various software engineering activities to improve productivity and quality.

text: A company is experiencing frequent project delays and cost overruns. Which fundamental software engineering principle would most directly address this?
options: Hiring additional developers without changing methodology
Reducing documentation to accelerate coding activities
Adopting structured process models with clear milestones
Purchasing more powerful servers for the development team
explanation: Structured process models with milestones help track progress and control schedule and budget.

text: In Lehman's laws of software evolution, what does the law of continuing change state?
options: Software quality improves automatically without any effort
Software maintenance effort reduces with each new release
Software complexity decreases naturally over time periods
Software must continually adapt or become less satisfactory
explanation: Lehman's law states that software must be continually adapted to remain satisfactory in its environment.

text: A team finds that fixing one bug consistently introduces two new defects. Which concept best explains this phenomenon?
options: Software entropy and increasing system complexity over time
Effective regression testing preventing all defect propagation
Proper documentation reducing overall maintenance difficulty
Successful modular design isolating component dependencies
explanation: Software entropy describes the tendency of software to become increasingly disordered over time.

text: When applying the 'separation of concerns' principle, what is the primary architectural benefit achieved?
options: Documentation requirements are completely eliminated from work
Each module addresses a distinct feature independently of others
Testing becomes unnecessary due to simplified code structure
All system functionality is consolidated into a single module
explanation: Separation of concerns divides a system into distinct features with minimal overlap between modules.

text: A startup must deliver a product quickly with uncertain requirements. Which combination of software engineering principles is most appropriate?
options: Comprehensive upfront documentation with rigid phase milestones
Iterative development with frequent stakeholder feedback cycles
Big bang integration testing after all modules are completed
Waterfall model with complete requirements before any coding
explanation: Iterative development with stakeholder feedback best handles uncertainty and time pressure.

text: What is Brooks' law and how does it impact late software projects?
options: Late projects benefit most from adding experienced engineers
Doubling the team size will exactly halve the delivery time
Adding people to a late project makes it even later overall
Project delays are always caused by insufficient team members
explanation: Brooks' law states that adding manpower to a late project increases communication overhead and delays.

text: In the context of sociotechnical systems, why must software engineers consider organizational factors?
options: Organizational factors have no measurable impact on software quality
Technical solutions must align with organizational processes and culture
Software operates independently of any human or organizational context
Engineering decisions should be based solely on technical merit alone
explanation: Sociotechnical systems theory recognizes that software success depends on both technical and organizational factors.

text: A legacy system handles critical operations but uses obsolete technology. What is the recommended engineering approach?
options: Ignoring the system until it fails completely in production
Complete system rewrite without referencing existing functionality
Immediate shutdown followed by building from scratch entirely
Incremental modernization while maintaining system availability
explanation: Incremental modernization allows updating technology while preserving critical business operations.

text: What distinguishes emergent system properties from designed properties in software engineering?
options: Emergent properties arise from component interactions not individual parts
Emergent properties can be fully predicted from individual components
Designed properties only appear after system deployment to production
Emergent properties are explicitly defined in requirements documentation
explanation: Emergent properties arise from interactions between components and cannot be attributed to any single part.

text: How does Conway's law relate software architecture to organizational structure?
options: Technical architecture solely determines how teams are organized
Software architecture is always independent of team organization
Organizational hierarchy has no influence on system module design
System designs mirror the communication structure of the organization
explanation: Conway's law states that system designs tend to mirror the communication structures of organizations.

text: What is the waterfall model in software development?
options: A random unstructured method without any planning steps
A circular iterative approach with no defined ending point
A sequential linear development approach with distinct phases
A parallel development technique using multiple teams only
explanation: The waterfall model follows a sequential approach where each phase must be completed before the next begins.

text: Which phase typically follows design in the waterfall model?
options: Implementation and coding
System deployment step
Requirements gathering
Project initiation phase
explanation: In the waterfall model, implementation follows the design phase.

text: What is an iterative development process?
options: Repeating development cycles to refine the software product
Skipping testing phases to accelerate delivery timelines
Completing all development in a single pass without changes
Eliminating design activities to focus on coding tasks
explanation: Iterative development repeats cycles of development to progressively refine the product.

text: What does the V-model emphasize in software development?
options: Eliminating all forms of documentation from the process
Combining all phases into a single continuous activity
Removing verification activities to save project time
Corresponding testing phase for each development stage
explanation: The V-model pairs each development phase with a corresponding testing phase.

text: Which model allows returning to previous phases if needed?
options: Incremental model
Pure waterfall model
Big bang model only
Code and fix model
explanation: The incremental model allows revisiting and refining earlier phases as needed.

text: What is the main advantage of using a process model?
options: It eliminates the need for skilled software developers
It guarantees the software will have zero defect count
It removes all project risks from the development work
It provides structure and predictability to development
explanation: Process models provide structure, predictability, and a framework for managing development.

text: In which model is software delivered in small functional pieces?
options: Ad hoc coding method
Big bang development way
Incremental delivery model
Pure waterfall approach
explanation: Incremental delivery releases software in small, functional increments over time.

text: What is a spiral model primarily concerned with?
options: Eliminating documentation
Avoiding testing phases
Reducing team size needs
Risk analysis and management
explanation: The spiral model emphasizes risk analysis at each iteration of the development process.

text: What is the prototyping model used for?
options: Replacing all testing with user observation sessions
Building early versions to clarify system requirements
Eliminating the need for any design documentation
Skipping requirements and jumping to final deployment
explanation: Prototyping builds early versions to help clarify and validate requirements with stakeholders.

text: Which model is best suited for well-understood requirements?
options: Spiral model technique
Prototyping model plan
Waterfall model approach
Agile methodology way
explanation: The waterfall model works best when requirements are well-understood and unlikely to change.

text: What is the key difference between incremental and iterative models?
options: Incremental refines features; iterative adds new functionality
Both approaches are completely identical in every possible way
Neither approach involves any form of planning or documentation
Incremental adds functionality; iterative refines existing features
explanation: Incremental adds new functionality in each cycle; iterative refines and improves existing features.

text: In the spiral model, what happens at the end of each spiral loop?
options: Testing is permanently skipped for all remaining spiral loops
The entire project is immediately deployed to production servers
A review determines whether to continue to the next iteration
All source code is discarded and rewritten from scratch again
explanation: Each spiral loop ends with a review to evaluate progress and plan the next iteration.

text: Why might a team choose the RAD model for development?
options: When no user involvement is needed throughout development
When requirements are completely fixed and will never change
When the project has unlimited budget and timeline available
When rapid delivery of a working system is the top priority
explanation: RAD (Rapid Application Development) prioritizes quick delivery through prototyping and iterative development.

text: What is a disadvantage of the waterfall model?
options: Excessive flexibility leading to uncontrolled scope expansion
Difficulty accommodating requirement changes after phase completion
Lack of any structured phases making progress hard to track
Too many iterative cycles causing confusion among developers
explanation: The waterfall model's rigidity makes it difficult to accommodate changes once a phase is completed.

text: How does the unified process (UP) organize development activities?
options: Into four phases: inception, elaboration, construction, transition
Into two phases: planning and coding without any other steps
Into six phases that must always be executed sequentially only
Into a single continuous phase without any distinct milestones
explanation: The Unified Process has four phases: inception, elaboration, construction, and transition.

text: What role does risk assessment play in the spiral model?
options: It drives decisions about which activities to perform each cycle
It has no significant impact on how the project is conducted
It replaces all other engineering activities in the entire model
It is performed only once at the very beginning of the project
explanation: Risk assessment in the spiral model determines priorities and activities for each iteration.

text: When is the evolutionary development model most appropriate?
options: When initial requirements are unclear and need exploration work
When all requirements are perfectly defined before starting
When the project has a very short and fixed delivery deadline
When the team prefers no stakeholder interaction during work
explanation: Evolutionary development is suitable when requirements are unclear and need to be discovered through building.

text: What is the main criticism of the code-and-fix development approach?
options: It requires too much upfront planning and design documentation
It mandates excessive testing before any code is ever written
It leads to poorly structured software that is hard to maintain
It forces teams to follow overly rigid process model guidelines
explanation: Code-and-fix leads to poorly structured, hard-to-maintain software due to lack of systematic process.

text: What is the purpose of the elaboration phase in the Unified Process?
options: To deploy the completed software to end users in production now
To analyze the problem domain and establish architectural foundation
To gather initial business case and scope project requirements
To perform final acceptance testing before system goes live now
explanation: Elaboration analyzes the problem domain, mitigates risks, and establishes the architectural foundation.

text: How does the component-based development model reduce development effort?
options: By eliminating all testing and quality assurance activities fully
By avoiding any form of architectural design or documentation
By reusing pre-built and tested software components extensively
By skipping the requirements gathering phase of development work
explanation: Component-based development reuses pre-built, tested components to reduce development time and effort.

text: A government agency requires extensive documentation and formal sign-offs at each development stage. Which process model best fits this environment?
options: Waterfall model with formal reviews and documentation at each phase
Extreme programming with minimal documentation and rapid iterations
Scrum framework with two-week sprints and daily standup meetings
Kanban method with continuous flow and no predefined phase gates
explanation: The waterfall model's sequential phases with formal documentation and sign-offs suit regulated environments.

text: A team is building a safety-critical system for an aircraft. Why would the V-model be preferred over agile approaches?
options: V-model ensures rigorous verification and validation at each level
V-model eliminates the need for any form of requirements analysis
Agile approaches always produce higher quality safety-critical code
Agile methods provide more thorough documentation for regulators
explanation: The V-model's emphasis on verification and validation at each level is critical for safety-critical systems.

text: In the spiral model, how does the team determine the scope of each iteration?
options: By implementing all remaining features in every single spiral loop
By evaluating identified risks and prioritizing their mitigation efforts
By following a fixed predetermined schedule regardless of any risks
By randomly selecting features from the product backlog for building
explanation: Risk evaluation drives scope decisions in each spiral iteration, focusing on mitigating the highest risks.

text: A project started with the waterfall model but requirements keep changing. What is the most pragmatic transition strategy?
options: Shift to an incremental model preserving completed phase artifacts
Continue with waterfall and reject all incoming change requests
Abandon process models entirely and use ad hoc development now
Restart the entire project from scratch using a different language
explanation: Transitioning to incremental development preserves existing work while accommodating ongoing changes.

text: What is the primary challenge of using evolutionary prototyping for large-scale enterprise systems?
options: Enterprise systems cannot use any iterative development technique
Large systems never benefit from any prototyping approach at all
Prototypes may evolve into poorly architected production systems
Evolutionary prototyping always produces perfectly structured code
explanation: Evolutionary prototypes risk becoming production systems with poor architecture and technical debt.

text: How does the Rational Unified Process (RUP) handle changing requirements compared to traditional plan-driven approaches?
options: RUP accommodates changes through iterative development within structured phases
RUP completely prevents any requirement changes after the inception phase ends
RUP requires a complete project restart whenever any requirement changes occur
RUP has no mechanism for handling requirements changes during development work
explanation: RUP uses iterative development within its structured phases to accommodate changing requirements.

text: A medical device company must comply with FDA validation requirements. How does this constraint affect process model selection?
options: It favors models with traceable verification and validation documentation
It mandates that no testing be performed until final system delivery
It eliminates all possible process models from consideration entirely
It requires using only agile methods without any formal documentation
explanation: FDA compliance requires traceable V&V documentation, favoring models like waterfall or V-model.

text: What is the fundamental trade-off between plan-driven and agile process models in terms of project governance?
options: Plan-driven offers more control and predictability; agile offers more flexibility and speed
Plan-driven and agile models provide identical levels of governance and predictability
Plan-driven models are always inferior to agile in every project context and scenario
Agile models provide more formal governance and control than plan-driven approaches do
explanation: Plan-driven models offer predictability and control; agile models offer flexibility and faster adaptation.

text: When applying the DevOps model, how does continuous integration change the traditional process model structure?
options: It separates development and operations into completely independent workflows
It requires all code to be written before any integration testing takes place
It merges development and operations phases into a continuous automated pipeline
It completely eliminates the need for any testing or quality assurance activities
explanation: DevOps CI/CD merges development and operations into a continuous, automated pipeline.

text: A startup pivots its product direction every three months based on market feedback. Which process model characteristic is most essential?
options: Fixed scope defined entirely at project start without any later changes
Long sequential phases with comprehensive documentation at every stage
Short iteration cycles with frequent delivery and customer feedback loops
Complete architecture designed before any implementation work begins
explanation: Short iterations with frequent feedback allow rapid adaptation to changing product direction.

text: What is the core principle of agile software development?
options: Following a strict sequential development process
Completing comprehensive documentation before coding
Avoiding customer collaboration during development
Responding to change over following a rigid plan
explanation: Agile values responding to change over following a fixed plan.

text: What is a sprint in Scrum methodology?
options: A method for deploying applications to production servers
A tool for measuring source code execution performance
A document describing all system requirements upfront
A fixed time period for completing a set of work items
explanation: A sprint is a time-boxed iteration, typically 2-4 weeks, in which a set of work is completed.

text: Who is the Product Owner in Scrum?
options: The developer who writes the most lines of source code
The tester who executes all automated regression test suites
The person who manages the server infrastructure setup
The person responsible for maximizing product value delivered
explanation: The Product Owner is responsible for maximizing the value of the product and managing the backlog.

text: What is a user story in agile development?
options: A formal contract between the client and development team
A detailed technical specification for database schema design
A short description of a feature from the user perspective
A comprehensive test plan covering all system components
explanation: A user story is a brief description of functionality from the end user's perspective.

text: What does the Agile Manifesto value most?
options: Contract negotiation over customer collaboration efforts
Following a plan over responding to change requests
Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Comprehensive documentation over working software delivery
explanation: The Agile Manifesto values individuals and interactions over processes and tools.

text: What is a daily standup meeting in agile?
options: A monthly review of the project budget and resources
A brief daily meeting to share progress and obstacles
A quarterly assessment of organizational strategy goals
A weekly planning session for the entire project scope
explanation: The daily standup is a brief meeting where team members share progress, plans, and blockers.

text: What is the purpose of a product backlog?
options: To list all desired features and work items for the product
To track employee attendance and working hours precisely
To document the organizational chart of the company fully
To store source code versions in a repository management tool
explanation: The product backlog is an ordered list of everything needed in the product.

text: How long is a typical Scrum sprint?
options: One to two days only
One to four weeks
Eight to ten months
Six to twelve months
explanation: Scrum sprints typically last between one and four weeks.

text: What is the Scrum Master's primary role?
options: To approve all design choices
To manage project budget
To facilitate the Scrum process
To write most of the code
explanation: The Scrum Master facilitates the Scrum process and removes impediments for the team.

text: What happens during a sprint review?
options: All unfinished tasks are permanently removed from scope
The team demonstrates completed work to stakeholders
The development environment is reset to default state
The project budget is reduced by senior management
explanation: During a sprint review, the team demonstrates completed work and gathers stakeholder feedback.

text: What is the difference between Scrum and Kanban?
options: Scrum avoids visualization; Kanban prohibits all meetings
Scrum has no backlog; Kanban requires sprint planning
Scrum has no roles; Kanban requires a Scrum Master role
Scrum uses time-boxed sprints; Kanban uses continuous flow
explanation: Scrum uses fixed-length sprints while Kanban emphasizes continuous flow with WIP limits.

text: What is velocity in agile project management?
options: The frequency of deploying code to the production servers
The amount of work a team completes in one iteration period
The speed at which the application executes user requests
The rate at which new developers join the project team
explanation: Velocity measures the amount of work a team completes during a single sprint or iteration.

text: What is the purpose of a sprint retrospective?
options: To create the initial project charter and scope document
To identify improvements for the team process going forward
To demonstrate completed features to external stakeholders
To assign blame for any defects found during the sprint
explanation: The retrospective helps the team reflect on their process and identify improvements.

text: How does pair programming benefit agile teams?
options: It allows one developer to do all work while the other rests
It doubles the number of features developed per time unit
It increases code quality through continuous peer review work
It eliminates the need for any automated testing frameworks
explanation: Pair programming improves code quality through real-time review and knowledge sharing.

text: What is the definition of 'done' in Scrum?
options: A legal document signed by the client at project conclusion
A shared understanding of when a work item is complete enough
A report generated automatically by the build server system
A metric measuring the total number of lines of code written
explanation: The definition of done is a shared agreement on what criteria must be met for work to be considered complete.

text: What is a burndown chart used for in agile?
options: Documenting all architectural decisions made during the project
Tracking remaining work against time in a sprint or release
Measuring the total revenue generated by the software product
Recording the number of team members joining or leaving work
explanation: A burndown chart visualizes remaining work versus time to track sprint or release progress.

text: What is the purpose of story points in agile estimation?
options: To measure the physical size of the application in megabytes
To estimate the relative effort required for completing tasks
To count the exact number of hours each task will take to do
To track the number of bugs reported by end users each week
explanation: Story points estimate relative effort, complexity, and uncertainty of user stories.

text: How does continuous integration support agile development?
options: By eliminating the need for version control systems entirely
By delaying all testing until the final sprint of the project
By preventing developers from committing code to the repository
By frequently merging and testing code to detect issues early
explanation: CI frequently integrates code changes and runs tests to catch issues early.

text: What is the role of acceptance criteria in user stories?
options: They list the hardware requirements for running the software
They specify the programming language to be used for coding
They define conditions that must be met for story completion
They document the salary expectations of the development team
explanation: Acceptance criteria define the conditions that must be satisfied for a user story to be considered done.

text: What is a spike in agile development?
options: A formal review process conducted by external quality auditors
A sudden increase in the number of production bugs per release
A time-boxed research task to reduce uncertainty or risk factors
A mandatory overtime period for all team members on a project
explanation: A spike is a time-boxed investigation to answer a question or reduce risk before committing to work.

text: A team's velocity has been declining over the past five sprints despite no personnel changes. What is the most likely root cause to investigate?
options: The team has become too skilled and experienced for the project work
Increasing technical debt making new development progressively harder
The product backlog has become too small with too few remaining items
Sprint duration has been too short for any meaningful work to occur
explanation: Accumulating technical debt typically slows development velocity over time.

text: How should a Scrum team handle a situation where the Product Owner frequently changes priorities mid-sprint?
options: Remove the Product Owner from the team to prevent further disruption to the process flow
Educate the Product Owner on sprint commitment and defer changes to next sprint planning
Immediately abandon current sprint work and start on the new priorities without question
Ignore all priority changes and continue working on the original plan without communication
explanation: Sprint commitments should be protected; changes should be deferred to the next sprint planning session.

text: In SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework), what is the purpose of a Program Increment planning event?
options: To eliminate the need for individual team sprint planning sessions throughout the increment
To replace all Scrum ceremonies with a single comprehensive monthly planning meeting event
To align multiple agile teams on shared objectives and dependencies for a planning period
To assign specific tasks to individual developers across all teams in the entire organization
explanation: PI planning aligns multiple teams on objectives, dependencies, and delivery plans for the increment.

text: A distributed agile team across three time zones is struggling with communication. Which practice would most effectively address this challenge?
options: Requiring all team members to work the same hours regardless of their local time zone
Establishing overlapping core hours and using asynchronous communication tools effectively
Eliminating all meetings and relying exclusively on email for all team communications
Splitting the team into independent groups that never need to communicate with each other
explanation: Overlapping core hours with async tools balance real-time collaboration and practical time zone constraints.

text: What is the relationship between the Agile Manifesto values and the twelve agile principles?
options: The values replace the principles and make them unnecessary for any agile team practice
The values and principles are completely unrelated documents with different authors entirely
The principles contradict the values and should be followed instead of the manifesto values
The principles provide specific guidance for applying the four abstract manifesto values
explanation: The twelve principles provide actionable guidance for implementing the four Agile Manifesto values.

text: When transitioning from waterfall to agile, what is the most significant organizational challenge?
options: Shifting from command-and-control management to servant leadership and team autonomy
Purchasing new software development tools and IDE licenses for all team members now
Reducing the number of meetings from weekly status updates to no meetings at all
Renaming existing project roles to match agile terminology without changing practices
explanation: The cultural shift from command-and-control to servant leadership is the hardest part of agile adoption.

text: How does technical debt impact agile teams differently than plan-driven teams?
options: Agile teams are immune to technical debt because they refactor code in every single sprint
Technical debt affects plan-driven teams more because they have longer development cycles
Agile teams feel the impact sooner due to shorter iteration cycles and continuous delivery
Plan-driven teams never accumulate technical debt due to their thorough upfront planning
explanation: Short agile iterations expose technical debt impacts sooner than longer plan-driven cycles.

text: A team is practicing Scrum but the sprint goal is never met. The team completes individual stories but lacks cohesion. What is the underlying issue?
options: The sprint goal is not well-defined or the team is not collaborating toward a shared objective
The Product Owner is providing too few user stories for the team to work on each sprint
The sprint duration is too long and the team runs out of meaningful work before it ends
Individual team members are too skilled and complete work too quickly without coordination
explanation: Poorly defined sprint goals and lack of collaborative focus lead to completing stories without meeting the goal.

text: In Lean software development, what does 'eliminating waste' specifically refer to?
options: Removing activities that do not add value to the customer from the development process
Reducing the physical waste produced by the office including paper and plastic materials
Firing underperforming team members to improve the overall productivity of the team now
Deleting all source code comments and documentation to reduce the size of the codebase
explanation: Lean waste elimination means removing activities that don't contribute value to the customer.

text: What is the difference between adaptive and predictive project management approaches in the context of agile?
options: Both approaches are identical in how they handle project scope and requirement changes fully
Adaptive approaches fix all requirements at start; predictive approaches change constantly now
Adaptive approaches embrace change and evolve plans; predictive approaches fix scope upfront
Predictive approaches are more responsive to change than adaptive approaches in all cases
explanation: Adaptive (agile) approaches embrace change; predictive (plan-driven) approaches fix scope early.

text: What is requirements engineering in software development?
options: The process of discovering and documenting system needs
A strategy for deploying applications to cloud servers
A technique for writing efficient source code algorithms
A method for testing software performance under load
explanation: Requirements engineering is the process of eliciting, analyzing, documenting, and managing requirements.

text: What is a functional requirement?
options: A specification of what the system should do for users
A plan for marketing the software product to customers
A schedule for training end users on system operations
A description of the hardware needed to run the system
explanation: Functional requirements define specific behaviors or functions the system must provide.

text: What is a non-functional requirement?
options: A project schedule milestone for the delivery deadline
A feature that the system must implement for the user
A programming language choice for the development team
A constraint on how the system performs its functions
explanation: Non-functional requirements define quality attributes like performance, security, and usability.

text: Who are the primary sources of software requirements?
options: Hardware manufacturers and network equipment vendors
Operating system kernel developers and maintainers
Compiler designers and programming language creators
Stakeholders and end users of the system being built
explanation: Stakeholders and end users are the primary sources of requirements for software systems.

text: What is a Software Requirements Specification (SRS)?
options: A network diagram showing system topology and connections
A database schema defining all tables and relationships
A source code file containing the main application logic
A document that describes all system requirements in detail
explanation: An SRS is a comprehensive document describing all functional and non-functional requirements.

text: What does 'requirements elicitation' mean?
options: Testing the software against its documented requirements
Gathering requirements from stakeholders and other sources
Deploying the finished software to the production server
Deleting unnecessary requirements from the project scope
explanation: Requirements elicitation is the process of gathering requirements from stakeholders and other sources.

text: Which of these is an example of a non-functional requirement?
options: The system shall generate monthly financial reports
The system shall send email notifications to users
The system shall respond to queries within two seconds
The system shall allow users to create new accounts
explanation: Response time is a performance requirement, which is a type of non-functional requirement.

text: What is the purpose of requirements validation?
options: To ensure requirements accurately reflect stakeholder needs
To train end users on how to operate the new system
To deploy the software to the staging environment first
To write source code that implements the system features
explanation: Requirements validation ensures that documented requirements accurately represent stakeholder needs.

text: What is a use case in requirements engineering?
options: A report on the system's performance test results
A list of programming languages used in the project
A diagram of the database schema and relationships
A description of how users interact with the system
explanation: A use case describes how a user interacts with the system to achieve a specific goal.

text: Why is requirements traceability important?
options: It links requirements to design, code, and test artifacts
It eliminates the need for user acceptance testing work
It reduces the amount of memory the application uses
It speeds up the compilation of the source code files
explanation: Traceability ensures every requirement is linked to corresponding design, code, and test elements.

text: What is the difference between user requirements and system requirements?
options: User requirements are high-level needs; system requirements are detailed specifications
User requirements are optional; system requirements are always mandatory for the project
User requirements come from developers; system requirements come from end user groups
User requirements are written in code; system requirements are written in plain language
explanation: User requirements describe high-level needs; system requirements provide detailed technical specifications.

text: What is requirements prioritization and why is it necessary?
options: It ranks requirements by importance to guide development order and resource allocation
It randomly assigns requirements to different sprints without any logical ordering method
It permanently removes low-priority requirements from the project scope and backlog
It ensures all requirements are implemented simultaneously in a single development phase
explanation: Prioritization ranks requirements by importance to guide development sequencing and resource allocation.

text: What challenges arise during requirements elicitation with multiple stakeholders?
options: All stakeholders always agree perfectly on every requirement without any discussion
Multiple stakeholders simplify the process by reducing the total number of requirements
Having more stakeholders eliminates the need for formal requirements documentation
Conflicting viewpoints and priorities that must be negotiated and resolved carefully
explanation: Multiple stakeholders often have conflicting needs and priorities requiring negotiation.

text: How do prototypes help in requirements engineering?
options: They replace the need for any written requirements documentation in the project
They help stakeholders visualize the system and clarify unclear requirements early
They guarantee that all requirements are correctly implemented in the final system
They eliminate the possibility of any requirements changes during the development
explanation: Prototypes help stakeholders understand and refine requirements by providing tangible demonstrations.

text: What is the MoSCoW method in requirements prioritization?
options: A geographic approach assigning requirements to teams based on physical location
A technique categorizing requirements as Must, Should, Could, and Won't have items
A testing strategy for validating requirements against acceptance criteria only
A programming paradigm for implementing requirements in object-oriented languages
explanation: MoSCoW categorizes requirements into Must have, Should have, Could have, and Won't have.

text: What is the difference between requirements verification and validation?
options: Verification writes test cases; validation executes marketing campaigns for launch
Verification checks document consistency; validation checks stakeholder satisfaction
Verification trains users; validation calculates the return on investment figures
Verification deploys the system; validation monitors production performance metrics
explanation: Verification ensures requirements are internally consistent; validation ensures they satisfy stakeholder needs.

text: Why is natural language problematic for writing requirements?
options: It is too precise and does not allow any flexibility in requirement statements
It is only understood by technical staff and not by any business stakeholders
It is inherently ambiguous and can lead to multiple conflicting interpretations
It cannot express complex requirements due to limited vocabulary available
explanation: Natural language ambiguity can lead to misunderstandings and conflicting interpretations of requirements.

text: What is a requirements review and who should participate?
options: A formal inspection of requirements involving stakeholders and technical staff
A marketing meeting to discuss product positioning and pricing strategies
An informal chat between two developers about their coding preferences only
A hardware procurement session to select servers for the production use
explanation: A requirements review is a formal inspection involving both stakeholders and technical team members.

text: How does requirements management handle change requests?
options: By delegating all change decisions to a single junior developer on the team
By rejecting all change requests to maintain the original project scope fully
By immediately implementing all requested changes without any analysis first
Through a formal change control process that evaluates impact before approval
explanation: Change control processes evaluate the impact of proposed changes before deciding whether to approve them.

text: What is the role of domain analysis in requirements engineering?
options: It focuses exclusively on selecting the programming language for development
It calculates the exact project budget and timeline before development starts
It determines the number of servers needed for production deployment only
It helps understand the application domain to identify relevant requirements
explanation: Domain analysis helps engineers understand the application context to identify relevant requirements.

text: A healthcare system must comply with HIPAA regulations. How should these regulatory constraints be captured in requirements?
options: As project management constraints affecting only the delivery timeline and budget
As functional requirements describing the user interface layout and color scheme
As non-functional requirements specifying security, privacy, and audit trail standards
As optional features that can be implemented if time and budget allow during work
explanation: Regulatory constraints like HIPAA should be captured as non-functional requirements for security and privacy.

text: During elicitation, stakeholders express contradictory requirements. What is the most effective resolution approach?
options: Implement all contradictory requirements simultaneously without resolving the conflicts
Facilitate negotiation sessions to find compromises and document agreed-upon priorities
Ignore the less senior stakeholder's requirements and implement only the manager's view
Postpone the entire project until all stakeholders naturally reach unanimous agreement
explanation: Facilitated negotiation helps resolve conflicts by finding compromises and documenting agreed priorities.

text: What is the relationship between requirements volatility and project risk in software engineering?
options: Requirements volatility has no measurable impact on project risk or delivery outcomes
Requirements stability increases project risk because it prevents creative innovation work
Volatile requirements always reduce project risk by keeping the team adaptable and ready
Higher requirements volatility increases project risk through scope creep and rework costs
explanation: Volatile requirements increase risk through scope creep, rework, schedule delays, and cost overruns.

text: How should safety-critical requirements be specified differently from standard functional requirements?
options: Using informal natural language descriptions without any additional precision or detail
With formal notation, rigorous verification criteria, and detailed failure mode analysis
Exactly the same way as any other functional requirement without special treatment given
By omitting them from documentation and relying on developer intuition during coding
explanation: Safety-critical requirements need formal specification, rigorous verification, and failure mode analysis.

text: A team discovers during implementation that a key requirement is technically infeasible. What is the appropriate requirements engineering response?
options: Conduct impact analysis, negotiate alternatives with stakeholders, and update the SRS document
Cancel the entire project because a single requirement cannot be implemented as specified
Continue attempting to implement the infeasible requirement regardless of technical limitations
Silently remove the requirement from the SRS without informing any stakeholders about it
explanation: Impact analysis and stakeholder negotiation for alternatives is the proper response to infeasible requirements.

text: What is the 'requirements gap' and how does it affect software projects?
options: It is the physical distance between the development team and the client office locations
It is the difference between documented requirements and actual stakeholder needs and expectations
It is the time delay between writing requirements and implementing them in source code only
It is the salary difference between requirements analysts and software development engineers
explanation: The requirements gap is the discrepancy between what is documented and what stakeholders actually need.

text: How do goal-oriented requirements engineering approaches differ from traditional approaches?
options: They focus exclusively on technical implementation details rather than business-level objectives
They eliminate the need for any stakeholder communication during the requirements phase entirely
They focus on stakeholder goals and derive requirements from high-level objectives systematically
They produce identical results to traditional approaches but use different terminology and tools
explanation: Goal-oriented approaches derive requirements from stakeholder goals, providing better rationale and traceability.

text: In a large enterprise system with hundreds of requirements, how should traceability be managed effectively?
options: By assigning one developer to memorize all requirement-to-code mappings without any tools
By avoiding traceability entirely since it adds overhead without providing any project value
By manually maintaining paper documents that cross-reference every requirement to code lines
Using automated traceability tools with bidirectional links between requirements and artifacts
explanation: Automated tools with bidirectional links efficiently manage traceability in large-scale systems.

text: What is the impact of cultural differences on requirements elicitation in global software development projects?
options: Cultural differences affect communication styles, expectations, and interpretation of requirements
Cultural differences have no impact on requirements because software is a universal language
All cultures have identical approaches to expressing needs and defining system requirements
Cultural factors only affect project management and have no relevance to requirements work
explanation: Cultural differences significantly impact communication, expectations, and requirement interpretation.

text: How does viewpoint-oriented requirements engineering handle the challenge of multiple perspectives?
options: It combines all viewpoints into one without distinguishing their sources or resolving conflicts
It selects a single viewpoint and ignores all other perspectives throughout the entire project
It systematically captures and reconciles requirements from different stakeholder viewpoints
It eliminates the need for stakeholder involvement by relying solely on developer assumptions
explanation: Viewpoint-oriented RE systematically captures and reconciles requirements from multiple perspectives.

text: What is the primary goal of software project management?
options: Installing and configuring the production server hardware equipment
Writing all the source code for the project without any team members
Designing the user interface layout and choosing the color schemes
Delivering software on time, within budget, and meeting quality standards
explanation: Project management ensures software is delivered on time, within budget, and to quality standards.

text: What is a project milestone?
options: A hardware component required for the server infrastructure
A significant checkpoint marking completion of a project phase
A type of programming error found during code review sessions
A daily task assigned to individual software developers on team
explanation: A milestone is a significant point in the project timeline marking the completion of a key deliverable.

text: What is the purpose of a Gantt chart in project management?
options: To document all the source code changes made by developers
To track the number of defects found during system testing
To visually represent the project schedule and task timelines
To calculate the total budget required for hardware purchases
explanation: A Gantt chart visually displays project tasks, durations, and dependencies over time.

text: What does WBS stand for in project management?
options: Workflow Business Strategy
Wireless Bandwidth System
Web Based Software tool
Work Breakdown Structure
explanation: WBS stands for Work Breakdown Structure, which decomposes project work into manageable pieces.

text: Who is responsible for overall project success?
options: The quality assurance tester only
The external client exclusively
The project manager leading the team
Each individual developer equally
explanation: The project manager is primarily responsible for planning, executing, and closing the project successfully.

text: What is project scope in software development?
options: The defined boundaries of what the project will deliver
The programming language selected for implementation
The total number of developers assigned to the team
The number of servers allocated for production use
explanation: Project scope defines the boundaries, deliverables, and constraints of what the project will produce.

text: What is resource allocation in project management?
options: Assigning people, tools, and budget to project activities
Designing the database schema for the system storage
Deleting unnecessary files from the project repository
Running automated tests on the application source code
explanation: Resource allocation assigns available people, tools, and budget to project tasks.

text: What is the critical path in a project schedule?
options: A shortcut method for skipping non-essential project activities
The easiest set of tasks that any team member can complete alone
A backup plan executed only when the main project plan fails
The longest sequence of dependent tasks determining project duration
explanation: The critical path is the longest sequence of dependent tasks that determines the minimum project duration.

text: What is a project stakeholder?
options: Only the software developers who write the application source code
Just the project manager who oversees the entire development team
Exclusively the client who pays for the software development work
Anyone who has an interest in or is affected by the project outcome
explanation: Stakeholders include anyone with an interest in or affected by the project and its outcomes.

text: What does project monitoring involve?
options: Tracking progress and comparing it against the original plan
Writing source code for the application features and modules
Conducting final user acceptance testing before deployment
Designing the user interface screens and navigation flows
explanation: Project monitoring tracks actual progress against the plan to identify deviations early.

text: What is the purpose of earned value management (EVM)?
options: To measure project performance by comparing planned versus actual progress and costs
To determine which programming language is most suitable for the project requirements
To calculate the total number of lines of code written by each developer on the team
To design the system architecture and select appropriate technology stack components
explanation: EVM measures project performance by integrating scope, schedule, and cost metrics.

text: How does the critical path method (CPM) help project managers?
options: It guarantees that no project will ever experience delays or budget overruns
It identifies tasks that directly impact project completion date if delayed at all
It randomly assigns priorities to tasks without considering their dependencies now
It eliminates the need for any project scheduling or timeline management work
explanation: CPM identifies tasks whose delays directly impact the project completion date.

text: What is scope creep and how can it be managed?
options: An issue that resolves itself automatically without any management intervention
A beneficial practice that should be encouraged for maximum project flexibility
A problem that only affects projects using agile development methodologies now
Uncontrolled scope expansion managed through formal change control processes
explanation: Scope creep is uncontrolled scope expansion, managed through formal change control processes.

text: What is the difference between effort estimation and duration estimation?
options: Effort measures code quality; duration measures the number of features implemented
Effort is only relevant for testing; duration is only relevant for coding activities
Effort is total work hours needed; duration is calendar time to complete the task
Effort and duration are identical concepts with no meaningful difference between them
explanation: Effort is total person-hours required; duration is elapsed calendar time accounting for resources.

text: What is the COCOMO model used for in software project management?
options: Managing source code versions and branches in the project code repository
Testing software applications for security vulnerabilities and performance issues
Estimating software development effort and cost based on project size parameters
Designing user interfaces for web-based applications and mobile platforms only
explanation: COCOMO estimates effort, cost, and schedule based on project size and complexity factors.

text: How do project managers handle team conflicts effectively?
options: By immediately removing the conflicting team members from the project team
By assigning all disputed tasks to a single person to avoid disagreements
By facilitating open communication and finding mutually acceptable solutions
By ignoring conflicts and hoping they resolve themselves without intervention
explanation: Effective conflict resolution involves facilitated communication and mutually acceptable solutions.

text: What is a risk register in project management?
options: A source code repository containing all versions of the application being built
A database of all employee records including salary and performance evaluations
A financial ledger tracking all project expenses and revenue over the timeline
A documented list of identified risks with their probability and impact assessments
explanation: A risk register documents identified risks with their probability, impact, and mitigation strategies.

text: What is the purpose of a project post-mortem or lessons learned session?
options: To calculate the final salary bonuses for the project team members after launch
To assign blame to team members who made mistakes during the project execution
To identify what went well and what can be improved for future project efforts
To permanently archive all project documents and prevent any future access now
explanation: Post-mortems identify successes and improvement areas to benefit future projects.

text: How does function point analysis help in project estimation?
options: It measures the physical size of the deployed application in storage megabytes
It tracks the number of team members assigned to the project at any given time
It counts the number of programming languages used in the project source code
It measures software size based on functional requirements independent of technology
explanation: Function point analysis measures software size based on user-facing functionality, independent of technology.

text: What is the triple constraint in project management?
options: The balance between scope, time, and cost that defines project boundaries
The three phases of testing: unit, integration, and system level testing
The minimum number of developers needed to start any software project
The three programming languages required for every software project build
explanation: The triple constraint (scope, time, cost) defines the boundaries within which projects must be managed.

text: A project is showing a Cost Performance Index (CPI) of 0.8 and Schedule Performance Index (SPI) of 0.9. What does this indicate?
options: The project is under budget by 20% and ahead of schedule by 10% of planned work
The project is over budget by 20% and behind schedule by 10% of planned progress
The project is exactly on budget and on schedule according to the baseline plan
The project has been completed successfully with all deliverables meeting standards
explanation: CPI < 1 means over budget; SPI < 1 means behind schedule. CPI 0.8 = 20% over budget, SPI 0.9 = 10% behind.

text: How should a project manager handle a situation where the estimated effort exceeds the available budget?
options: Cancel the project immediately without exploring any alternative approaches or solutions
Proceed with the full scope and hope the team can work faster than the estimates suggest
Add more developers to reduce the schedule without adjusting the budget or scope at all
Negotiate scope reduction with stakeholders while maintaining core functionality intact
explanation: Scope negotiation with stakeholders helps align deliverables with available budget constraints.

text: In Brooks' Mythical Man-Month, why does adding developers to a late project make it later?
options: New developers instantly become productive and reduce the workload on existing team members
Projects always finish on time regardless of team size changes during the development period
Adding developers has no measurable effect on project schedule in any known situation today
Communication overhead grows quadratically and new members need ramp-up training time
explanation: Adding people increases communication overhead (n*(n-1)/2 channels) and requires training time.

text: A team uses PERT estimation with optimistic=2 weeks, most likely=4 weeks, and pessimistic=12 weeks. What is the expected duration?
options: Five weeks based on the weighted average formula used in PERT estimation technique
Two weeks because PERT always selects the most optimistic estimate for planning work
Twelve weeks because PERT always uses the pessimistic estimate for safety margin only
Four weeks because the most likely estimate is always the final expected duration value
explanation: PERT expected = (O + 4M + P) / 6 = (2 + 16 + 12) / 6 = 5 weeks.

text: How does the theory of constraints apply to software project management?
options: It requires constraining all team members to work at exactly the same speed and pace
It identifies and focuses improvement efforts on the bottleneck limiting overall throughput
It suggests adding constraints to every activity to improve project discipline overall
It eliminates all project constraints by removing deadlines and budget limitations now
explanation: Theory of constraints identifies the bottleneck that limits throughput and focuses improvement there.

text: A project manager discovers that two critical team members plan to leave mid-project. What proactive measures should be taken?
options: Ignore the risk and assume the remaining team can absorb all additional workload easily
Immediately cancel the project since replacing team members is impossible to accomplish
Reduce product quality standards so the remaining team can deliver with less total effort
Document knowledge, cross-train team members, and plan for replacement hiring immediately
explanation: Knowledge documentation, cross-training, and replacement planning mitigate key-person dependency risks.

text: What is the relationship between project size and defect density in software engineering?
options: Project size has absolutely no correlation with defect density in any published research data
Defect density remains perfectly constant regardless of project size or complexity levels
Smaller projects always have more defects than larger projects due to less thorough testing
Larger projects tend to have higher defect density due to increased complexity and coupling
explanation: Research shows larger projects tend to have higher defect density due to complexity and integration issues.

text: How should a project manager balance the competing demands of multiple stakeholders with different priorities?
options: Randomly select which stakeholder requirements to implement without any systematic approach
Ignore all stakeholder input and make decisions based solely on the development team opinion
Use stakeholder analysis to understand influence and interest, then negotiate compromises
Always prioritize the requirements of the most senior stakeholder without any negotiation
explanation: Stakeholder analysis and negotiation help balance competing demands based on influence and interest.

text: What is Monte Carlo simulation used for in project management and how does it improve estimation accuracy?
options: It eliminates all uncertainty from project estimates by using a fixed mathematical formula only
It provides a single deterministic estimate that is always accurate for every project situation
It replaces the need for expert judgment by using random numbers without any project data
It runs thousands of simulations with varying inputs to produce probability-based schedule forecasts
explanation: Monte Carlo simulation runs many iterations with variable inputs to produce probabilistic forecasts.

text: In agile project management, how does the concept of 'servant leadership' change the traditional project manager role?
options: The manager delegates all responsibility and has no involvement in the project activities at all
The manager increases direct control over all individual tasks and decisions made by the team
The manager role is completely eliminated and no leadership exists within agile project teams
The manager focuses on removing impediments and empowering the team rather than directing work
explanation: Servant leadership shifts focus from directing to facilitating, empowering, and removing impediments.

text: What is software design in the development process?
options: A technique for deploying software to production servers
A strategy for gathering requirements from stakeholders
A method for testing software under heavy load conditions
The process of defining system structure and components
explanation: Software design defines the architecture, components, interfaces, and data for a system.

text: What is cohesion in software design?
options: The degree to which elements within a module belong together
The total number of lines of code in a software application
The speed at which the application responds to user actions
The amount of dependencies between different software modules
explanation: Cohesion measures how strongly related the elements within a module are to each other.

text: What is coupling in software design?
options: The speed at which the application compiles from source
The degree of interdependence between software modules
The total number of classes in the application codebase
The amount of documentation written for each module
explanation: Coupling measures the degree of interdependence between software modules.

text: What is the goal of good software design?
options: Neither cohesion nor coupling matter in quality design work
High coupling between modules and low cohesion within them
High cohesion within modules and low coupling between them
Equal cohesion and coupling across all system components
explanation: Good design aims for high cohesion (focused modules) and low coupling (minimal dependencies).

text: What is an interface in software design?
options: A network protocol for transmitting data between servers
A contract defining how components interact with each other
A database table storing application configuration settings
A visual screen layout designed for end user interactions only
explanation: An interface defines the contract for how software components communicate and interact.

text: What is modularity in software design?
options: Writing all code in a single file without any organization
Maximizing dependencies between all parts of the system
Dividing software into separate independent functional units
Avoiding any separation of concerns in the code structure
explanation: Modularity divides software into separate, independent modules that can be developed and maintained separately.

text: What is abstraction in software design?
options: Avoiding any simplification of the system's internal logic
Making all internal data structures visible to every module
Including every implementation detail in the public interface
Hiding complex details and showing only essential features
explanation: Abstraction hides complexity by exposing only essential features to the outside.

text: What is the purpose of a design pattern?
options: To increase the complexity of the software architecture
To provide reusable solutions to common design problems
To replace the need for requirements analysis activities
To create unique solutions that cannot be reused anywhere
explanation: Design patterns provide proven, reusable solutions to commonly occurring design problems.

text: What is encapsulation in object-oriented design?
options: Exposing all internal data to every part of the program
Allowing unrestricted access to all object internal states
Bundling data and methods together and restricting access
Separating data from the methods that operate on the data
explanation: Encapsulation bundles data with methods and restricts direct access to internal state.

text: What is a class diagram used for in software design?
options: To display the project timeline and scheduling milestones
To show the structure of classes and their relationships
To illustrate network topology and server configurations
To represent database queries and their execution plans
explanation: Class diagrams show classes, their attributes, methods, and relationships in the system.

text: What is the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)?
options: A class should handle all possible system responsibilities now
A function should always return multiple values simultaneously
A class should have only one reason to change its behavior
A module should depend on as many other modules as possible
explanation: SRP states that a class should have only one reason to change, meaning one responsibility.

text: What is the Open-Closed Principle in software design?
options: Software entities should never be extended or modified after initial release
Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
Software entities should be both open and closed for all types of changes
Software entities should be closed for extension and open for modification
explanation: The Open-Closed Principle states entities should be extendable without modifying existing code.

text: What is the difference between the Strategy and State design patterns?
options: Strategy and State are identical patterns with no differences between them at all
Strategy selects algorithms; State changes behavior based on internal object state
Strategy manages object creation; State handles object destruction and cleanup work
Strategy is for UI design only; State is exclusively for database operations work
explanation: Strategy selects among interchangeable algorithms; State changes behavior based on object state.

text: How does the Observer pattern support loose coupling?
options: Observers must inherit from the subject class to receive any notification updates
Observers must know the complete internal state of the subject at all times now
Subjects notify observers without knowing their concrete implementation details
The pattern requires tight coupling between all observers and the subject object
explanation: The Observer pattern allows subjects to notify observers without knowing their concrete types.

text: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle?
options: High-level modules should depend on abstractions not on low-level module details
Dependencies should always flow from abstractions down to concrete implementations
High-level modules should directly depend on the implementation of low-level modules
Low-level modules should control the behavior of all high-level modules in system
explanation: DIP states high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concrete low-level details.

text: What is the Factory Method pattern used for?
options: Connecting to external databases and executing queries against stored data records
Sorting collections of objects based on their attributes in ascending or descending order
Destroying objects and freeing memory after they are no longer needed by the program
Creating objects without specifying the exact class to instantiate at compile time
explanation: Factory Method defines an interface for creating objects but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate.

text: How does the MVC pattern separate concerns in application design?
options: It eliminates the need for any user interface by focusing solely on data storage
It only separates the database from the rest of the application into two layers
It separates data (Model), presentation (View), and logic (Controller) into layers
It combines all application logic into a single layer without any separation at all
explanation: MVC separates the application into Model (data), View (presentation), and Controller (logic).

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?
options: Base types should be substitutable for their subtypes in all program situations
Subtypes should completely change the behavior defined by their base type classes
Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering correctness
Inheritance should be avoided entirely in favor of composition in every case
explanation: LSP states that objects of a subtype should be usable wherever the base type is expected.

text: What is the purpose of the Adapter design pattern?
options: To make incompatible interfaces work together through a wrapper component layer
To add new functionality to objects dynamically at runtime without inheritance use
To ensure a class has only one instance throughout the entire application system
To create new objects by cloning existing ones without using constructors directly
explanation: The Adapter pattern makes incompatible interfaces compatible through a wrapper.

text: What is the difference between composition and inheritance in design?
options: Composition is always inferior to inheritance for all software design scenarios today
Composition uses has-a relationships; inheritance uses is-a relationships between types
Composition and inheritance are identical concepts with no meaningful differences at all
Inheritance provides more flexibility than composition in every possible design context
explanation: Composition creates has-a relationships (containing objects); inheritance creates is-a relationships (extending types).

text: A system needs to support multiple database backends (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB) without changing business logic. Which design approach is most appropriate?
options: Hardcoding database queries directly into business logic classes for maximum performance
Abstract Factory pattern with database-specific factory implementations for each backend
Using global variables to store database connection strings throughout the application
Writing separate copies of the business logic for each database backend independently
explanation: Abstract Factory provides database-specific implementations behind a common interface, isolating business logic.

text: How does the SOLID principles violation manifest in a God class anti-pattern?
options: The class violates SRP by handling too many responsibilities and having many reasons to change
The class follows SRP perfectly by focusing on a single well-defined responsibility only
The class demonstrates ideal OCP by being completely closed to any future extensions work
The class exemplifies LSP by being perfectly substitutable for all of its subtype classes
explanation: God classes violate SRP by accumulating too many responsibilities, making them hard to maintain.

text: A real-time trading system must process millions of events per second. How should the design balance performance with maintainability?
options: Sacrifice all code readability and maintainability to achieve maximum raw processing speed
Implement all processing in a single monolithic function for maximum execution throughput
Use the simplest possible design without considering performance requirements at any point
Use event-driven architecture with optimized hot paths while keeping non-critical paths clean
explanation: Event-driven design with optimized hot paths balances performance-critical and maintainable code.

text: What is the relationship between design patterns and anti-patterns in software engineering?
options: Anti-patterns are always better than design patterns for solving complex engineering problems
Design patterns cause problems while anti-patterns provide effective solutions for all cases
Design patterns solve recurring problems; anti-patterns are commonly used but ineffective solutions
Design patterns and anti-patterns are identical concepts with interchangeable names and usage
explanation: Design patterns are proven solutions; anti-patterns are common but counterproductive approaches.

text: How does the Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters) improve testability of business logic?
options: It isolates business logic from external dependencies through ports allowing easy mock injection
It embeds external dependencies directly into business logic making testing straightforward now
It makes testing harder by creating multiple layers of indirection between all components now
It eliminates the need for any testing by ensuring the business logic is always correct already
explanation: Hexagonal architecture isolates business logic behind ports, enabling easy substitution of test doubles.

text: A development team is debating between using inheritance hierarchies and composition for code reuse. What factors should guide this decision?
options: Always use composition because inheritance should never be used in any software system today
Use inheritance for genuine is-a relationships; prefer composition for flexible behavior reuse
