Always use inheritance because it is fundamentally superior to composition in every scenario
The choice is purely aesthetic and has no impact on system flexibility or maintainability
explanation: Inheritance suits true is-a relationships; composition offers more flexibility for behavior reuse.

text: How does the Command pattern enable undo functionality in software applications?
options: The Command pattern only supports redo operations and cannot implement undo functionality now
Undo is implemented by restarting the entire application from its initial state each time needed
The Command pattern prevents any actions from being undone once they have been executed fully
Each command object encapsulates an action and its inverse allowing execution reversal on demand
explanation: Command objects encapsulate actions with enough state to reverse them, enabling undo operations.

text: What is the impact of circular dependencies on software design quality and maintainability?
options: They simplify the build process by reducing the number of modules that need compilation work
They have no measurable impact on software design quality or long-term maintainability at all
They increase coupling, complicate builds, and make modules impossible to test in isolation easily
They improve design quality by ensuring all modules are tightly integrated with each other now
explanation: Circular dependencies increase coupling, complicate builds, and prevent independent testing of modules.

text: In domain-driven design, how does the Bounded Context pattern help manage complexity in large systems?
options: It requires a single unified domain model used identically across all parts of the entire system
It eliminates the need for any domain modeling by using generic data structures everywhere now
It defines clear boundaries where specific domain models apply preventing model conflicts globally
It increases complexity by requiring every team to understand all domain models in the system
explanation: Bounded Contexts define boundaries for domain models, preventing conflicts and managing complexity.

text: How should a designer handle the tension between the DRY principle and the need for module independence?
options: Accept controlled duplication when shared abstractions would create inappropriate coupling between modules
Always eliminate every instance of duplication regardless of the coupling it introduces between parts
Force all modules to share a single implementation even when their requirements diverge significantly
Ignore the DRY principle entirely because code duplication has no negative consequences on quality
explanation: Some duplication is acceptable when shared abstractions would create inappropriate coupling between modules.

text: What is software architecture?
options: A detailed line-by-line description of the source code logic
A specific programming language used for building applications
A testing framework for validating user interface interactions
The high-level structure of a software system and its components
explanation: Software architecture defines the high-level structure, components, and their relationships in a system.

text: What is a client-server architecture?
options: A design pattern for sorting data in ascending order quickly
A system where all processing occurs on the user device only
A system where clients request services from a central server
A testing methodology for validating database query results
explanation: Client-server architecture separates the system into clients that request services and servers that provide them.

text: What is a layered architecture?
options: An architecture using a single flat structure without layers
An architecture organizing software into hierarchical layers
A deployment strategy for distributing code across servers
A testing technique for validating multi-step user workflows
explanation: Layered architecture organizes software into layers where each layer provides services to the layer above.

text: What is a monolithic architecture?
options: A single unified application where all components are interconnected
A database architecture using multiple separate data storage nodes
A network topology with redundant connections between all nodes
A distributed system with many independent microservice components
explanation: Monolithic architecture builds the entire application as a single, tightly coupled unit.

text: What is the purpose of an architectural pattern?
options: To define specific algorithms for data processing and sorting
To provide proven solutions for common structural design problems
To specify the exact programming language for implementation
To determine the number of developers needed for a project
explanation: Architectural patterns provide proven structural solutions for commonly recurring design challenges.

text: What is a microservices architecture?
options: Small independent services that communicate over network protocols
A single large application built as one deployable unit together
A programming style using only one function for all logic needs
A database design using only a single table for all data items
explanation: Microservices architecture builds applications as collections of small, independent, communicating services.

text: What is a pipe-and-filter architecture?
options: Data flows through processing components connected in sequence
A user interface pattern for displaying forms and input fields
All components execute simultaneously without any data flow order
A database technique for filtering query results by conditions
explanation: Pipe-and-filter passes data through sequential processing components, each transforming the data.

text: What is a repository architecture?
options: Data is distributed randomly across components without any order
Each component maintains its own isolated copy of all system data
Components never share any data and operate fully independently
Components share data through a central data store or repository
explanation: Repository architecture uses a central data store that all components access for shared data.

text: What does 'architectural style' refer to?
options: A family of systems sharing structural and behavioral properties
A method for writing unit tests for individual code functions
A specific color scheme used for the application user interface
A technique for compressing files to reduce storage space used
explanation: An architectural style defines a family of systems with shared structural and behavioral characteristics.

text: What is the role of an architect in software development?
options: Writing all the unit tests for the application source code now
Making high-level design decisions about system structure choices
Managing the project budget and timeline schedules for delivery
Conducting user interviews to gather system requirements only
explanation: A software architect makes high-level decisions about system structure, technologies, and design principles.

text: What is the difference between software architecture and software design?
options: Architecture and design are identical concepts with no meaningful differences between them
Architecture focuses on code details; design focuses on system-level structural decisions
Architecture defines high-level structure; design details component internals and algorithms
Architecture is only relevant for small systems; design is only for large-scale systems
explanation: Architecture addresses system-level structure; design addresses component-level details and algorithms.

text: How does the Model-View-Controller architecture support separation of concerns?
options: It eliminates the need for a user interface by focusing exclusively on data processing
It only separates the database from the application without any further decomposition
It separates data management, user interface, and control logic into distinct components
It combines all concerns into a single component for simplicity and ease of maintenance
explanation: MVC separates data (Model), presentation (View), and control logic (Controller) into distinct components.

text: What are the trade-offs of microservices versus monolithic architecture?
options: Microservices offer scalability and independence but add complexity in communication and deployment
There are no meaningful trade-offs because both approaches produce identical system qualities
Microservices are always superior to monolithic architectures in every possible project scenario
Monolithic architectures are always more scalable than microservices in all system contexts today
explanation: Microservices offer scalability and team independence but increase operational and communication complexity.

text: What is an event-driven architecture?
options: Components communicate by producing and consuming events asynchronously via message brokers
All system events are processed in a strictly sequential order without any parallel execution
Components communicate exclusively through synchronous direct function calls without any delay
Event-driven systems cannot handle more than one event at any given point in time today
explanation: Event-driven architecture uses asynchronous events and message brokers for component communication.

text: How does the service-oriented architecture (SOA) promote reusability?
options: Services are designed as reusable, self-contained units with well-defined standard interfaces
SOA discourages reusability by requiring unique implementations for every system integration
Services in SOA are always built from scratch and never leverage any existing functionality
Services are tightly coupled and cannot be reused outside their original application context
explanation: SOA designs services as reusable, self-contained units with standardized interfaces.

text: What is an architectural decision record (ADR)?
options: A project management artifact tracking team member assignments and work allocations
A source code file containing the main application entry point and initialization logic
A database schema definition file used for creating tables and relationships in storage
A document capturing the context, decision, and consequences of an architectural choice
explanation: An ADR documents the context, decision made, and consequences of significant architectural choices.

text: What is the strangler fig pattern in architecture migration?
options: Running both old and new systems permanently without ever completing the migration work
Immediately shutting down the old system and deploying the new one all at once together
Abandoning the migration and keeping the legacy system unchanged for all future operation
Gradually replacing parts of a legacy system with new components until fully migrated
explanation: The strangler fig pattern incrementally replaces legacy components until the old system is fully replaced.

text: How does a message queue improve system architecture?
options: It tightly couples all components requiring synchronous communication between every service
It decouples producers and consumers allowing asynchronous communication and load leveling
It reduces system reliability by introducing additional points of failure without any benefit
It eliminates the need for any communication between system components or services at all
explanation: Message queues decouple components, enable asynchronous communication, and provide load leveling.

text: What is the CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern?
options: Separating read and write operations into different models for optimization flexibility
Using the same data model and access path for both queries and commands in all cases
Combining all read and write operations into a single model for maximum simplicity only
Eliminating all read operations and only allowing write operations in the system design
explanation: CQRS separates read and write models, allowing each to be independently optimized.

text: What is the role of middleware in a distributed architecture?
options: It only stores data and has no role in communication or transformation between services
It provides communication, data transformation, and integration between distributed components
It eliminates the need for any networking between distributed system components entirely
It replaces all business logic components and handles every processing requirement directly
explanation: Middleware facilitates communication, data transformation, and integration in distributed systems.

text: A company needs to migrate a monolithic e-commerce platform to microservices. What is the recommended architectural migration strategy?
options: Identify bounded contexts, extract services incrementally, and use an API gateway for routing
Rewrite the entire system from scratch in a new language without referencing existing code
Keep the monolith exactly as is and simply rename it as a microservices architecture now
Split the monolith randomly into services without analyzing domain boundaries or dependencies
explanation: Incremental extraction based on bounded contexts with an API gateway is the safest migration approach.

text: How does the CAP theorem constrain the design of distributed database architectures?
options: The CAP theorem only applies to relational databases and not to any NoSQL database systems
A distributed system can guarantee at most two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
Partition tolerance is optional in distributed systems and can be safely ignored in all designs
A distributed system can easily achieve all three properties simultaneously without any trade-offs
explanation: CAP theorem states distributed systems can guarantee at most two of: consistency, availability, partition tolerance.

text: A streaming analytics platform must process ten million events per second with sub-second latency. Which architectural pattern is most suitable?
options: Single-threaded monolithic application processing events sequentially one at a time in order
Batch processing architecture that collects events for hourly processing in large bulk operations
Traditional request-response architecture with synchronous database queries for each single event
Event sourcing with partitioned stream processing and in-memory computation for hot data paths
explanation: Event sourcing with partitioned streams and in-memory processing handles high-throughput, low-latency needs.

text: What are the architectural implications of choosing eventual consistency over strong consistency?
options: Eventual consistency has no implications and behaves identically to strong consistency in all cases
Strong consistency always provides better performance than eventual consistency in every scenario
Eventual consistency eliminates all data inconsistency problems permanently without any trade-offs
The system gains availability and performance but must handle temporary data inconsistencies gracefully
explanation: Eventual consistency improves availability and performance but requires handling temporary inconsistencies.

text: How should an architect design a system to be resilient against cascading failures in a microservices environment?
options: Remove all error handling to reduce code complexity and improve overall system performance metrics
Implement circuit breakers, bulkheads, and graceful degradation patterns between all service boundaries
Use a single shared database for all services to eliminate any communication failures between them
Make every service synchronously dependent on every other service to ensure data consistency always
explanation: Circuit breakers, bulkheads, and graceful degradation prevent cascading failures across services.

text: What is the architectural significance of Conway's law when designing large-scale distributed systems?
options: Conway's law has been disproven and has no relevance to modern software architecture decisions
System architecture should be designed without any consideration of team organization structure
Team structure should align with desired system architecture to avoid communication mismatches
Conway's law only applies to monolithic systems and is irrelevant for distributed architectures
explanation: Conway's law suggests system architecture mirrors team communication structure, so alignment is essential.

text: A healthcare system requires 99.999% availability. How does this requirement influence the architectural decisions?
options: It requires a single centralized server to minimize complexity and potential points of failure in system
It mandates redundancy, automatic failover, geographic distribution, and elimination of single points of failure
It means the system should avoid any redundancy to reduce the number of components that can fail
It has no impact on architecture because availability is purely an operational and infrastructure concern
explanation: Five-nines availability requires redundancy, failover, geographic distribution, and no single points of failure.

text: How does the saga pattern handle distributed transactions across microservices?
options: It uses traditional two-phase commit protocol locking all services until the transaction completes fully
It coordinates a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions for failure rollback scenarios
It requires all microservices to share a single database to ensure transactional consistency always
It avoids any form of transaction management and accepts data corruption as an acceptable outcome
explanation: The saga pattern manages distributed transactions through local transactions with compensating rollbacks.

text: What architectural considerations are necessary when designing a multi-tenant SaaS application?
options: Multi-tenant applications should use a separate deployed instance for every individual tenant always
All tenants must share identical configurations with no customization options available for any tenant
Data isolation strategies, per-tenant customization, scalability, and fair resource allocation mechanisms
Multi-tenancy requires no special architectural considerations beyond a standard single-user application
explanation: Multi-tenant SaaS requires data isolation, customization, scalability, and fair resource allocation.

text: How does the sidecar pattern enhance service mesh architectures in cloud-native applications?
options: It deploys supporting functionality alongside each service without modifying the service code itself
It embeds all infrastructure concerns directly into business logic code of every service instance
It requires every service to implement its own service discovery and load balancing logic fully
It eliminates the need for any networking between services by running everything in one process
explanation: The sidecar pattern deploys infrastructure concerns alongside services without modifying their code.

text: What is user interface (UI) design?
options: Managing version control repositories for source code
Writing backend database queries for data retrieval tasks
Designing visual elements that users interact with on screen
Configuring server infrastructure for application hosting
explanation: UI design focuses on designing the visual and interactive elements users engage with.

text: What is usability in UI design?
options: The total number of colors used in the application theme
The programming language used to implement the interface
How easy and efficient it is for users to use the interface
The amount of data stored in the application database
explanation: Usability measures how easy, efficient, and satisfying an interface is to use.

text: What is a wireframe in UI design?
options: A basic visual layout showing the structure of a page
A database diagram showing tables and their relationships
A network map displaying server connections and topology
A fully functional prototype with all features implemented
explanation: A wireframe is a low-fidelity visual guide showing the basic structure and layout of a page.

text: What does 'responsive design' mean?
options: Design that adapts to different screen sizes and devices
Design that responds quickly to user input and actions
Design that avoids any visual elements on the interface
Design that uses only one fixed layout for all screens
explanation: Responsive design adapts the layout to work across different screen sizes and device types.

text: What is the purpose of user feedback in UI design?
options: To reduce the number of features available to end users
To understand user needs and improve the interface design
To increase the amount of code in the application files
To eliminate the need for any future design improvements
explanation: User feedback helps designers understand needs and iteratively improve the interface.

text: What is a call-to-action (CTA) in UI design?
options: A prompt encouraging users to take a specific action step
A backend process running on the application server side
A database constraint enforcing data integrity rules now
A technical error message displayed during system failure
explanation: A CTA is a visual element prompting users to take a desired action, like signing up or buying.

text: What is navigation in user interface design?
options: The server-side logic that processes user form inputs
The encryption algorithm protecting sensitive user data
The means by which users move through an application
The database indexing strategy for faster data queries
explanation: Navigation refers to the system of elements that allows users to move through an application.

text: What is visual hierarchy in UI design?
options: Hiding important elements behind multiple menu layers
Removing all visual distinctions between page elements
Arranging elements to guide user attention by importance
Placing all elements at the same size and visual weight
explanation: Visual hierarchy arranges elements to guide the user's attention based on importance.

text: What is the goal of accessibility in UI design?
options: Making interfaces only usable by experienced tech users
Reducing the number of interactive elements on a page
Restricting access to the interface based on user roles
Making interfaces usable by people with diverse abilities
explanation: Accessibility ensures interfaces are usable by people of all abilities, including those with disabilities.

text: What is a tooltip in user interface design?
options: A small popup providing information when hovering over an element
A large modal window requiring user input before proceeding further
A background service processing data without any user interaction
A database record storing user preferences and account settings
explanation: A tooltip is a brief informational popup that appears when a user hovers over a UI element.

text: What are Nielsen's ten usability heuristics used for?
options: Evaluating user interface designs for common usability problems and issues
Configuring network firewalls for protecting against security threats
Designing database schemas for storing application data efficiently now
Writing backend server code for processing user authentication requests
explanation: Nielsen's heuristics are guidelines for evaluating UI designs against common usability principles.

text: What is the difference between UI design and UX design?
options: UI and UX are identical disciplines with no differences in scope or focus areas
UI covers project management; UX handles server infrastructure configuration
UI focuses on visual elements; UX encompasses the entire user experience journey
UI deals with backend logic; UX focuses exclusively on database design work now
explanation: UI focuses on visual interface elements; UX covers the broader user experience including interactions and flows.

text: What is Fitts' law and how does it apply to UI design?
options: Smaller and distant targets are easier to click, so buttons should be minimized always
Fitts' law only applies to physical devices and has no relevance to digital interfaces
Larger and closer targets are faster to select, influencing button size and placement
Target size has no impact on user interaction speed or accuracy in any interface now
explanation: Fitts' law states that time to reach a target depends on distance and size, guiding button placement.

text: How does the principle of consistency improve user interfaces?
options: It makes the interface unpredictable so users must think carefully about every action
It allows users to apply learned behaviors across the interface reducing cognitive load
It forces every page to look completely different from all other pages in the system
It increases the time users need to learn how to navigate and use the application
explanation: Consistency lets users transfer learned behaviors across the interface, reducing learning time.

text: What is a design system in the context of UI development?
options: A single page design template that cannot be customized or reused across the application
A collection of reusable components and guidelines ensuring visual and behavioral consistency
A database management system for storing user interface configuration data and settings
A server monitoring tool for tracking application performance metrics and error rates
explanation: A design system provides reusable components and guidelines for consistent UI development.

text: What is the purpose of A/B testing in UI design?
options: Simultaneously releasing both design variants as permanent features for all users
Testing the application's backend server performance under heavy load conditions
Validating the database schema against the documented requirements specification
Comparing two design variants to determine which performs better with real users
explanation: A/B testing compares two design variants with real users to determine which performs better.

text: How does progressive disclosure improve complex user interfaces?
options: It reveals information gradually, showing only what is needed at each step of interaction
It hides all information permanently and never reveals anything to the user at any time
It displays all possible options and information simultaneously on a single crowded screen
It forces users to read complete documentation before they can interact with the system
explanation: Progressive disclosure reduces complexity by revealing information and options only when needed.

text: What is the importance of color contrast in UI design?
options: It ensures text and elements are readable for users including those with visual impairments
High contrast should be avoided because it makes interfaces harder for everyone to read
Color contrast only matters for printed materials and is irrelevant for digital interfaces
It is purely decorative and has no impact on readability or accessibility of the interface
explanation: Adequate color contrast ensures readability and accessibility for users with visual impairments.

text: What is a persona in user-centered design?
options: A software bot that automatically generates user interface layouts without human input
A legal document defining the terms of service between the company and its end users
A real person hired to test the application before it is released to the general public
A fictional character representing a target user group based on research data analysis
explanation: A persona is a research-based fictional character representing a key user group's characteristics and goals.

text: What is the purpose of user journey mapping in UI design?
options: To create the database schema for storing all application data and configurations
To write the server-side code for processing API requests from the client side
To configure the deployment pipeline for releasing the application to production
To visualize the complete user experience across all touchpoints and interactions
explanation: User journey mapping visualizes the complete experience across all touchpoints to identify improvement areas.

text: A healthcare application is used by elderly patients with limited technical experience. Which UI design principles should be prioritized?
options: Large touch targets, high contrast, simple navigation, clear typography, and minimal cognitive load
Advanced keyboard shortcuts, minimal visual feedback, and hidden navigation behind icon-only menus
Small fonts, complex multi-level menus, gesture-only navigation, and low contrast color schemes
Dense information displays, small buttons, animation-heavy interfaces, and technical terminology use
explanation: Elderly users benefit from large targets, high contrast, simple navigation, and reduced cognitive load.

text: How does cognitive load theory influence the number of options presented in a UI at one time?
options: The number of options has no measurable impact on user performance or satisfaction in any context
Cognitive load theory suggests options should never be limited because users can process infinite items
Too many options increase cognitive load and decision time, following Hick's law principles closely
Users always prefer having maximum options displayed simultaneously regardless of task complexity
explanation: Hick's law and cognitive load theory show that more options increase decision time and mental effort.

text: A team is designing a dashboard for data analysts who monitor real-time metrics. What design pattern best supports their workflow?
options: A slideshow that automatically rotates through metrics without any user control or interaction
Full-screen modal dialogs for each individual metric requiring sequential manual navigation
A single fixed text-based report with no interactive elements or visualization capabilities
Overview plus detail pattern with drill-down capabilities and customizable widget arrangements
explanation: Overview plus detail with drill-down and customization supports data analysts' monitoring workflows.

text: How should error messages be designed to minimize user frustration and support error recovery?
options: Cryptic error codes without any explanation or guidance for users on how to resolve the problem
Blaming the user for causing the error and demanding they contact technical support immediately
Clear language explaining what went wrong, why it happened, and specific steps to fix the issue
Hiding all errors silently without any notification so the user does not experience any frustration
explanation: Effective error messages explain the problem, its cause, and provide clear recovery steps.

text: What is the impact of animation and micro-interactions on perceived application performance?
options: All animations slow down perceived performance and should be completely eliminated from every app
Animations should only be used for decorative purposes and never for functional feedback to users
Animations have no measurable impact on how users perceive the speed of the application interface
Well-designed animations mask loading times and provide continuity making the app feel faster overall
explanation: Purposeful animations and micro-interactions improve perceived performance by providing continuity and feedback.

text: A multi-language application serves users across twenty countries. What UI design challenges must be addressed?
options: Text expansion and contraction, RTL layout support, cultural color meanings, and locale-specific formats
Internationalization only requires translating text strings and has no impact on layout or design work
All languages and cultures use identical UI conventions so no design adaptations are ever necessary
Multi-language support only affects backend code and has no visible impact on the user interface
explanation: Internationalization requires handling text length variation, RTL support, cultural norms, and locale formats.

text: How does the concept of 'dark patterns' in UI design undermine user trust?
options: Dark patterns refer exclusively to using dark color themes and have no ethical implications at all
Dark patterns manipulate users into unintended actions through deceptive design techniques intentionally
Dark patterns are always beneficial because they guide users toward the best possible decisions
Dark patterns have no impact on user trust because most users cannot detect manipulative design
explanation: Dark patterns deliberately manipulate users into unintended actions, eroding trust in the product.

text: A financial trading application must display rapidly changing data without overwhelming the user. How should the UI handle this?
options: Use identical visual treatment for all data regardless of importance or rate of change observed
Use visual prioritization, controlled update frequencies, and highlight only significant data changes
Remove all real-time data and only show daily summary reports to reduce information overload
Display every data change immediately with full-screen alerts for each individual price movement
explanation: Visual prioritization, controlled updates, and change highlighting prevent information overload.

text: What is the relationship between information architecture and UI design in creating effective digital products?
options: Information architecture defines content structure and organization that UI design then visualizes
UI design determines information architecture since visual layout always precedes content structure
Information architecture only applies to physical spaces and has no relevance to digital products
Information architecture and UI design are completely unrelated disciplines with no overlap at all
explanation: Information architecture defines content structure; UI design creates the visual layer for that structure.

text: How should a design team approach creating an inclusive design that works for users with motor, visual, cognitive, and auditory disabilities?
options: Design only for the average user and provide a separate simplified version for disabled users later
Assistive technologies handle all accessibility needs so designers do not need to consider them now
Follow WCAG guidelines, test with assistive technologies, and involve users with disabilities in testing
Accessibility is a legal requirement only and does not require any changes to the actual design work
explanation: Inclusive design follows WCAG, tests with assistive tech, and involves users with disabilities in the process.

text: What is source code in software development?
options: A network configuration file for setting up server connections
The physical hardware on which software applications run today
Human-readable instructions written in a programming language
A project management document describing the timeline and budget
explanation: Source code consists of human-readable instructions written in a programming language.

text: What is a compiler used for in software development?
options: Translating source code into machine-executable binary code
Creating database schemas and defining table relationships
Designing the user interface layout for web applications only
Managing project team members and assigning work to them
explanation: A compiler translates source code into machine-executable binary code.

text: What is code refactoring?
options: Removing all comments and documentation from the source code
Deploying the application to a new production server environment
Restructuring existing code without changing its external behavior
Adding new features and functionality to the software product
explanation: Refactoring improves code structure and readability without changing its external behavior.

text: What is version control in software development?
options: A system for tracking and managing changes to source code files
A tool for managing hardware inventory in the server data room
A method for testing the application performance under load now
A technique for designing the user interface with color themes
explanation: Version control tracks and manages changes to source code over time.

text: What is the purpose of code comments?
options: To explain the intent and logic of code for future readers
To encrypt the source code to prevent unauthorized access now
To increase the execution speed of the compiled application
To add new features without modifying any existing source code
explanation: Code comments explain intent and logic, making code easier to understand and maintain.

text: What is an Integrated Development Environment (IDE)?
options: A database management system for storing application data now
A software tool combining editor, compiler, and debugger features
A physical room where developers work together on a project
A hardware device used for testing software on mobile devices
explanation: An IDE combines code editing, compilation, debugging, and other tools in one application.

text: What is a syntax error in programming?
options: A violation of the programming language grammar and structure rules
A performance issue that causes the application to run very slowly
A logical mistake that produces incorrect output from the program
A design flaw in the system architecture causing scalability issues
explanation: A syntax error occurs when code violates the grammar rules of the programming language.

text: What is debugging in software development?
options: Finding and fixing defects and errors in the source code logic
Deploying the application to the production server environment
Writing new source code to add features to the application
Designing the database schema for the application data store
explanation: Debugging is the process of finding, analyzing, and fixing defects in source code.

text: What is a code review?
options: Running automated performance tests on the production environment
Deleting all source code and starting the project from scratch now
Installing software updates on all developer workstation machines
Examining source code by peers to find defects and improve quality
explanation: Code review is peer examination of source code to find defects and improve quality.

text: What is the purpose of coding standards?
options: To prevent any developer from modifying code written by other people
To ensure consistent code style and quality across the development team
To eliminate the need for testing by ensuring code is always correct
To restrict developers from using any external libraries or frameworks
explanation: Coding standards ensure consistency, readability, and maintainability across the codebase.

text: What is the difference between compiled and interpreted languages?
options: Compiled languages run slower than interpreted languages in all performance benchmarks and tests
Compiled languages translate code before execution; interpreted languages translate during execution
Compiled and interpreted languages are identical with no differences in execution approach at all
Interpreted languages cannot be used for building any production-grade software applications
explanation: Compiled languages translate code to machine code before running; interpreted languages translate during execution.

text: What is continuous integration (CI) in software development?
options: Manually combining all code at the end of the project without any automated testing or builds
Writing all source code in a single file to avoid any merge conflicts between team members
Deploying code directly to production without any testing or review processes being performed
Frequently merging code changes into a shared repository with automated build and test validation
explanation: CI frequently merges code changes into a shared repository with automated builds and tests.

text: How does pair programming improve code quality?
options: It reduces code quality because two developers disagree and produce inconsistent solutions
It doubles development speed by having two people type simultaneously on the same keyboard
It eliminates the need for any code reviews since the code is already reviewed during writing
Real-time review catches errors immediately and promotes knowledge sharing between developers
explanation: Pair programming provides immediate code review and facilitates knowledge sharing.

text: What is technical debt and how does it affect implementation?
options: Accumulated shortcuts requiring future rework that slows down development and increases costs
A financial investment made by the company to purchase better development tools and hardware
A type of software license that requires payment for each line of code written in the project
A project management metric measuring the total budget remaining for the development effort
explanation: Technical debt accumulates from shortcuts, requiring future rework that slows development.

text: What is the purpose of unit testing during implementation?
options: To check the project schedule against the planned milestones and delivery date targets
To test the entire application system from end to end including all integrated components
To validate the user interface design against the approved wireframes and mockup designs
To verify individual code components work correctly in isolation from other system parts
explanation: Unit testing verifies individual components work correctly in isolation.

text: What is dependency injection and why is it useful?
options: Providing dependencies externally rather than creating them internally for better testability
Creating all dependencies inside the class constructor to simplify initialization logic now
Injecting database records directly into the application without using any query language
Removing all dependencies from a class so it operates completely independently of others
explanation: Dependency injection provides dependencies externally, improving testability and flexibility.

text: What is the purpose of a build automation tool?
options: To create project management reports showing team productivity and budget utilization data
To manually compile each source code file individually without any automated processes at all
To design user interfaces by automatically generating HTML and CSS from wireframe images
To automate compilation, testing, packaging, and deployment of software applications reliably
explanation: Build automation tools automate compilation, testing, packaging, and deployment processes.

text: How does static code analysis help during implementation?
options: It analyzes the application user interface for usability problems and accessibility violations
It replaces the need for any manual code review by automatically fixing all detected issues
It identifies potential bugs, security vulnerabilities, and style violations without running code
It only detects errors that occur during runtime execution of the application in production
explanation: Static analysis identifies potential issues in code without executing it.

text: What is the difference between a framework and a library?
options: Frameworks are always superior to libraries for all software development projects and tasks
Frameworks and libraries are identical concepts with no meaningful differences between them
A framework controls the flow and calls your code; a library is called by your code directly
A library controls application flow; a framework provides utility functions called by code
explanation: A framework dictates application flow (inversion of control); a library provides utilities you call.

text: What is containerization and how does it benefit implementation?
options: Packaging applications with dependencies into isolated containers ensuring consistent environments
Running applications directly on the host operating system without any isolation or packaging
Manually configuring each server individually to match the development environment settings
Removing all application dependencies to reduce the size of the deployed software package
explanation: Containerization packages applications with dependencies into isolated, consistent environments.

text: A team is experiencing frequent merge conflicts in a large codebase. What implementation practices would most effectively reduce this problem?
options: Larger infrequent commits combining many changes to reduce the total number of merge operations
Smaller frequent commits, feature branching strategies, and modular code with clear ownership areas
Having all developers work on the same branch and file simultaneously without any coordination
Avoiding version control entirely and using shared network drives for all source code storage
explanation: Smaller commits, strategic branching, and modular code with clear ownership reduce merge conflicts.

text: How does test-driven development (TDD) change the implementation workflow compared to traditional development?
options: Tests are completely eliminated from the development process to accelerate feature delivery speed
TDD and traditional development follow identical workflows with no differences in approach at all
Implementation is completed fully before any tests are considered or written for the codebase
Tests are written before implementation code, driving design decisions and ensuring test coverage
explanation: TDD writes tests first, then implements code to pass those tests, ensuring coverage and driving design.

text: A legacy codebase has no automated tests. What is the safest approach to introduce testing?
options: Delete all existing code and replace it with test stubs that will be implemented later on
Skip testing entirely since the codebase has worked without tests for its entire lifetime so far
Write characterization tests capturing current behavior before making any code changes to system
Rewrite the entire codebase from scratch with tests rather than adding tests to existing code
explanation: Characterization tests capture current behavior, providing a safety net before making changes.

text: How does feature flag implementation enable continuous deployment while managing risk?
options: Feature flags completely prevent any bugs from reaching the production environment automatically
Feature flags are only useful for UI changes and cannot be applied to backend logic or services
Flags allow deploying code to production with features disabled, enabling gradual controlled rollout
Flags eliminate the need for any testing since features can be toggled off if problems occur now
explanation: Feature flags deploy code to production with features disabled, allowing gradual, controlled rollout.

text: What is the impact of choosing a monorepo versus polyrepo strategy on implementation workflow?
options: Monorepos are only suitable for small projects and cannot scale to large enterprise codebases
Monorepos and polyrepos have identical workflow implications with no meaningful differences at all
Monorepos simplify cross-project changes but require sophisticated build and CI tooling at scale
Polyrepos are always superior because they eliminate all cross-project dependency management needs
explanation: Monorepos simplify cross-project changes and refactoring but need sophisticated tooling at scale.

text: A performance-critical system component is a bottleneck. How should the team approach optimization?
options: Apply every known optimization technique to all code regardless of whether it is a bottleneck
Rewrite the entire codebase in assembly language without profiling or measuring performance first
Profile to identify actual bottlenecks, optimize measured hot paths, and benchmark improvements
Avoid any optimization since premature optimization is the root of all evil in every situation
explanation: Profile-guided optimization identifies real bottlenecks and measures the impact of improvements.

text: How does clean code philosophy address the trade-off between code readability and cleverness?
options: It treats readability and cleverness as identical qualities that never conflict with each other
It suggests that code readability is irrelevant since compilers handle all code interpretation
It encourages maximally clever code because shorter code is always better regardless of clarity
It prioritizes readability over cleverness because code is read far more often than it is written
explanation: Clean code prioritizes readability over cleverness because code is read much more often than written.

text: What are the implementation challenges of handling distributed system failures gracefully?
options: All distributed failures can be prevented entirely through proper implementation techniques alone
Distributed systems never experience failures because modern networks are perfectly reliable now
Network partitions, partial failures, and timeout handling require careful idempotent retry design
Failure handling is identical in distributed and single-machine systems with no added complexity
explanation: Distributed systems face network partitions, partial failures, and timeouts requiring idempotent retry logic.

text: How does immutable infrastructure change the deployment and implementation practices?
options: Immutable infrastructure prevents any updates or patches from being applied to the system ever
Immutable infrastructure is identical to traditional mutable server management in all aspects
Servers are continuously patched and modified in place to apply updates and configuration changes
Servers are never modified after creation; changes require building and deploying new instances
explanation: Immutable infrastructure replaces servers instead of modifying them, ensuring consistency.

text: What is the role of code complexity metrics like cyclomatic complexity in guiding implementation decisions?
options: Complexity metrics should only be used for documentation purposes and never influence coding work
Higher complexity always means better code because it handles more edge cases and scenarios now
Cyclomatic complexity has no correlation with defect rates or maintenance difficulty in any study
High complexity indicates code that is harder to test, maintain, and more likely to contain defects
explanation: High cyclomatic complexity correlates with increased defect rates and maintenance difficulty.

text: What is the main purpose of software testing?
options: To design the user interface layout and visual style elements
To deploy the application to the production server environment
To find defects and verify the software meets its requirements
To write the source code for the application being developed
explanation: Software testing aims to find defects and verify the software meets specified requirements.

text: What is a test case in software testing?
options: A set of conditions and expected results for verifying functionality
A hardware component required for running the test environment
A programming language used for writing application source code
A project management document listing all team member assignments
explanation: A test case defines conditions, inputs, and expected results for verifying specific functionality.

text: What is unit testing?
options: Testing network connectivity between all production server nodes
Testing the entire integrated system from end to end completely
Testing the user interface for visual design and layout accuracy
Testing individual components or functions in isolation from others
explanation: Unit testing verifies individual components or functions work correctly in isolation.

text: What is integration testing?
options: Testing the project schedule against the planned delivery dates
Testing how multiple components work together as a combined unit
Testing individual functions in complete isolation from all others
Testing the application user interface for accessibility standards
explanation: Integration testing verifies that multiple components work correctly together.

text: What is a bug or defect in software?
options: A new feature requested by the client during the development phase
A hardware failure in the production server causing system downtime
A scheduled maintenance window for updating the operating system
A flaw causing the software to produce incorrect or unexpected results
explanation: A bug is a flaw in the software that causes incorrect or unexpected behavior.

text: What is regression testing?
options: Testing new features for the first time before release to production
Removing features from the software to simplify the application now
Retesting software after changes to ensure existing features still work
Writing new source code to implement additional system requirements
explanation: Regression testing verifies that recent changes haven't broken existing functionality.

text: What is system testing?
options: Testing a single function or method in isolation from all other code
Testing only the network configuration of the production servers
Testing only the database queries for correctness and performance
Testing the complete integrated system against specified requirements
explanation: System testing validates the complete, integrated system against its specified requirements.

text: What is the difference between manual and automated testing?
options: Manual testing is done by humans; automated testing uses scripts and tools
Automated testing cannot find any bugs that manual testing would miss
Manual and automated testing produce identical results in all scenarios
Manual testing is always faster than automated testing in every situation
explanation: Manual testing uses human testers; automated testing uses scripts and tools to execute tests.

text: What is acceptance testing?
options: Testing the network bandwidth between the client and server nodes
Testing to determine if the system satisfies the acceptance criteria
Testing individual database tables for data integrity constraints
Testing the internal code structure for compliance with style guides
explanation: Acceptance testing determines whether the system satisfies agreed-upon acceptance criteria.

text: What is a test plan?
options: A database query for retrieving user account information data
A document describing the testing scope, approach, and schedule
A network diagram showing the production server architecture
A source code file containing the main application entry point
explanation: A test plan documents the scope, approach, resources, and schedule for testing activities.

text: What is the difference between black-box and white-box testing?
options: Black-box testing is always superior to white-box testing for finding all types of software bugs
Black-box tests functionality without code knowledge; white-box tests with code structure knowledge
Black-box and white-box testing are identical approaches with no meaningful differences at all
Black-box testing examines code structure; white-box testing ignores internal implementation fully
explanation: Black-box tests based on specifications; white-box tests based on internal code structure knowledge.

text: What is boundary value analysis in software testing?
options: Avoiding all edge cases and testing only the most common inputs that users are expected to use
Testing only the maximum possible input value and ignoring all other boundary conditions now
Testing at the edges of input ranges where defects are most likely to occur in the software
Testing random values from the middle of input ranges without focusing on any specific values
explanation: Boundary value analysis tests at input range boundaries where defects frequently occur.

text: What is equivalence partitioning?
options: Testing every possible input value individually to ensure complete coverage of the system
Randomly selecting test inputs without any systematic approach to input space partitioning
Removing all invalid inputs from the test suite to focus only on valid expected scenarios
Dividing inputs into groups where all values in a group are expected to behave identically
explanation: Equivalence partitioning divides input into classes where values in each class behave the same.

text: What is code coverage and why does it matter?
options: It measures the percentage of code exercised by tests indicating thoroughness of test suite
It counts the total number of lines of code in the project regardless of testing activities
It measures the speed at which the application executes under normal operating conditions
It tracks the number of developers who have reviewed each source code file in the project
explanation: Code coverage measures what percentage of code is exercised by tests, indicating test thoroughness.

text: What is mutation testing?
options: Changing the programming language of the application to improve its overall test coverage
Introducing small changes to code to check if tests can detect the introduced modifications
Modifying the project requirements to make them easier to test with existing test suites
Deleting all test cases and relying on manual testing for all quality assurance activities
explanation: Mutation testing introduces small code changes (mutants) to verify tests can detect them.

text: What is performance testing?
options: Checking the visual design of the user interface against approved wireframes and mockups
Validating that the project schedule aligns with the planned milestones and target dates
Verifying that individual functions return correct results for all valid input combinations
Evaluating system behavior under various load conditions to identify bottlenecks and limits
explanation: Performance testing evaluates system behavior under load to identify bottlenecks and capacity limits.

text: What is the purpose of test-driven development (TDD)?
options: Writing tests before code to drive design and ensure comprehensive test coverage from start
Using test results to determine developer salary bonuses at the end of each project phase
Eliminating all testing activities to focus developer time on feature implementation only
Writing code first and then deciding which tests to write based on the bugs found later
explanation: TDD writes tests before code, driving design and ensuring coverage from the beginning.

text: What is exploratory testing?
options: Simultaneous test design, execution, and learning without predefined scripts or test cases
Following a strictly predefined test script without any deviation or creativity in approach
Running only automated tests without any human involvement in the testing process at all
Exploring the source code without executing the application to find potential defect areas
explanation: Exploratory testing simultaneously designs and executes tests based on tester learning and intuition.

text: What is the testing pyramid concept?
options: Only unit tests are needed and integration and end-to-end tests should be completely avoided
More end-to-end tests at the base and fewer unit tests at the top of the testing pyramid
More unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests, and fewest end-to-end tests at the top
Equal numbers of unit, integration, and end-to-end tests at every level of the testing pyramid
explanation: The testing pyramid advocates many unit tests, fewer integration tests, and fewest E2E tests.

text: What is the difference between verification and validation in testing?
options: Verification and validation are identical activities with no differences in their purposes
Verification focuses on user satisfaction; validation focuses on technical correctness only
Verification checks if built correctly; validation checks if the right product was built
Verification is done after deployment; validation is done before development starts only
explanation: Verification ensures correct construction; validation ensures the right product was built.

text: A team has 90% code coverage but still experiences production bugs. What does this reveal about their testing strategy?
options: High coverage does not guarantee test quality; tests may lack meaningful assertions and edge cases
Code coverage is meaningless and should be completely ignored as a testing quality metric now
The remaining ten percent of uncovered code contains all bugs and coverage should reach exactly full
Ninety percent coverage guarantees that ninety percent of all possible bugs have been detected
explanation: High coverage without meaningful assertions and edge case testing still misses defects.

text: How does property-based testing complement example-based testing?
options: It generates hundreds of random inputs to test properties that should hold for all valid inputs
It is identical to example-based testing and provides no additional value to the test suite now
It only tests a single predefined example and is less thorough than example-based approaches
It replaces example-based testing entirely and provides complete coverage of all possibilities
explanation: Property-based testing generates random inputs to verify invariants, finding edge cases examples miss.

text: A safety-critical aviation system requires DO-178C compliance. How does this affect the testing approach?
options: It allows the team to choose any testing approach without following any specific testing standards
It only requires basic unit testing without any structural coverage or formal verification work
It mandates structural coverage analysis, requirements-based testing, and formal verification methods
It eliminates the need for any testing because certification replaces all quality activities now
explanation: DO-178C requires structural coverage, requirements-based testing, and formal verification for safety-critical systems.

text: What is the oracle problem in software testing and how does it affect test automation?
options: The oracle problem has been completely solved and no longer affects any testing activities today
The oracle problem only applies to manual testing and has no impact on automated testing work
Difficulty in determining expected outputs for complex scenarios limits automated test effectiveness
It refers to the challenge of selecting which database system to use for storing test data only
explanation: The oracle problem is the difficulty of determining expected results, limiting automated verification.

text: How does chaos engineering differ from traditional testing approaches?
options: It replaces all traditional testing and eliminates the need for unit and integration test suites
It intentionally introduces failures in production to verify system resilience and recovery capability
It follows traditional scripted test cases executed in a controlled staging environment only today
It avoids any deliberate failure injection and relies on monitoring to detect issues passively
explanation: Chaos engineering deliberately introduces production failures to verify system resilience.

text: A microservices architecture has complex service interactions. What testing strategy best addresses integration risks?
options: Full end-to-end testing of every possible service interaction path for complete coverage goal
Contract testing verifying service interfaces plus consumer-driven contracts between service pairs
Skipping all testing and relying on production monitoring to detect integration failures only
Only unit testing individual services without any integration or contract testing between them
explanation: Contract testing with consumer-driven contracts effectively verifies microservice integration.

text: What is the relationship between testability and software design quality?
options: Poorly designed code is always easier to test than well-designed code due to simpler structure
Highly testable code typically exhibits good design qualities like loose coupling and high cohesion
Testability has no relationship with design quality and they are completely independent concerns
Testability should never influence design decisions because it compromises performance always
explanation: Testable code correlates with good design: loose coupling, high cohesion, and clear interfaces.

text: How should a team prioritize test automation efforts when resources are limited?
options: Automate high-value repetitive tests first: smoke tests, regression suites, and critical path scenarios
Automate only the tests that are easiest to write regardless of their value to the project now
Avoid all automation and rely entirely on manual testing regardless of the available resources
Automate random tests without considering their value or frequency of execution by the team
explanation: Prioritize automating high-value, frequently run tests: smoke tests, regression suites, critical paths.

text: What is the impact of flaky tests on development team productivity and confidence?
options: Flaky tests are beneficial because they simulate real-world conditions and improve test coverage
Flaky tests improve team productivity by adding unpredictability that keeps developers alert now
Flaky tests erode confidence in the test suite and cause teams to ignore legitimate test failures
Flaky tests have no measurable impact on team productivity or confidence in the testing suite
explanation: Flaky tests undermine confidence in the test suite, causing teams to ignore real failures.

text: How does shift-left testing change the economics of defect detection in software projects?
options: Testing earlier increases costs because more comprehensive testing infrastructure is needed upfront
Finding defects earlier in development is significantly cheaper than finding them in production later
Shift-left testing has no impact on the cost of defect detection at any stage of development
Defects found in production are always cheaper to fix than those found during early development
explanation: Shift-left testing finds defects earlier when they are significantly cheaper to fix.

text: What is software maintenance?
options: Modifying software after delivery to correct faults or improve performance
Writing the initial source code for a brand new software application
Designing the system architecture before any development work begins
Gathering user requirements during the early project planning phases
explanation: Software maintenance involves modifying software after delivery to fix defects or add improvements.

text: What is corrective maintenance?
options: Fixing bugs and defects discovered after software has been deployed
Adapting software to work in a new operating system environment
Improving performance without changing any existing functionality
Adding brand new features requested by the users after deployment
explanation: Corrective maintenance fixes bugs and defects found after the software is deployed.

text: What is adaptive maintenance?
options: Adding new features that were not in the original requirements set
Fixing defects found during production use of the deployed system
Modifying software to work in a changed or new environment context
Restructuring code to improve readability without behavior change
explanation: Adaptive maintenance modifies software to work in changed environments like new OS versions.

text: What is perfective maintenance?
options: Fixing critical bugs that prevent the system from functioning at all
Removing the software from production and replacing it with a new one
Modifying the software to run on a completely different platform now
Enhancing software by adding new features or improving existing ones
explanation: Perfective maintenance enhances software by adding features or improving existing functionality.

text: What percentage of total software costs does maintenance typically represent?
options: About twenty percent of total lifecycle costs
Over sixty percent of total lifecycle costs
Less than five percent of total lifecycle costs
Exactly ten percent of total lifecycle costs
explanation: Maintenance typically accounts for over 60% of total software lifecycle costs.

text: What is preventive maintenance?
options: Making changes to prevent future problems and improve maintainability
Adding features only when explicitly requested by the project sponsor
Fixing bugs only after users report them in the production environment
Removing all documentation from the codebase to simplify the system
explanation: Preventive maintenance makes changes to prevent future problems and improve maintainability.

text: What is a software patch?
options: A full version upgrade adding many new features and capabilities now
A small update released to fix specific bugs or security issues quickly
A complete rewrite of the entire application from scratch using new code
A hardware replacement required to run updated software on the server
explanation: A patch is a small, targeted update that fixes specific bugs or security vulnerabilities.

text: Why is documentation important for software maintenance?
options: It increases the file size of the project without adding any value
It is only useful during initial development and never after that
It helps maintainers understand the system to make changes correctly
It slows down maintenance by requiring reading before any changes
explanation: Documentation helps maintenance teams understand the system to make accurate and effective changes.

text: What is software evolution?
options: The continuous process of changing software throughout its lifetime
A one-time event that happens only during initial development phase
The process of permanently discontinuing a software product fully
A testing technique applied only before the first release of code
explanation: Software evolution is the continuous process of adapting and improving software throughout its lifetime.

text: What is legacy software?
options: Brand new software developed using the latest programming frameworks
A type of testing framework used exclusively for mobile applications
Older software that remains in use despite outdated technology choices
A project management methodology for small startup development teams
explanation: Legacy software is older software still in use despite being built with outdated technology.

text: What is Lehman's law of increasing complexity?
options: Software complexity naturally decreases over time without any deliberate effort being made
Software complexity increases with each change unless work is done to reduce it actively
Software complexity remains constant throughout the entire lifetime of the application now
Software complexity is only affected by the initial design and never by later modifications
explanation: Lehman's law states that software complexity grows with each modification unless actively managed.

text: How does software aging affect system reliability over time?
options: Aging has no measurable effect on software reliability because code does not physically degrade
Software reliability improves automatically as hardware becomes faster and more powerful now
Software always becomes more reliable over time as more bugs are found and fixed in system
Accumulated changes and environmental drift gradually degrade system reliability and performance
explanation: Software aging causes gradual degradation through accumulated changes and environmental drift.

text: What is the difference between re-engineering and reverse engineering?
options: Re-engineering recovers design knowledge; reverse engineering restructures for improvements now
Reverse engineering and re-engineering are identical processes with no differences between them
Reverse engineering adds new features; re-engineering only fixes bugs in the existing codebase
Reverse engineering recovers design; re-engineering restructures the system for improvement work
explanation: Reverse engineering recovers design information; re-engineering restructures the system for improvement.

text: What is the impact of code smells on software maintenance effort?
options: Code smells always improve code quality by making the codebase more interesting to read now
Code smells indicate design problems that increase maintenance difficulty and defect likelihood
Code smells have no impact on maintenance because they do not represent actual defects today
Code smells are only cosmetic issues that never affect functionality or maintenance costs
explanation: Code smells signal design problems that make maintenance harder and increase defect probability.

text: How does modular design facilitate software maintenance?
options: Modular design makes maintenance harder because changes must be applied to every module now
Modular design has no relationship to maintenance effort or quality of the maintenance work
Modules prevent any changes from being made to the software after initial deployment to users
Changes can be isolated to specific modules without affecting the rest of the system at all
explanation: Modular design isolates changes to specific modules, reducing the risk and effort of maintenance.

text: What is the ripple effect in software maintenance?
options: A technique for efficiently propagating beneficial changes across all system modules quickly
A design pattern that ensures changes in one module are automatically applied everywhere now
Changes in one part of the system causing unintended effects in seemingly unrelated parts
A method for testing software by sending signals through all connected components sequentially
explanation: The ripple effect occurs when changes in one area cause unexpected consequences in other areas.

text: What is program comprehension and why is it critical for maintenance?
options: Understanding existing code is essential for making correct modifications without introducing bugs
Program comprehension is unnecessary because maintainers can change code without understanding it
Program comprehension only matters for documentation purposes and not for actual code changes
Understanding code is only important during initial development and never during maintenance work
explanation: Understanding existing code is critical for making accurate modifications without introducing new defects.

text: What role does impact analysis play in software maintenance?
options: It identifies all system areas affected by a proposed change before implementation begins now
It only identifies positive impacts and ignores any negative consequences of proposed changes
It is only performed after changes are deployed to assess the damage caused by the change
It has no role in maintenance because changes can be made without considering their effects
explanation: Impact analysis identifies all areas affected by a proposed change before it is implemented.

text: How does automated testing support software maintenance activities?
options: It eliminates the need for any human involvement in the maintenance decision-making process
It replaces all maintenance activities including code modification and deployment processes
It quickly validates that changes have not broken existing functionality through regression tests
It automatically fixes all bugs found during testing without any developer intervention needed
explanation: Automated tests quickly validate that maintenance changes haven't broken existing functionality.

text: What is software rejuvenation?
options: Adding cosmetic visual updates to the user interface without any functional changes at all
Permanently retiring software and replacing it with an entirely new system from scratch now
Periodically restarting or refreshing software to counteract performance degradation over time
Increasing the hardware resources allocated to the software without changing any code at all
explanation: Software rejuvenation periodically restarts or refreshes systems to counteract degradation.

text: A legacy system with no documentation or original developers must be maintained. What is the recommended approach?
options: Make changes randomly and deploy them immediately without understanding the system structure
Use reverse engineering and program comprehension techniques to recover design knowledge first
Refuse to maintain the system and recommend shutting it down immediately without replacement
Rewrite the entire system from scratch without studying the existing system behavior at all
explanation: Reverse engineering and program comprehension recover design knowledge needed for safe maintenance.

text: How does Lehman's law of conservation of organizational stability affect maintenance planning?
options: Adding more developers always proportionally increases the rate of maintenance activity and output
The rate of development activity tends to remain constant over a system's lifetime regardless of resources
Organizational stability has no measurable effect on maintenance planning or resource allocation
Maintenance effort always decreases over time as the system becomes more stable and mature now
explanation: Lehman's law states that development activity rate tends to remain constant regardless of resource changes.

text: A system experiences performance degradation every few months requiring manual restarts. What maintenance strategy addresses this systematically?
options: Add more hardware resources each time degradation occurs without investigating the root cause
Ignore the degradation until the system completely fails and then perform emergency maintenance
Manually restart the system only when users complain about noticeable performance problems now
Implement proactive software rejuvenation with automated scheduled restarts and resource cleanup
explanation: Proactive rejuvenation with scheduled restarts and cleanup systematically addresses performance degradation.

text: What is the relationship between software architecture erosion and increasing maintenance costs?
options: Architecture erosion increases coupling and decreases cohesion making changes progressively harder
Architecture erosion has no impact on maintenance costs because architecture is only relevant initially
Erosion decreases maintenance costs by simplifying the system structure through organic evolution now
Architecture quality always improves over time through maintenance activities without deliberate effort
explanation: Architecture erosion increases coupling and reduces cohesion, making maintenance progressively more expensive.

text: How should a maintenance team prioritize between corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive maintenance requests?
options: Preventive maintenance should always have lowest priority since it addresses only future problems
Prioritize critical corrections first, then assess adaptive, perfective, and preventive by business value
All maintenance types should be treated with equal priority without any systematic prioritization
Always complete all corrective maintenance before considering any other type of maintenance request
explanation: Critical corrections take priority; other types are assessed by business value and urgency.

text: What is the economic justification for investing in preventive maintenance of software systems?
options: Preventing defects is significantly cheaper than fixing them after they manifest in production use
There is no economic basis for preventive maintenance since all software eventually needs replacing
Preventive maintenance never provides any return on investment and should always be avoided now
The cost of preventive maintenance always exceeds the cost of fixing defects after they occur
explanation: Prevention is cheaper than cure; investing in preventive maintenance reduces future corrective costs.

text: How does microservices architecture change the maintenance approach compared to monolithic systems?
options: Individual services can be maintained, deployed, and scaled independently reducing maintenance scope
Microservices and monolithic systems require identical maintenance approaches without differences
Microservices eliminate all maintenance needs because services are small enough to rewrite quickly
Monolithic systems are always easier to maintain than microservices in every possible scenario now
