explanation: A PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) chart shows task dependencies and time estimates using a network diagram to plan and schedule projects.

text: What is 'effort estimation' in software project management?
options: Counting the number of lines of code already written in the project
Measuring the physical effort required to install server hardware
Calculating the electricity consumption of the development office
Predicting the amount of work needed to complete a software project
explanation: Effort estimation predicts the amount of work, typically measured in person-hours or person-months, needed to complete a software project or task.

text: What is the purpose of a project status report?
options: To communicate project progress, issues, and risks to stakeholders
To provide source code documentation for the development team
To describe the physical layout of the development workspace
To list all the programming languages known by the team members
explanation: Project status reports communicate current progress, issues, risks, and upcoming milestones to stakeholders, keeping everyone informed about the project state.

text: What is 'resource allocation' in project management?
options: Purchasing new office furniture for the development team members
Assigning available people, tools, and budget to project tasks and activities
Writing test cases for each requirement in the specification document
Deleting unused files from the project repository to save disk space
explanation: Resource allocation involves assigning available resources such as personnel, equipment, and budget to project activities to ensure efficient completion.

text: What is a project baseline?
options: The first line of code written in the project repository
The lowest priority feature in the product requirements backlog
An approved plan used as a reference point for measuring project performance
The minimum number of developers required for any software project
explanation: A project baseline is an approved version of the project plan that serves as a reference point for measuring and comparing actual project performance.

text: What is 'slack time' or 'float' in project scheduling?
options: The time developers spend on breaks during the working day
The time required to restart a failed build on the continuous integration server
The time spent waiting for customer approval of all project deliverables
The amount of time a task can be delayed without affecting the project end date
explanation: Slack or float is the amount of time an activity can be delayed from its earliest start without delaying the overall project completion date.

text: What does project scope define?
options: The boundaries of the project including what will and will not be delivered
The physical office space allocated to the development team members
The number of years the project manager has been with the company
The programming language and framework that must be used for the project
explanation: Project scope defines the boundaries of the project, specifying what features and deliverables are included and what is excluded from the project.

text: What is the purpose of a project kickoff meeting?
options: To celebrate the successful completion of all project deliverables
To formally start the project and align the team on goals and expectations
To terminate the project and release all allocated resources
To conduct the final user acceptance testing before deployment
explanation: A project kickoff meeting formally launches the project, aligns stakeholders and team members on objectives, roles, timelines, and expectations.

text: What is the COCOMO model used for in software engineering?
options: Designing the graphical user interface for web-based applications
Managing the version control system for collaborative code development
Estimating software development effort, cost, and schedule based on project size
Testing the performance of database queries under high concurrent load
explanation: COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model) estimates software project effort, cost, and schedule based on the size of the software, typically measured in lines of code.

text: What is 'earned value management' (EVM) in project management?
options: A process for evaluating the market value of the completed software product
A technique for calculating developer salaries based on years of experience
A system for tracking the value of office equipment used by the team
A method for measuring project performance by comparing planned work to actual progress and cost
explanation: EVM integrates scope, schedule, and cost data to objectively measure project performance, comparing planned value, earned value, and actual cost.

text: What is Brooks' Law and how does it affect project management?
options: Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later due to communication overhead
Projects with fewer developers always produce lower quality software products
The project schedule should always be doubled to account for unexpected delays
Adding more developers to a project always reduces the completion time proportionally
explanation: Brooks' Law states that adding people to a late software project makes it later because new members need training and communication overhead increases.

text: What is the difference between 'lead time' and 'lag time' in scheduling?
options: Lead time is the total project duration; lag time is the time spent in testing
Lead time allows a successor to start before its predecessor finishes; lag adds a waiting period
Lead time applies only to hardware tasks; lag time applies only to software tasks
Lead time and lag time are identical concepts used interchangeably in scheduling
explanation: Lead time allows a successor activity to start before its predecessor completes, while lag time adds a mandatory waiting period between activities.

text: What is 'function point analysis' used for?
options: Analyzing the performance of individual JavaScript functions at runtime
Measuring the number of functions in a programming language's library
Estimating software size based on the functionality delivered to users
Counting the number of function calls in the source code during testing
explanation: Function point analysis measures software size by quantifying the functionality delivered to users through inputs, outputs, inquiries, files, and interfaces.

text: What is the 'triple constraint' or 'iron triangle' in project management?
options: The relationship between team size, office space, and computer equipment
The three levels of management: junior, middle, and senior leadership
The three testing phases: unit testing, integration testing, and system testing
The balance between scope, time, and cost that defines project boundaries
explanation: The triple constraint represents the interdependency of scope, time, and cost, where changing one constraint affects the others and project quality.

text: How does 'Monte Carlo simulation' help in project estimation?
options: It runs multiple simulations with varying inputs to produce probability-based estimates
It randomly assigns tasks to developers without any consideration for skills
It provides a single exact estimate for project completion with guaranteed accuracy
It eliminates all uncertainty from project schedules and cost calculations
explanation: Monte Carlo simulation runs thousands of simulations with varying input assumptions to produce a range of possible outcomes with associated probabilities.

text: What is the purpose of a 'risk register' in project management?
options: To track the registration status of software licenses used by the team
To document identified risks, their likelihood, impact, and planned responses
To record the attendance of all team members at daily meetings
To register all team members for project-related training courses
explanation: A risk register documents identified risks along with their probability, impact, priority, and planned mitigation or contingency strategies.

text: What is 'crashing' in the context of project schedule compression?
options: Allowing the project to fail and starting over from the beginning
Reducing the scope of the project by eliminating non-essential features
Adding extra resources to critical path activities to reduce their duration
Removing all testing activities to shorten the overall project timeline
explanation: Crashing involves adding resources to critical path activities to reduce their duration, typically increasing cost to achieve a shorter schedule.

text: What is 'fast tracking' in project scheduling?
options: Increasing the team velocity by hiring additional senior developers
Skipping the testing phase to deliver the project earlier than planned
Using a faster programming language to reduce development time
Performing sequential activities in parallel to compress the project schedule
explanation: Fast tracking compresses the project schedule by performing activities that were originally planned sequentially in parallel, accepting increased risk.

text: What is the COCOMO II model's key improvement over the original COCOMO?
options: It accommodates modern development practices including reuse, prototyping, and iterative development
It uses only lines of code as the size metric, ignoring all other factors
It requires no calibration or adjustment for different types of projects
It eliminates the need for any project size estimation entirely
explanation: COCOMO II improves on the original by supporting modern practices like software reuse, prototyping, and iterative development, with multiple size estimation options.

text: What does the 'Schedule Performance Index' (SPI) indicate in earned value management?
options: The average number of overtime hours worked by the development team
The ratio of earned value to planned value, indicating schedule efficiency
The percentage of requirements that have been fully implemented and tested
The total number of defects found per testing cycle in the project
explanation: SPI equals earned value divided by planned value; an SPI greater than 1 indicates ahead of schedule, while less than 1 indicates behind schedule.

text: How does the 'Delphi technique' improve estimation accuracy?
options: It relies entirely on historical data without any expert input whatsoever
It automatically generates estimates from the project requirements document
It uses anonymous iterative rounds of expert estimation to reach consensus
It uses a single expert opinion to provide the most authoritative estimate
explanation: The Delphi technique uses multiple rounds of anonymous expert estimation with feedback, converging toward consensus while reducing bias from dominant personalities.

text: What is 'Putnam's SLIM model' and how does it differ from COCOMO?
options: SLIM is a testing framework while COCOMO is a design methodology
SLIM focuses on user interface design while COCOMO focuses on database design
SLIM and COCOMO are identical models with different names used interchangeably
SLIM uses the Rayleigh curve to model effort distribution over time while COCOMO uses regression equations
explanation: Putnam's SLIM model uses the Rayleigh distribution to model staffing and effort over time, while COCOMO uses regression equations based on project size and cost drivers.

text: What is 'critical chain project management' (CCPM) and how does it handle buffers?
options: It places buffers strategically at the end of chains to protect the project completion date
It assigns individual task buffers that each developer can use independently
It doubles all task estimates to create maximum safety margins for every activity
It eliminates all buffers to create the shortest possible project schedule
explanation: CCPM removes individual task safety margins and aggregates them into strategically placed project and feeding buffers to protect the overall project completion date.

text: How does 'parametric estimation' differ from 'analogous estimation'?
options: Parametric estimation is always less accurate than analogous estimation methods
Parametric uses statistical models and historical data relationships; analogous uses similar past project data
Analogous estimation uses mathematical formulas while parametric uses expert judgment
Both methods produce identical estimates regardless of the project characteristics
explanation: Parametric estimation uses statistical relationships between historical data and project parameters, while analogous estimation bases estimates on data from similar completed projects.

text: What is 'Estimate at Completion' (EAC) in earned value management and how is it calculated?
options: EAC is the original project budget and never changes during the project lifetime
EAC is the number of remaining tasks divided by the number of available developers
EAC is the forecasted total cost based on current performance, calculated as BAC divided by CPI
EAC is the total revenue expected from selling the completed software product
explanation: EAC forecasts the total project cost based on current performance; a common formula is BAC/CPI, where BAC is Budget at Completion and CPI is Cost Performance Index.

text: What is the 'Planning Poker' estimation technique and why is it effective?
options: A method where the most senior developer dictates all estimates for the team
A game where managers assign random estimates to tasks for entertainment
A technique that uses playing cards to randomly select which tasks to implement
A consensus-based technique where team members simultaneously reveal estimates to reduce anchoring bias
explanation: Planning Poker has team members independently select estimates and reveal simultaneously, preventing anchoring bias and encouraging discussion when estimates diverge.

text: What is 'resource leveling' and when is it necessary?
options: Adjusting the schedule to resolve resource overallocation while maintaining constraints
Assigning the same number of tasks to every developer regardless of complexity
Distributing office supplies equally among all project team members
Hiring all team members at the same seniority level for fairness
explanation: Resource leveling adjusts the project schedule to resolve situations where resources are overallocated, potentially extending the schedule to keep resource usage within limits.

text: How does the 'cost of quality' concept apply to software project management?
options: It proves that testing should be eliminated to reduce project budget and timeline
It shows that prevention and appraisal costs reduce more expensive failure costs over time
It demonstrates that quality is irrelevant to the financial success of a project
It suggests that investing in quality always increases overall project costs significantly
explanation: The cost of quality concept shows that investing in prevention and appraisal activities reduces the much higher costs of internal and external failures.

text: What is 'coupling' in software design?
options: The visual layout of the software user interface elements
The speed at which the software processes user input
The degree of interdependence between software modules
The number of lines of code within a single module
explanation: Coupling measures the degree of interdependence between software modules; low coupling is desirable for maintainable and flexible designs.

text: What is 'cohesion' in software design?
options: How many developers contributed code to the module over time
How quickly the module can be compiled by the build system
How many external libraries the module depends on for functionality
How tightly connected the elements within a single module are
explanation: Cohesion measures how closely related the responsibilities within a single module are; high cohesion is desirable as it indicates a focused, well-defined module.

text: What does the 'Single Responsibility Principle' state?
options: A class should have only one reason to change
Each project should use only one programming language throughout
A class should have multiple responsibilities to maximize code reuse
A single developer should be responsible for the entire project
explanation: The Single Responsibility Principle states that a class should have only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one job or responsibility.

text: What is an 'interface' in object-oriented design?
options: A type of database table used to store configuration settings
A contract that defines methods a class must implement without providing implementation
The visual elements displayed to the user on the screen
The physical connection between the computer and a peripheral device
explanation: An interface defines a contract of method signatures that implementing classes must provide, enabling polymorphism and loose coupling in design.

text: What is 'abstraction' in software design?
options: Making the source code as complex and detailed as possible
Copying code from one module to another to avoid dependencies
Hiding implementation details and showing only essential features
Writing code without any comments or documentation whatsoever
explanation: Abstraction involves hiding implementation details while exposing only the essential features, simplifying complex systems by focusing on relevant aspects.

text: What is 'encapsulation' in object-oriented design?
options: Placing all classes in a single file to reduce the number of files
Converting all private methods to public methods for easier testing
Making all data and methods publicly accessible to every class
Bundling data and methods together and restricting direct access to internal data
explanation: Encapsulation bundles data and the methods that operate on that data within a class, restricting direct access to internal state to maintain data integrity.

text: What is the 'Observer' design pattern?
options: A pattern where objects are notified automatically when a subject's state changes
A pattern where every method call is logged to a file for auditing
A pattern where a single class handles all user interface interactions
A pattern where one object monitors all network traffic in the system
explanation: The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency where when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified and updated automatically.

text: What is the 'Factory' design pattern used for?
options: Destroying objects when they are no longer needed in memory
Creating objects without specifying the exact class to be instantiated
Sorting a collection of objects based on a comparison criterion
Connecting to a remote database server for data retrieval operations
explanation: The Factory pattern provides an interface for creating objects without specifying their concrete classes, allowing flexibility in object creation.

text: What is 'inheritance' in object-oriented design?
options: A process for converting legacy code to a modern programming language
A method for distributing tasks across multiple servers in a cluster
A mechanism where a class acquires properties and behaviors from a parent class
A technique for encrypting data before storing it in a database
explanation: Inheritance allows a class to acquire the properties and behaviors of another class, promoting code reuse and establishing hierarchical relationships.

text: What is the 'Singleton' design pattern?
options: A pattern that separates a class into multiple smaller sub-classes
A pattern that allows unlimited instances of a class in memory
A pattern that creates a new object for every method call in the system
A pattern that ensures a class has only one instance with a global access point
explanation: The Singleton pattern restricts a class to a single instance and provides a global point of access to that instance.

text: What is the 'Open/Closed Principle' and why is it important?
options: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
Source code files should always remain open in the editor during development
Databases should be open for reading and closed for writing during business hours
Classes should be open for modification and closed for extension to prevent changes
explanation: The Open/Closed Principle states that software entities should be open for extension through mechanisms like inheritance but closed for modification of existing code.

text: What is the 'Liskov Substitution Principle'?
options: All variables should be substituted with constants to improve performance
Objects of a supertype should be replaceable with objects of a subtype without altering correctness
Developers should be substituted between teams every week to share knowledge
Any class can be substituted with any other class regardless of its type
explanation: The Liskov Substitution Principle states that objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of a subclass without affecting the correctness of the program.

text: What is the 'Strategy' design pattern used for?
options: Implementing a single fixed algorithm that cannot be changed after compilation
Planning the overall project timeline and resource allocation strategy
Defining a family of algorithms and making them interchangeable at runtime
Determining which team member should perform each development task
explanation: The Strategy pattern defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable, letting the algorithm vary independently from clients.

text: What is the 'Dependency Inversion Principle'?
options: All module dependencies should be removed to create completely independent components
Dependencies should always be inverted to create circular references between modules
Low-level modules should depend on high-level modules for their implementation
High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions
explanation: The Dependency Inversion Principle states that high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not on low-level modules, promoting flexible and maintainable designs.

text: What is the difference between 'composition' and 'inheritance' in object-oriented design?
options: Composition builds complex objects by combining simpler ones while inheritance extends existing classes
Composition and inheritance are identical concepts with different terminology
Composition uses 'is-a' relationships while inheritance uses 'has-a' relationships
Inheritance provides more flexibility than composition in all design scenarios
explanation: Composition creates complex objects by combining simpler objects (has-a), while inheritance extends existing classes (is-a); composition is often preferred for greater flexibility.

text: What is the 'Adapter' design pattern?
options: A pattern that creates new objects by cloning existing prototype instances
A pattern that converts the interface of a class into another interface clients expect
A pattern that adds new behavior to objects dynamically at runtime
A pattern that provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
explanation: The Adapter pattern converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect, enabling classes with incompatible interfaces to work together.

text: What is the 'Decorator' design pattern?
options: A pattern for applying visual themes and styles to user interface components
A pattern that converts objects from one type to another type at compile time
A pattern that adds responsibilities to objects dynamically without modifying their class
A pattern that removes unnecessary methods from a class to simplify its interface
explanation: The Decorator pattern attaches additional responsibilities to objects dynamically by wrapping them, providing a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality.

text: What is 'information hiding' and how does it relate to modular design?
options: Hiding project information from stakeholders to avoid scope changes
Encrypting all data in the database to prevent unauthorized access
Removing all comments from source code to keep implementation details secret
Concealing module implementation details behind well-defined interfaces to reduce complexity
explanation: Information hiding, proposed by David Parnas, conceals module internals behind interfaces, reducing complexity and enabling changes without affecting other modules.

text: What is the 'Interface Segregation Principle'?
options: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use
Interfaces should segregate developers into specialized teams by expertise
All interfaces should be combined into a single large interface for simplicity
Each interface must contain at least ten method signatures to be useful
explanation: The Interface Segregation Principle states that clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use, favoring many specific interfaces over one general-purpose one.

text: What is the 'Template Method' design pattern?
options: A pattern that generates code templates from requirements documents automatically
A pattern that defines the skeleton of an algorithm, deferring some steps to subclasses
A pattern that creates multiple copies of an object from a single template
A pattern that stores HTML templates for rendering web page user interfaces
explanation: The Template Method pattern defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, allowing subclasses to override specific steps without changing the overall structure.

text: What is the 'Law of Demeter' (Principle of Least Knowledge) in software design?
options: All classes must be documented with at least one hundred lines of comments
Software systems should be designed by the least experienced team members
A method should only communicate with its immediate friends, not with strangers
Every developer should have full knowledge of the entire codebase at all times
explanation: The Law of Demeter states that a method should only call methods on its own object, parameters, objects it creates, or direct component objects, reducing coupling.

text: What is 'design by contract' and what are its key elements?
options: A legal framework for outsourcing software development work to contractors
A method for automatically generating user interface designs from specifications
A process where clients sign contracts before receiving software deliverables
A design approach using preconditions, postconditions, and invariants to define component behavior
explanation: Design by contract, introduced by Bertrand Meyer, uses preconditions, postconditions, and class invariants to formally define the obligations and benefits of software components.

text: How does the 'GRASP' (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns) framework guide design decisions?
options: It provides patterns for assigning responsibilities to classes and objects in design
It establishes team management structures for large software development projects
It defines the hardware specifications required for running software applications
It provides programming language syntax rules for writing object-oriented code
explanation: GRASP provides nine patterns including Creator, Information Expert, and Controller that guide the assignment of responsibilities to classes and objects in object-oriented design.

text: What is the 'Visitor' design pattern and when should it be used?
options: A pattern that logs all user visits to the application for compliance auditing
A pattern that separates algorithms from object structures, enabling new operations without modifying classes
A pattern for tracking website visitors and their browsing behavior analytics
A pattern that limits the number of concurrent users accessing the system
explanation: The Visitor pattern separates algorithms from the objects they operate on, allowing new operations to be added to object structures without modifying the classes.

text: What is 'aspect-oriented design' and what problem does it solve?
options: It requires designing the system in exactly two distinct phases without overlap
It designs software from multiple visual angles to improve the user interface
It modularizes cross-cutting concerns like logging and security that span multiple modules
It focuses on designing only the most important aspect of the system first
explanation: Aspect-oriented design modularizes cross-cutting concerns such as logging, security, and transaction management that would otherwise be scattered across multiple modules.

text: How does the 'State' design pattern differ from using conditional statements?
options: The State pattern is slower and more complex than conditional statements in every case
Conditional statements are always preferable because they are easier to understand
The State pattern and conditional statements produce identical results and performance
The State pattern encapsulates state-specific behavior in separate classes, improving extensibility
explanation: The State pattern encapsulates state-specific behavior in separate classes, making it easier to add new states and transitions compared to growing conditional logic.

text: What is the 'Mediator' design pattern and how does it reduce complexity?
options: It centralizes complex communications between objects through a single mediator object
It removes all communication between objects to simplify the overall system design
It increases the number of direct connections between objects for faster communication
It assigns a human mediator to resolve conflicts between different development teams
explanation: The Mediator pattern reduces complexity by centralizing communication between objects through a mediator, replacing many-to-many interactions with one-to-many.

text: What is 'domain-driven design' (DDD) and what is its core concept?
options: A design approach where domain experts write all the source code for the project
A design approach that structures software around the business domain using a ubiquitous language
A design approach focused on optimizing database domain names for web applications
A design approach that eliminates all domain-specific terminology from the codebase
explanation: Domain-driven design structures software around the business domain, using a ubiquitous language shared by developers and domain experts to model complex business logic.

text: What are 'design patterns anti-patterns' and why should they be avoided?
options: They are patented patterns that cannot be used without paying licensing fees
They are patterns that only work in functional programming languages exclusively
They are commonly occurring poor solutions that create more problems than they solve
They are design patterns that are too simple and should be made more complex
explanation: Anti-patterns are commonly occurring design mistakes that appear to be solutions but actually create more problems, such as the God class, spaghetti code, or golden hammer.

text: What is the 'Command' design pattern and how does it enable undo functionality?
options: It provides a command-line interface for system administrators to manage servers
It automatically undoes all database transactions when the application crashes
It sends terminal commands to the operating system for file management tasks
It encapsulates requests as objects, storing enough state to reverse each operation
explanation: The Command pattern encapsulates requests as objects with execute and undo methods, enabling features like undo/redo, queuing, and logging of operations.

text: What is software architecture?
options: The high-level structure of a system defining its components and their relationships
The source code written for the user interface components
The physical layout of servers in a data center building
The programming language chosen for implementing the project
explanation: Software architecture defines the high-level structure of a software system, including its components, their properties, and the relationships between them.

text: What is a 'client-server' architecture?
options: An architecture that requires no network connection between any components
An architecture that separates the system into clients that request services and servers that provide them
An architecture where all processing happens on the client side only
An architecture where servers communicate with other servers without clients
explanation: Client-server architecture separates the system into client components that request services and server components that provide those services over a network.

text: What is the 'layered architecture' pattern?
options: An architecture where all code is placed in a single file without separation
An architecture that uses only one programming language across all components
An architecture organized into horizontal layers where each layer provides services to the layer above
An architecture where layers communicate in random order without any rules
explanation: Layered architecture organizes the system into layers such as presentation, business logic, and data access, where each layer provides services to the layer above it.

text: What is a 'monolithic' architecture?
options: An architecture designed exclusively for mobile application development
An architecture that uses only stone-age programming techniques and tools
An architecture where each feature is deployed as an independent service
An architecture where the application is built as a single unified deployable unit
explanation: A monolithic architecture builds the entire application as a single deployable unit where all components are tightly integrated and deployed together.

text: What is a 'pipe and filter' architecture?
options: An architecture where data flows through a series of processing components connected by pipes
An architecture where developers communicate through named pipes only
An architecture for managing water filtration systems in buildings
An architecture that filters out all network traffic to protect the system
explanation: The pipe and filter architecture processes data through a series of filter components connected by pipes, where each filter transforms its input data.

text: What is the MVC (Model-View-Controller) architectural pattern?
options: A pattern specifically designed for command-line applications without any views
A pattern that separates an application into model, view, and controller components
A pattern that eliminates the need for a database in the application
A pattern that combines all logic into a single component for simplicity
explanation: MVC separates an application into three components: the Model (data and logic), the View (user interface), and the Controller (handles input and updates).

text: What is a 'repository' architecture pattern?
options: A pattern that stores code repositories on multiple cloud platforms simultaneously
A pattern focused exclusively on version control system management
A pattern where all components share data through a central data store
A pattern where each component has its own private database that cannot be shared
explanation: The repository pattern centralizes data management where all components share data through a common data store, providing a consistent data access mechanism.

text: What is a 'distributed system' architecture?
options: A system where all processing occurs on a single machine in one location
A system that distributes printed documents to all team members daily
A system that only works when all components are on the same physical server
A system where components run on multiple networked computers that coordinate their actions
explanation: A distributed system architecture runs components on multiple networked computers that communicate and coordinate to appear as a single coherent system to users.

text: What is an 'event-driven' architecture?
options: An architecture where components communicate by producing and consuming events
An architecture designed for event planning and management software only
An architecture where all operations run on a fixed schedule without any triggers
An architecture that processes all requests synchronously in sequential order
explanation: Event-driven architecture uses events as the primary communication mechanism, where components produce events and other components react to them asynchronously.

text: What is a 'three-tier' architecture?
options: An architecture with three development teams working on separate projects
An architecture organized into presentation, business logic, and data access tiers
An architecture where the software has exactly three user interface screens
An architecture that requires exactly three servers to function properly
explanation: Three-tier architecture separates an application into presentation tier, business logic tier, and data access tier, allowing independent development and scaling.

text: What is a 'microservices' architecture and what problem does it solve?
options: An architecture where all services share a single database and deployment pipeline
An architecture using very small code files to reduce compilation time
An architecture that decomposes applications into small independent services that can be deployed separately
An architecture designed exclusively for microcontroller programming and embedded devices
explanation: Microservices architecture decomposes applications into small, independently deployable services, solving monolithic scalability, deployment, and maintenance challenges.

text: What is an 'architectural decision record' (ADR)?
options: A database record that stores the application configuration settings
A performance benchmark recording how fast the architecture processes requests
A video recording of the architecture review meeting for future reference
A document that captures important architectural decisions with context and consequences
explanation: An ADR captures significant architectural decisions, including the context, decision rationale, and consequences, serving as documentation for future reference.

text: What is a 'service-oriented architecture' (SOA)?
options: An architecture where functionality is provided as reusable services with well-defined interfaces
An architecture that requires all components to be serviced monthly by hardware technicians
An architecture where each developer provides services to exactly one other developer
An architecture focused on customer service and help desk applications only
explanation: SOA provides application functionality as reusable, loosely coupled services with well-defined interfaces that can be composed to create business processes.

text: What are 'quality attributes' in software architecture and why do they matter?
options: They are the quality ratings given by end users in app store reviews
They are non-functional properties like scalability and performance that architecture must support
They are the visual quality settings of the user interface display resolution
They are the coding standards that developers must follow in their source code
explanation: Quality attributes are non-functional properties such as performance, scalability, security, and maintainability that the architecture must support to meet stakeholder needs.

text: What is the 'publish-subscribe' pattern in software architecture?
options: A pattern for publishing software documentation to a website for subscribers
A pattern where software licenses are published and subscribers must pay monthly
A pattern where publishers send messages to topics and subscribers receive messages of interest
A pattern that requires all developers to subscribe to the same code repository
explanation: The publish-subscribe pattern decouples message producers from consumers, where publishers send messages to topics and subscribers receive only messages they are interested in.

text: How does a 'message broker' contribute to system architecture?
options: It manages the financial transactions and billing for cloud hosting services
It translates source code between different programming languages automatically
It provides a visual dashboard for monitoring developer productivity metrics
It intermediates between services, handling message routing, transformation, and delivery guarantees
explanation: A message broker acts as an intermediary that routes messages between services, providing features like message transformation, delivery guarantees, and load balancing.

text: What is 'architectural erosion' and how can it be prevented?
options: The gradual deviation of implementation from the intended architecture, prevented through governance
The natural improvement in architecture as developers gain more experience
The process of removing unnecessary architectural components to improve performance
Physical wear on server hardware over time requiring replacement parts
explanation: Architectural erosion occurs when implementation gradually deviates from the intended architecture; it can be prevented through governance, reviews, and automated conformance checks.

text: What is a 'gateway' pattern in microservices architecture?
options: A physical device that controls access to the server room in a building
A single entry point that routes client requests to appropriate microservices
A pattern for migrating all data from one database to another simultaneously
A gateway drug that leads developers to adopt increasingly complex architectures
explanation: An API gateway provides a single entry point for clients, routing requests to appropriate microservices while handling cross-cutting concerns like authentication and rate limiting.

text: What is the 'strangler fig' pattern for migrating from monolith to microservices?
options: A pattern that immediately replaces the entire monolith with microservices overnight
A pattern that strengthens the monolith by adding more features and services to it
A pattern that gradually replaces monolith functionality with microservices while both coexist
A pattern that removes all legacy code without building any replacement services
explanation: The strangler fig pattern incrementally replaces monolith functionality with microservices, allowing old and new systems to coexist during the gradual migration.

text: What is the 'CQRS' (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern?
options: A pattern that prevents any query from modifying data in the database
A pattern that uses the same model for all read and write operations
A pattern that requires all queries to be written as stored procedures
A pattern that separates the read and write models of a system for independent optimization
explanation: CQRS separates read (query) and write (command) operations into different models, allowing each to be independently optimized, scaled, and secured.

text: What is the 'CAP theorem' and how does it constrain distributed system architecture?
options: It states that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
It states that the capacity of a system is always proportional to the number of servers
It states that all communications in a distributed system must be encrypted for security
It states that distributed systems can achieve consistency, availability, and partition tolerance simultaneously
explanation: The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can provide at most two of three guarantees: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance, forcing architectural trade-offs.

text: What is the 'saga pattern' for managing distributed transactions?
options: A long-running story about the history of software development
A pattern that coordinates distributed transactions through a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions
A pattern that locks all services until a single global transaction completes
A pattern that eliminates the need for any transaction management in distributed systems
explanation: The saga pattern manages distributed transactions by breaking them into a sequence of local transactions, with compensating actions to undo changes if any step fails.

text: What is 'event sourcing' and how does it differ from traditional state storage?
options: It stores only the current state of the system in a relational database
It sources events from external third-party systems and discards internal events
It stores all state changes as a sequence of events, allowing state reconstruction from the event log
It eliminates all events from the system to simplify data management
explanation: Event sourcing stores every state change as an immutable event, enabling complete audit trails, temporal queries, and state reconstruction by replaying events.

text: What is 'architectural fitness function' in evolutionary architecture?
options: A genetic algorithm function used to automatically generate source code
A mathematical function used to calculate the cost of server infrastructure
A function that measures the physical fitness of the development team
An objective measure that assesses how well an architecture meets a specific quality attribute
explanation: Architectural fitness functions are objective, automated measures that assess how well the architecture meets specific quality attributes, enabling continuous architectural governance.

text: How does the 'hexagonal architecture' (ports and adapters) promote testability?
options: By isolating the core business logic from external concerns through ports and adapters
By requiring all tests to be written in a hexagonal programming paradigm
By placing all code in a single hexagonal class for easy unit testing
By eliminating the need for any test infrastructure or test automation tools
explanation: Hexagonal architecture isolates core business logic from external dependencies through ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations), enabling easy substitution for testing.

text: What is the 'circuit breaker' pattern in distributed systems?
options: A pattern that limits the total electrical power consumption of the data center
A pattern that prevents cascading failures by detecting faults and stopping requests to failing services
A physical circuit breaker installed in the server room for electrical safety
A pattern that breaks all network circuits when a security breach is detected
explanation: The circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures by monitoring calls to a service and stopping requests when failures exceed a threshold, allowing the service to recover.

text: What is the 'ATAM' (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method)?
options: A method for automatically generating architecture diagrams from code
A tool for automatically testing all API endpoints in a microservices system
A structured method for evaluating software architectures against quality attribute requirements
A method for calculating the total cost of architecture migration projects
explanation: ATAM is a structured method for evaluating software architectures, identifying sensitivity points, tradeoffs, and risks related to quality attribute requirements.

text: What are 'bounded contexts' in domain-driven design and how do they affect architecture?
options: They restrict the number of developers who can access each service codebase
They set maximum response time bounds for each architectural component
They limit the physical size of each microservice to a specific number of files
They define explicit boundaries within which a domain model is consistent and applicable
explanation: Bounded contexts define explicit boundaries within which a particular domain model applies consistently, guiding service decomposition and team organization in architectures.

text: What is 'Conway's Law' and how does it influence software architecture?
options: It states that organizations design systems that mirror their own communication structures
It states that software should always be designed using the Conway programming methodology
It states that the complexity of software doubles with every new team member added
It states that all architectural decisions must be approved by a committee vote
explanation: Conway's Law states that organizations tend to produce system designs that mirror their communication structures, meaning team organization directly influences architecture.

text: What is the 'sidecar pattern' in microservices architecture?
options: A pattern where all logging is disabled to improve the performance of the main service
A pattern where a secondary service runs alongside the main service in the same deployment unit
A pattern where backup services are kept offline until the primary service fails
A pattern where non-essential features are removed from the main service permanently
explanation: The sidecar pattern deploys a helper service alongside the main service in the same host, providing supplementary features like logging, monitoring, and networking.

text: What is 'usability' in user interface design?
options: The visual attractiveness of the color scheme and typography used
The speed at which the application loads on a web browser
The ease with which users can learn and use the software effectively
The total number of features available in the software application
explanation: Usability measures how easily and effectively users can learn, use, and interact with a software system to achieve their goals.

text: What is a 'wireframe' in UI design?
options: A type of cable used to connect multiple monitors to a workstation
A security framework that protects the network from unauthorized access
A physical wire model of the computer hardware components
A low-fidelity visual guide showing the layout and structure of a user interface
explanation: A wireframe is a simplified visual representation of a user interface layout, showing the structure and placement of elements without detailed design.

text: What is 'consistency' in user interface design?
options: Using similar patterns, layouts, and behaviors throughout the entire application
Changing the navigation structure on each page to challenge the user
Making every screen look completely different to maintain user interest
Using a different font style for every paragraph of text in the interface
explanation: Consistency means using uniform patterns, visual elements, and behaviors throughout the application, making it predictable and easier for users to learn.

text: What is 'user feedback' in interface design?
options: The financial feedback from investors about the product's market performance
Visual or auditory responses that inform users about the result of their actions
The code review comments that developers provide on pull request submissions
The complaints users submit about the software to the support team
explanation: User feedback refers to the system's visual, auditory, or tactile responses that inform users about the status and result of their interactions.

text: What is 'accessibility' in the context of UI design?
options: The speed at which the application downloads from the internet
The number of programming languages the development team can access
Designing the interface so it can be used by people with disabilities
The ability to access the software from any geographic location worldwide
explanation: Accessibility in UI design means ensuring the interface can be effectively used by people with various disabilities, including visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive impairments.

text: What is a 'mockup' in user interface design?
options: A satirical version of the software created for entertainment purposes
A duplicate copy of the production database for development testing
A broken version of the software with intentionally introduced bugs
A high-fidelity visual representation of how the final interface will look
explanation: A mockup is a realistic, high-fidelity visual representation of the user interface that shows colors, typography, and imagery, but is typically not interactive.

text: What does the term 'navigation' refer to in UI design?
options: The way users move between different screens and sections of the application
The network routing configuration for data packets between servers
The GPS system used by the delivery team to transport hardware
The process of navigating the source code to find and fix bugs
explanation: Navigation in UI design refers to the methods and structures that allow users to move between different screens, sections, and features of the application.

text: What is 'responsive design' in web user interfaces?
options: A design that uses fast-responding servers to improve loading speed
A design that adapts its layout to different screen sizes and devices
A design that responds to user complaints quickly and efficiently
A design that requires users to respond to a survey before using it
explanation: Responsive design creates web interfaces that automatically adapt their layout, content, and navigation to provide optimal viewing across different screen sizes and devices.

text: What is the purpose of a 'call to action' (CTA) in UI design?
options: To call the development team into a meeting when a bug is discovered
To make emergency phone calls when the system encounters errors
To prompt users to take a specific desired action like signing up or purchasing
To activate the system's voice recognition feature for hands-free operation
explanation: A call to action is a UI element, typically a button or link, designed to prompt users to take a specific desired action such as signing up, purchasing, or downloading.

text: What is a 'breadcrumb' in user interface navigation?
options: A type of cookie used to track user behavior across websites
A small error message displayed at the bottom of the screen
A technique for spreading application data across multiple databases
A visual trail showing the user's location within the application hierarchy
explanation: Breadcrumbs are a navigation aid that shows users their current location within the application's hierarchy, allowing easy navigation back to higher levels.

text: What are Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics primarily used for?
options: Evaluating user interface designs against established usability principles
Calculating the exact time users need to complete each task in the system
Determining the optimal number of colors to use in the interface design
Writing source code that is free of all syntax errors and bugs
explanation: Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics are broad rules of thumb used to evaluate user interface designs for common usability problems through heuristic evaluation.

text: What is 'Fitts' Law' and how does it apply to UI design?
options: A law about the physical fitness requirements for software developers
A predictive model stating that time to reach a target depends on distance and target size
A regulation requiring all UI elements to be the same size and shape
A law mandating that all interfaces must fit within a standard screen resolution
explanation: Fitts' Law predicts that the time to reach a target is a function of the distance to the target and its size, guiding the placement and sizing of interactive elements.

text: What is 'progressive disclosure' in user interface design?
options: Progressively slowing down the interface to encourage users to upgrade hardware
Disclosing the project source code progressively to the open-source community
Showing only essential information initially and revealing more details as needed
Gradually increasing the application price as more features are added
explanation: Progressive disclosure reduces complexity by initially showing only the most important information and controls, revealing additional details when users need them.

text: What is 'affordance' in the context of user interface design?
options: The cost of the software that users can afford to purchase
The amount of disk space the application affords on the user's device
The financial budget afforded to the UI design team for their work
A design property that suggests how an element can be used through its visual cues
explanation: Affordance is a design property where an element's visual appearance suggests how it can be used, such as a raised button suggesting it can be clicked.

text: What is 'A/B testing' in user interface design?
options: Comparing two versions of a design element to determine which performs better
Testing the application on both Android and iOS mobile platforms
Testing alphabetically ordered features from A to B in the interface
Having developer A test developer B's code through peer review processes
explanation: A/B testing compares two versions of a design element by showing each to different user groups and measuring which version achieves better results.

text: What is the 'Gestalt principle of proximity' in visual design?
options: Proximity sensors should be used to detect users near the computer screen
Elements placed close together are perceived as belonging to the same group
Elements should be placed as far apart as possible to maximize screen usage
All interface elements must be the same distance from the screen center
explanation: The Gestalt principle of proximity states that objects placed close together are perceived as related or belonging to the same group, guiding layout decisions.

text: What is 'cognitive load' and why is it important in UI design?
options: The number of CPU cores needed to run the application at full performance
The total storage space occupied by the application on the user's device
The amount of mental effort required to use an interface, which should be minimized
The physical weight of the hardware running the software application
explanation: Cognitive load is the mental effort required to process information and interact with the interface; good UI design minimizes cognitive load for better user experience.

text: What is 'user persona' and how is it used in UI design?
options: The personality traits that UI designers must possess to be successful
The personal information of actual users stored in the application database
A user authentication mechanism that verifies personal identity documents
A fictional representation of a target user type used to guide design decisions
explanation: A user persona is a fictional, detailed representation of a target user type, created from research data to guide design decisions and ensure the interface meets user needs.

text: What is 'error prevention' as a usability principle?
options: Designing the interface to prevent errors from occurring in the first place
Displaying error messages in the smallest font possible to avoid alarming users
Preventing users from reporting any errors they encounter in the system
Hiding all error messages from users to maintain a positive user experience
explanation: Error prevention designs the interface to minimize the chance of errors occurring, using techniques like confirmation dialogs, input validation, and disabling invalid actions.

text: What is the purpose of 'usability testing' with real users?
options: To test whether the server hardware can handle the expected number of users
To observe real users interacting with the interface to identify usability problems
To verify that the application passes all automated unit tests successfully
To test whether the development team can use their programming tools effectively
explanation: Usability testing involves observing real users as they complete tasks with the interface, identifying usability problems and areas for improvement.

text: What is 'Hick's Law' and how does it influence menu design?
options: A law stating that users prefer menus with the most options possible available
A law requiring that all menus have exactly seven items for optimal usability
A law stating that decision time increases logarithmically with the number of choices
A law stating that menu items should always be sorted alphabetically
explanation: Hick's Law states that the time to make a decision increases logarithmically with the number of choices, guiding designers to minimize options and categorize menu items.

text: What is 'dark pattern' in user interface design?
options: A testing pattern that simulates system failures to test error handling
A design pattern used for creating interfaces for dark mode applications
A color scheme that uses dark backgrounds to reduce eye strain at night
A deceptive design technique that tricks users into unintended actions
explanation: Dark patterns are deceptive UI design techniques that manipulate users into performing unintended actions, such as hidden subscriptions or confusing opt-out processes.

text: How does 'Miller's Law' (7 plus or minus 2) apply to information architecture?
options: It suggests that working memory can hold about seven items, guiding grouping and chunking strategies
It states that development teams should have seven plus or minus two members
It requires that all databases have between five and nine tables total
It mandates that all web pages must have exactly seven navigation links
explanation: Miller's Law suggests that the average person can hold about 7 (plus or minus 2) items in working memory, influencing how information is grouped and presented in interfaces.

text: What is 'WCAG' and what are its conformance levels?
options: Web Configuration Administration Gateway with levels Low, Medium, and High
Web Content Accessibility Guidelines with levels A, AA, and AAA
Web Component Architecture Guide with levels Basic, Standard, and Premium
Web Code Analysis Guidelines with levels Alpha, Beta, and Release
explanation: WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) provides standards for web accessibility with three conformance levels: A (minimum), AA (mid-range), and AAA (highest).

text: What is the 'Norman Door' concept and what does it teach about design?
options: A specific type of secure authentication mechanism for door access systems
A door design pattern used in physical building architecture for fire safety
A design that gives wrong signals about how to use it, illustrating the importance of affordances
A programming technique for handling doorway transitions between application screens
explanation: A Norman Door, named after Don Norman, is a design that gives wrong signals about its operation, illustrating how poor affordances lead to user confusion and frustration.

text: What is the 'Gulf of Execution' and 'Gulf of Evaluation' in interaction design?
options: Two physical locations where software companies have headquarters
Two types of runtime errors that occur during JavaScript code execution
Two competing design frameworks that cannot be used in the same project
The gaps between user intention and system actions, and between system state and user understanding
explanation: The Gulf of Execution is the gap between user intentions and available actions; the Gulf of Evaluation is the gap between system state and user understanding of that state.

text: What is 'design thinking' and how does it apply to UI/UX design?
options: A human-centered iterative approach involving empathize, define, ideate, prototype, and test phases
A technique for generating design documentation without any user research
A method for calculating the exact cost of interface design and development
A linear process focused exclusively on visual aesthetics and color theory
explanation: Design thinking is a human-centered approach with five phases: empathize, define, ideate, prototype, and test, iteratively developing solutions based on deep user understanding.

text: What is the 'Von Restorff effect' and how can it be used in UI design?
options: An effect where users always click the first item in any list or menu
An effect where distinctive items among similar items are more likely to be remembered
An effect where removing all visual distinctions improves user comprehension
An effect where users prefer interfaces with the most uniform visual elements
explanation: The Von Restorff effect states that items that stand out visually from similar items are more likely to be remembered, guiding the design of emphasis for important elements.

text: How does the 'F-pattern' influence web page layout design?
options: It requires all web pages to be shaped like the letter F for branding purposes
It requires all web pages to have exactly six columns for optimal readability
It reflects how users scan web content in an F-shaped pattern, guiding content placement
It mandates using only fonts that start with the letter F in web design
explanation: Eye-tracking research shows users scan web pages in an F-shaped pattern, reading across the top, then moving down and reading less, guiding where to place important content.

text: What is 'emotional design' as described by Don Norman?
options: A design approach focused exclusively on making users feel happy at all times
A design approach that intentionally frustrates users to create memorable experiences
A design approach that removes all emotional elements to create neutral interfaces
A design approach considering visceral, behavioral, and reflective levels of user experience
explanation: Don Norman's emotional design framework addresses three levels: visceral (appearance), behavioral (usability), and reflective (self-image and satisfaction) to create compelling experiences.

text: What is 'code refactoring'?
options: Restructuring existing code without changing its external behavior
Converting code from one programming language to another one
Deleting all source code and rewriting from scratch completely
Adding new features to the software without any testing
explanation: Code refactoring is the process of restructuring existing code to improve its internal structure and readability without changing its observable behavior.

text: What is a 'code review'?
options: A tool that automatically compiles and runs the source code
A process where developers examine source code to find defects and improve quality
A document listing all the features implemented in the latest release
A meeting to review the project budget and financial forecasts
explanation: Code review is a systematic examination of source code by peers to identify defects, improve quality, and share knowledge among team members.

text: What is the purpose of coding standards?
options: To limit the number of programming languages used worldwide
To ensure all developers use the same brand of computer hardware
To provide consistent formatting and naming conventions for readable code
To determine the salary scale for developers based on seniority level
explanation: Coding standards establish consistent formatting, naming conventions, and best practices that improve code readability and maintainability across a team.

text: What is 'version control' in software development?
options: A technique for limiting the number of software releases per year
A process for deciding which software version customers must buy
A method for controlling the version of hardware in the office
A system for managing changes to source code over time
explanation: Version control is a system that tracks and manages changes to source code over time, enabling collaboration and maintaining a history of modifications.

text: What is 'debugging' in software development?
options: The process of finding and fixing defects in source code
The process of writing documentation for the end users
The process of removing insects from the server room
The process of adding new features to an existing application
explanation: Debugging is the process of identifying, analyzing, and removing errors or defects in software source code to ensure correct behavior.

text: What is a 'build system' in software development?
options: A system for building relationships between team members socially
A tool that automates compiling source code and creating deployable artifacts
A system for constructing office buildings for the development team
A physical system of shelves for storing printed documentation
explanation: A build system automates the process of compiling source code, running tests, and creating deployable artifacts from the project's source files.

text: What is 'pair programming'?
options: Two teams competing to implement the same feature for comparison
A developer working with two monitors simultaneously for productivity
Two developers working together at one workstation on the same code
Two developers working on completely separate tasks independently
explanation: Pair programming is a practice where two developers work together at one workstation, with one writing code (driver) and the other reviewing (navigator).

text: What is 'technical documentation' for source code?
options: Marketing brochures that promote the software product to customers
The physical hardware specifications for the development computers
Legal contracts between the company and its software vendors
Written descriptions of code structure, APIs, and design decisions
explanation: Technical documentation describes the code structure, APIs, design decisions, and usage instructions, helping developers understand and maintain the software.

text: What is a 'code smell'?
options: A surface indication of a deeper problem in the source code design
A pleasant notification that the code passes all automated tests
A syntax error that prevents the code from compiling successfully
A physical odor detected in the server room from overheating hardware
explanation: A code smell is a surface-level indicator in the source code that suggests a deeper design problem, such as duplicated code, long methods, or large classes.

text: What does 'DRY' stand for in software development?
options: Design, Review, Yank
Don't Repeat Yourself
Deploy, Run, Yield
Debug, Refactor, Yell
explanation: DRY stands for Don't Repeat Yourself, a principle that states every piece of knowledge should have a single, unambiguous representation in the system.

text: What is 'continuous deployment' and how does it differ from continuous delivery?
options: Continuous deployment and continuous delivery are identical practices with no differences
Continuous delivery releases to production automatically while continuous deployment stops at staging
Continuous deployment automatically releases every change to production while continuous delivery requires manual approval
Continuous deployment only applies to database changes while continuous delivery applies to code
explanation: Continuous deployment automatically releases every passing change to production, while continuous delivery ensures code is always ready for release but requires a manual decision to deploy.

text: What is 'static code analysis' and what problems does it detect?
options: Analysis performed while the code is running in production to detect runtime errors
Analysis of code that never changes to determine if it can be safely deleted
Analysis of the static electricity levels in the server room for safety purposes
Analysis of source code without executing it to detect potential bugs and style violations
explanation: Static code analysis examines source code without executing it, identifying potential bugs, security vulnerabilities, code style violations, and complexity issues.

text: What is 'dependency injection' and why is it useful?
options: Providing dependencies to a component from outside rather than creating them internally
Injecting malicious code into a dependency to test security vulnerabilities
Adding new database dependencies to the project configuration file automatically
Injecting additional requirements into the project scope during development
explanation: Dependency injection provides a component's dependencies from outside rather than having it create them internally, improving testability and loose coupling.

text: What is the 'boy scout rule' in software development?
options: Code should be organized into scout groups based on file type
Always leave the code cleaner than you found it when making changes
New developers must complete a camping trip before joining the team
Developers should wear uniforms while writing code in the office
explanation: The boy scout rule states that developers should always leave code in a better state than they found it, making small improvements whenever they touch existing code.

text: What is 'feature toggling' (feature flags) used for?
options: Switching between different database engines based on query performance
Toggling between different programming languages during development
Enabling or disabling features at runtime without deploying new code
Physically toggling power switches on servers to restart applications
explanation: Feature toggles allow teams to enable or disable features at runtime through configuration, supporting techniques like canary releases and A/B testing without redeployment.

text: What is 'trunk-based development' as a branching strategy?
options: Developing code exclusively during the trunk season between summer and winter
Storing all code in a tree trunk-shaped directory structure on the file system
Developing all code in long-lived feature branches that are merged once a year
Developers committing small, frequent changes directly to the main branch
explanation: Trunk-based development has developers make small, frequent commits to a shared main branch, reducing merge conflicts and enabling continuous integration.

text: What is 'code coverage' and what does it measure?
options: The percentage of source code executed by automated tests
The insurance coverage purchased for the software intellectual property
The amount of comments and documentation covering the source code
The number of developers who have read and understood the codebase
explanation: Code coverage measures the percentage of source code lines, branches, or paths executed during automated testing, indicating how thoroughly the code is tested.

text: What is the 'YAGNI' principle in software development?
options: You Always Generate New Interfaces for every class in the system
You Aren't Gonna Need It - don't implement features until they are needed
You Absolutely Generate Numerous Iterations before releasing software
You Always Get New Issues when refactoring legacy code systems
explanation: YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) states that developers should not implement functionality until it is actually needed, avoiding unnecessary complexity.

text: What is 'containerization' and how does it benefit software deployment?
options: Organizing source code files into container-shaped directory structures
Placing servers inside physical shipping containers for transport security
Packaging applications with their dependencies into isolated, portable containers
Limiting the memory available to each application to prevent resource waste
explanation: Containerization packages applications with their dependencies into isolated containers, ensuring consistent behavior across development, testing, and production environments.

text: What is 'infrastructure as code' (IaC)?
options: Building physical data center infrastructure using construction blueprints
Writing application source code on physical infrastructure diagrams
Converting infrastructure documentation from PDF format to source code
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable definition files
explanation: Infrastructure as code manages computing infrastructure through machine-readable definition files rather than manual configuration, enabling version control and repeatability.

text: What is 'semantic versioning' (SemVer) and what do the three numbers represent?
options: Major.Minor.Patch where major breaks compatibility, minor adds features, patch fixes bugs
Year.Month.Day representing when each version was released to the public
Primary.Secondary.Tertiary representing the importance level of each release
Team.Developer.Build representing which team member created the latest build
explanation: Semantic versioning uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH where major indicates breaking changes, minor adds backward-compatible features, and patch makes backward-compatible fixes.

text: What is 'cyclomatic complexity' and why does it matter for implementation?
options: The number of lines of code in a program file measuring its physical size
A metric measuring the number of independent paths through code, indicating testing difficulty
The number of programming languages a developer must learn to join the team
The complexity of bicycle routes to the office affecting developer commute times
explanation: Cyclomatic complexity measures the number of linearly independent paths through code, indicating its structural complexity and the minimum number of test cases needed.

text: What is the 'strangler fig' refactoring pattern for legacy systems?
options: A pattern that documents legacy code without making any actual changes to it
A pattern that deletes all legacy code at once and replaces it with new code
A pattern that incrementally replaces legacy system components while keeping the system running
A pattern that wraps legacy code in multiple layers of abstraction indefinitely
explanation: The strangler fig pattern incrementally replaces legacy system components with new implementations, allowing the system to continue operating during the migration.

text: What are 'microcommits' and what benefit do they provide in version control?
options: Commits made by microservice applications to their individual databases
Commits made exclusively to microcontroller firmware and embedded system code
Commits that are so small they are automatically ignored by the version control system
Very small, focused commits that make changes easy to review, revert, and understand
explanation: Microcommits are very small, focused commits that capture a single logical change, making code reviews easier, reverts safer, and the change history more understandable.

text: What is 'dead code elimination' and why is it important?
options: Removing code that can never be executed, reducing complexity and maintenance burden
Deleting all code comments to make the source files smaller in size
Killing processes that are consuming too much CPU on the production server
Terminating developer accounts that have been inactive for over one year
explanation: Dead code elimination removes code that can never be executed or is never called, reducing codebase complexity, maintenance burden, and potential confusion.

text: What is 'technical debt quadrant' as described by Martin Fowler?
options: A four-person team structure for managing technical infrastructure resources
A classification of technical debt along reckless/prudent and deliberate/inadvertent dimensions
A financial model for calculating the cost of technical equipment purchases
A quarterly report on the number of technical issues found in the project
explanation: Fowler's technical debt quadrant classifies debt along two dimensions: reckless vs. prudent (decision quality) and deliberate vs. inadvertent (awareness), creating four types.

text: What is 'mutation testing' and how does it assess test quality?
options: Randomly changing test data to see if the application produces different outputs
Testing software on mutated or corrupted hardware to check fault tolerance
Introducing small changes (mutants) into code to check if tests detect them
Testing genetically modified organisms using specialized laboratory software
explanation: Mutation testing introduces small deliberate changes (mutants) into the source code and checks whether existing tests detect these changes, assessing test suite effectiveness.

text: What is the 'broken windows theory' applied to software development?
options: A theory about the physical security of office windows in development buildings
A theory that Windows operating system applications are inherently more prone to bugs
