A theory that broken code should be displayed publicly to shame the developer
A theory that tolerating small code quality issues leads to progressively worse code
explanation: The broken windows theory in software states that tolerating small quality problems like bad naming or dead code signals that quality doesn't matter, leading to worse code over time.

text: What is 'GitFlow' branching model and what are its main branches?
options: A branching model using main, develop, feature, release, and hotfix branches
A model where all code flows directly to the main branch without branching
A water flow simulation tool with input and output data streams
A flow chart tool for designing the graphical user interface layout
explanation: GitFlow uses main (production), develop (integration), feature (new work), release (preparation), and hotfix (urgent fixes) branches to organize parallel development.

text: What is 'polyglot programming' and what are its trade-offs?
options: Programming in polygonal shapes to improve the visual appearance of code diagrams
Using multiple programming languages in a project, gaining best-fit tools but increasing complexity
Translating all source code comments into multiple spoken languages for global teams
Writing all code in a single language to maximize team expertise and consistency
explanation: Polyglot programming uses multiple languages in a project to leverage each language's strengths, but increases complexity in tooling, deployment, and required team skills.

text: What is 'unit testing'?
options: Testing the system's performance under heavy user load conditions
Testing the entire system as a whole to verify all requirements are met
Testing individual components or functions in isolation from the rest of the system
Testing the user interface layout on different screen resolutions
explanation: Unit testing verifies individual components or functions in isolation, ensuring each unit works correctly before integrating with other components.

text: What is 'integration testing'?
options: Testing the installation process on a freshly formatted computer
Testing whether the software integrates well with marketing campaigns
Testing each function individually without connecting to other modules
Testing the interactions between integrated components or subsystems
explanation: Integration testing verifies the interactions between integrated components or subsystems, ensuring they work correctly together.

text: What is 'black-box testing'?
options: Testing based on input and output without knowledge of internal code structure
Testing performed in a dark room to reduce visual distractions
Testing that examines every line of source code in detail
Testing performed exclusively on black-colored hardware devices
explanation: Black-box testing evaluates software based on inputs and expected outputs without knowledge of the internal code structure or implementation details.

text: What is 'white-box testing'?
options: Testing where only the user interface appearance is evaluated
Testing that examines the internal code structure and logic paths
Testing performed on white-labeled software products without branding
Testing performed exclusively by external quality assurance contractors
explanation: White-box testing examines the internal structure, logic paths, and code of the software, requiring knowledge of the implementation to design test cases.

text: What is 'regression testing'?
options: Testing performed to check if the software regresses to a previous version
Testing performed only during the regression phase of the development cycle
Re-running tests to ensure changes have not broken existing functionality
Testing the software by going backwards through the user interface pages
explanation: Regression testing re-runs existing test cases after code changes to verify that modifications have not introduced new defects into previously working functionality.

text: What is 'system testing'?
options: Testing the operating system compatibility before installing the application
Testing only the system administrator functions and configuration settings
Testing the hardware system on which the software will be deployed
Testing the complete integrated system to verify it meets specified requirements
explanation: System testing evaluates the complete, integrated software system to verify that it meets the specified functional and non-functional requirements.

text: What is 'acceptance testing'?
options: Testing performed by users or clients to determine if the system meets their needs
Testing the acceptance rate of marketing emails sent to potential customers
Testing performed by the development team to accept new code commits
Testing that determines whether job applicants are accepted to the team
explanation: Acceptance testing is performed by end users or clients to determine whether the software system meets their requirements and is acceptable for delivery.

text: What is a 'test case' in software testing?
options: A protective case for the computer used during performance testing
A set of conditions and expected results used to verify a specific function
A physical case used to store testing equipment and documentation
A legal case filed against developers who introduce bugs into the code
explanation: A test case is a documented set of conditions, inputs, and expected results designed to verify a specific function or feature of the software.

text: What is 'smoke testing'?
options: A test that generates smoke signals to notify the team of test completion
Testing if the hardware produces smoke when powered on for the first time
A preliminary test to check if the most critical functions work before deeper testing
Testing the software in a smoke-filled room to simulate adverse conditions
explanation: Smoke testing is a preliminary, shallow test that verifies the most critical functions of the software work, determining if it is stable enough for further testing.

text: What is a 'test plan' in software testing?
options: A floor plan showing where testers will sit in the office building
A plan for hiring new testers for the quality assurance department
A plan for purchasing new testing hardware and equipment for the lab
A document describing the scope, approach, resources, and schedule for testing
explanation: A test plan is a document that describes the scope, objectives, approach, resources, schedule, and activities for software testing.

text: What is 'equivalence partitioning' as a test design technique?
options: Dividing input data into equivalent classes where one test per class is sufficient
Dividing the development team into equal-sized groups for testing assignments
Splitting test execution equally across multiple testing servers
Partitioning the hard drive into equal segments for test data storage
explanation: Equivalence partitioning divides input data into classes of equivalent behavior, where testing one value from each class is sufficient to represent the entire partition.

text: What is 'boundary value analysis' and why is it effective?
options: Evaluating the budget boundaries to determine maximum testing investment
Testing values at the edges of equivalence partitions where errors commonly occur
Analyzing the physical boundaries of the data center to optimize server placement
Analyzing the organizational boundaries between development and testing teams
explanation: Boundary value analysis tests values at the edges of equivalence partitions because experience shows that errors frequently occur at boundary conditions.

text: What is 'performance testing' and what does it measure?
options: Testing how fast the development team can write code in a coding competition
Testing the performance of individual developers through code quality metrics
Testing how the system behaves under various load conditions measuring response time and throughput
Testing the performance of the project manager in meeting deadlines
explanation: Performance testing evaluates system behavior under various load conditions, measuring response times, throughput, resource utilization, and stability.

text: What is 'exploratory testing' and when is it most useful?
options: Testing performed exclusively in unexplored geographic locations for objectivity
Testing performed by exploring the source code line by line systematically
A fully scripted testing approach with no room for deviation from test cases
Simultaneous test design and execution guided by the tester's knowledge and intuition
explanation: Exploratory testing combines test design and execution simultaneously, guided by the tester's expertise and intuition, useful for finding issues that scripted tests miss.

text: What is a 'test stub' and how is it used in testing?
options: A simplified implementation that replaces a component being depended upon during testing
A leftover piece of code that was not fully implemented before deadline
A ticket stub given to testers for attending the testing kick-off meeting
A small piece of paper used to record manual test results
explanation: A test stub is a simplified replacement for a component that the unit under test depends on, providing predefined responses to allow testing in isolation.

text: What is the difference between 'verification' and 'validation' in testing?
options: Verification applies only to code; validation applies only to documentation
Verification checks the product is built correctly; validation checks the right product is built
Verification is performed by users; validation is performed by developers only
Verification and validation are identical processes with the same objectives
explanation: Verification confirms the product is built correctly per specifications, while validation confirms the right product is built to meet actual user needs.

text: What is 'stress testing' in software testing?
options: Testing the structural stress on server rack hardware during earthquakes
Testing the software during stressful weather conditions like thunderstorms
Testing the system beyond its specified limits to determine its breaking point
Testing the mental stress levels of developers during project deadlines
explanation: Stress testing evaluates system behavior under extreme conditions beyond normal capacity to identify breaking points and failure modes.

text: What is 'test automation' and what are its primary benefits?
options: Replacing all manual processes in the organization with automated workflows
Automatically hiring testers when the project reaches the testing phase
Automating the creation of test documentation without running any tests
Using software tools to execute tests automatically, increasing speed and repeatability
explanation: Test automation uses tools to execute tests automatically, providing faster feedback, consistent execution, better regression coverage, and freeing testers for exploratory work.

text: What is 'decision table testing'?
options: A technique that uses tables of input combinations and expected outputs to design test cases
A table showing which developer is responsible for writing each test case
A technique where project managers decide which tests to skip
A database table that stores all test results for historical analysis
explanation: Decision table testing uses a table listing combinations of inputs and their corresponding expected outputs to systematically design test cases for complex logic.

text: What is a 'mock object' in unit testing?
options: An incomplete object that crashes intentionally to test error handling
A simulated object that mimics the behavior of a real dependency and verifies interactions
A humorous fake object created for team entertainment during testing sessions
A physical model of the software used for demonstrations to clients
explanation: A mock object simulates a real dependency, providing controlled responses and allowing verification that specific interactions occurred during testing.

text: What is 'pairwise testing' (all-pairs testing) and when is it effective?
options: Running two identical tests simultaneously to compare results for consistency
Testing that pairs each requirement with exactly one test case for traceability
Testing all possible pairs of input parameter values to detect interaction faults efficiently
Testing performed by pairs of developers working together at one workstation
explanation: Pairwise testing covers all possible combinations of each pair of input parameters, effectively detecting interaction faults with far fewer test cases than exhaustive testing.

text: What is 'property-based testing' and how does it differ from example-based testing?
options: Testing the physical properties of hardware components before software installation
Testing real estate property management software with fixed test data sets
Testing by validating CSS properties of user interface elements across browsers
Testing that verifies general properties hold for randomly generated inputs rather than specific examples
explanation: Property-based testing verifies that general properties or invariants hold for randomly generated inputs, discovering edge cases that example-based tests might miss.

text: What is 'chaos engineering' and how does it improve system reliability?
options: Deliberately introducing failures into production systems to identify weaknesses
Creating chaotic work environments to test developer resilience under pressure
Disabling all monitoring tools to see how long before failures are noticed
Randomly deleting source code to test the effectiveness of backup systems
explanation: Chaos engineering deliberately introduces controlled failures into production systems to identify weaknesses, verify resilience, and improve system reliability before real failures occur.

text: What is 'model-based testing' and what advantages does it provide?
options: Testing performed by fashion models who evaluate the visual design of interfaces
Generating test cases automatically from a model of the system's expected behavior
Creating physical scale models of the software architecture for review meetings
Testing that uses only the latest model of hardware for performance evaluation
explanation: Model-based testing automatically generates test cases from a formal model of the system's expected behavior, providing systematic coverage and reducing manual test design effort.

text: What is the 'test pyramid' concept and why is it important?
options: A hierarchy showing that pyramid schemes should be tested more thoroughly
A technique for building tests from the top of the application down to the database
A strategy recommending many unit tests, fewer integration tests, and even fewer UI tests
A physical pyramid-shaped structure where testing equipment is stored
explanation: The test pyramid recommends having many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and few slow UI/E2E tests at the top for efficient testing.

text: What is 'fuzz testing' (fuzzing) and what types of defects does it find?
options: Testing software with fuzzy images to check image processing accuracy
Testing software while wearing fuzzy gloves to simulate accessibility challenges
Applying blur filters to the interface to test readability under poor conditions
Providing random, unexpected, or malformed input to find crashes and security vulnerabilities
explanation: Fuzz testing provides random, invalid, or unexpected data as input to software to discover crashes, memory leaks, buffer overflows, and security vulnerabilities.

text: What is 'contract testing' in microservices architectures?
options: Verifying that services adhere to the communication contracts defined between consumer and provider
Testing the contractual obligations of developers as specified in employment agreements
Validating that software license contracts are properly stored in the database
Testing that legal contracts between vendors are properly formatted and signed
explanation: Contract testing verifies that service consumers and providers adhere to agreed-upon communication contracts, ensuring compatibility without requiring full integration testing.

text: What is the difference between 'top-down' and 'bottom-up' integration testing?
options: Top-down is performed by managers; bottom-up is performed by junior developers
Top-down tests from the UI first using stubs; bottom-up tests from low-level modules first using drivers
Top-down tests the highest priority features; bottom-up tests the lowest priority features
Top-down tests on the newest hardware; bottom-up tests on the oldest hardware
explanation: Top-down integration starts from the top module using stubs for lower modules, while bottom-up starts from the lowest modules using drivers for higher ones.

text: What is 'test oracle problem' in software testing?
options: The difficulty of accessing the Oracle database during testing sessions
The difficulty of hiring oracle-certified professionals for the testing team
The challenge of determining the expected correct output for a given test input
The problem of Oracle software licenses being too expensive for test environments
explanation: The test oracle problem is the challenge of determining the expected correct output for a given input, which can be difficult for complex computations or novel systems.

text: What is 'metamorphic testing' and how does it address the oracle problem?
options: Testing software that undergoes metamorphosis during its development lifecycle
Testing the transformation of data from one format to another in the pipeline
Testing software on metamorphic rock-shaped hardware for geological applications
Testing by verifying relationships between inputs and outputs across related test cases
explanation: Metamorphic testing addresses the oracle problem by verifying metamorphic relations, expected relationships between inputs/outputs of related test cases, rather than checking exact outputs.

text: What is 'perfective maintenance'?
options: Modifying software to improve performance or add new features based on user feedback
Fixing bugs and defects found in the production software system
Adapting the software to work in a changed operating environment
Preparing the software for potential future changes that have not yet occurred
explanation: Perfective maintenance involves modifying software to improve performance, maintainability, or other attributes, or to add new features requested by users.

text: What is 'preventive maintenance' in software?
options: Maintaining the development tools to prevent them from becoming outdated
Making changes to software to prevent future problems before they occur
Maintaining the physical security of the office to prevent break-ins
Preventing any changes to the software to maintain its current stability
explanation: Preventive maintenance involves making changes to software to detect and correct latent faults before they manifest as operational failures.

text: What percentage of total software costs is typically attributed to maintenance?
options: Less than 10 percent of total costs
Exactly 100 percent of total costs
About 60 to 80 percent of total costs
About 20 to 30 percent of total costs
explanation: Studies consistently show that maintenance accounts for approximately 60-80% of total software lifecycle costs, making it the most expensive phase.

text: What is 'software re-engineering'?
options: Engineering software for the second time after the first attempt failed completely
Training new engineers to join the software development team immediately
Developing entirely new software without any reference to existing systems
Restructuring or rewriting existing software to improve its quality without changing functionality
explanation: Software re-engineering involves examining and modifying existing software to reconstitute it in a new form, improving maintainability, performance, or other qualities.

text: What is a 'change request' in software maintenance?
options: A formal proposal for modifying the software to fix a bug or add a feature
A request to change the office layout for the development team members
A form for requesting new computer hardware for development purposes
A request to change the project manager to a more experienced person
explanation: A change request is a formal proposal to modify the software system, whether to fix a defect, add a feature, or make an improvement.

text: What is 'reverse engineering' in the context of software maintenance?
options: Writing code backwards to make it more difficult for competitors to copy
Analyzing existing software to understand its design and implementation
Engineering software that runs in reverse chronological order
Reversing all recent changes to restore the software to an earlier version
explanation: Reverse engineering analyzes existing software to extract design and implementation information, helping maintainers understand systems with poor or missing documentation.

text: What is 'impact analysis' in software maintenance?
options: Analyzing the physical impact of dropping hardware from a height
Measuring the marketing impact of new software features on sales
Assessing the effects of a proposed change on the rest of the system
Evaluating the environmental impact of running servers continuously
explanation: Impact analysis evaluates the potential effects of a proposed change on other parts of the system, helping assess the cost, risk, and scope of maintenance activities.

text: What is 'software migration'?
options: Moving birds tracking software from one research station to another
The seasonal movement of development teams between different office locations
Transferring project management responsibilities from one manager to another
Moving software from one operating environment to another platform or technology
explanation: Software migration is the process of moving software from one operating environment to another, such as changing platforms, databases, or programming languages.

text: What is 'software retirement' or 'decommissioning'?
options: The planned removal of a software system from active service
Giving retirement benefits to senior software developers who are leaving
The process of archiving project documentation after a sprint ends
Retiring old hardware while keeping the same software running
explanation: Software retirement is the planned withdrawal of a software system from active service, including data migration, user notification, and transition to replacement systems.

text: What is 'software documentation maintenance'?
options: Deleting all documentation after the software is deployed to production
Keeping documentation up to date with changes made to the software
Writing documentation only during the initial development phase
Printing and binding all project documentation into physical books
explanation: Documentation maintenance involves updating software documentation to reflect changes made during maintenance activities, keeping it accurate and useful.

text: What is 'Lehman's law of continuing growth' in software maintenance?
options: The development team must continuously grow in size for every maintenance release
The project budget must increase by a fixed percentage each fiscal year
The functional content of a system must be continually increased to maintain user satisfaction
The physical size of the server room must continue to grow over time
explanation: Lehman's law of continuing growth states that to maintain user satisfaction over the lifetime of a system, its functional content must be continually increased.

text: What is 'code decay' and what factors contribute to it?
options: The automatic deletion of code files that have not been accessed recently
The physical degradation of storage media containing the source code files
The process by which compiled code becomes incompatible with newer CPUs
The gradual deterioration of code quality due to repeated modifications and poor practices
explanation: Code decay is the gradual deterioration of software quality due to repeated modifications, lack of refactoring, accumulated technical debt, and inadequate documentation.

text: What is the 'ripple effect' in software maintenance?
options: The phenomenon where a change in one module causes unexpected changes in other modules
The pattern of water ripples used to test fluid simulation software
The visual ripple animation effect used in mobile user interface design
The effect of dropping a stone into the office water cooler during meetings
explanation: The ripple effect occurs when a change to one part of the software causes unintended side effects in other parts, increasing the cost and risk of maintenance.

text: What is 'program comprehension' and why is it critical for maintenance?
options: A test to check if developers can comprehend a programming language textbook
The process of understanding existing code, which takes most of a maintainer's time
A document that comprehensively lists all programs installed on a computer
A certification exam that maintainers must pass before being allowed to modify code
explanation: Program comprehension is the process of understanding existing source code and its behavior, consuming up to 60% of a maintainer's time and directly affecting maintenance efficiency.

text: What is the difference between 'restructuring' and 're-engineering' in maintenance?
options: Restructuring and re-engineering are identical processes used interchangeably
Re-engineering is always cheaper and faster than restructuring in all projects
Restructuring changes code organization without adding features; re-engineering may include new functionality
Restructuring only applies to databases; re-engineering only applies to source code
explanation: Restructuring reorganizes code structure without changing functionality, while re-engineering may involve a broader examination and transformation that could include adding new capabilities.

text: What factors should be considered when deciding whether to maintain or replace a legacy system?
options: Only the personal preferences of the development team should be considered
Only the age of the system measured in calendar years since initial deployment
Only the programming language the system was originally written in
Business value, maintenance cost, technical risk, and availability of replacement options
explanation: The decision to maintain or replace a legacy system should consider its business value, ongoing maintenance costs, technical risks, and the feasibility of replacement options.

text: What is 'service level agreement' (SLA) in the context of software maintenance?
options: A formal agreement defining maintenance response times, availability targets, and support levels
A contract for purchasing new server hardware for the development environment
An agreement between building management and tenants about office maintenance
An agreement between developers about which programming language to use
explanation: An SLA in maintenance defines agreed-upon service levels including response times for different severity issues, system availability targets, and support coverage.

text: What is 'software wrapping' as a maintenance strategy for legacy systems?
options: Physically wrapping servers in protective material for shipping purposes
Encapsulating legacy components with new interfaces to enable integration with modern systems
Compressing the source code to reduce its storage size on the file system
Gift-wrapping the software product for presentation to clients and stakeholders
explanation: Software wrapping encapsulates legacy system components with new interfaces, allowing them to be accessed by modern systems without modifying the original code.

text: How does 'configuration management' support software maintenance?
options: It manages the configuration of office computers and printers for the team
It configures the office layout for optimal maintenance team productivity
It tracks and controls changes to software artifacts, ensuring consistency and traceability
It configures the marketing strategy for software maintenance service offerings
explanation: Configuration management tracks all changes to software artifacts, maintaining version history, ensuring consistency across environments, and supporting change traceability.

text: What is 'maintainability index' and how is it calculated?
options: The number of maintenance requests received divided by the team size
A measure of how many maintenance windows are available each month
A ranking of maintainers from best to worst based on manager evaluations
A composite metric using Halstead volume, cyclomatic complexity, and lines of code
explanation: The maintainability index is a composite metric calculated from Halstead volume, cyclomatic complexity, and lines of code, indicating how easy software is to maintain.

text: What is 'software archaeology' and when is it needed?
options: Investigating and understanding poorly documented legacy systems through code analysis techniques
Excavating buried hardware from old data centers for museum exhibitions
Studying the ancient history of computer science as an academic discipline
Collecting vintage software artifacts for preservation in technology museums
explanation: Software archaeology uses techniques like code analysis, version history examination, and pattern recognition to understand poorly documented legacy systems.

text: What is the 'bathtub curve' concept as applied to software reliability?
options: A design curve used for creating bathtub-shaped user interface elements
A failure rate curve showing high initial failures, stable middle period, and increasing late failures
A curve showing that software works best when developed in a relaxing environment
A curve that measures the water usage of data center cooling systems over time
explanation: The bathtub curve shows high failure rates in early life (bugs discovered), a stable middle period, and increasing failures in later life due to accumulated changes and decay.

text: What are 'anti-regressive' maintenance practices?
options: Practices that discourage development teams from regressing to older technologies
Practices that remove all regression test suites to speed up the release process
Practices that prevent maintenance changes from introducing new defects into the system
Practices that prevent the software from returning to a previous version
explanation: Anti-regressive practices prevent maintenance from introducing new defects, including thorough regression testing, code reviews, and impact analysis before changes.

text: How does 'software product line engineering' reduce maintenance effort?
options: By eliminating all maintenance activities and replacing them with new development
By creating a single product that serves all customers without any customization
By outsourcing all maintenance work to external contractors in different countries
By managing shared core assets and systematic reuse across a family of related products
explanation: Software product line engineering reduces maintenance by managing shared core assets across a family of products, so fixes and improvements to core assets benefit all products.

text: What is 'refactoring to patterns' and how does it improve maintainability?
options: Gradually restructuring code toward known design patterns to improve structure and flexibility
Randomly applying design patterns to all classes regardless of whether they are needed
Converting all patterns in fabric design software to improve rendering quality
Removing all design patterns from the code to simplify the overall architecture
explanation: Refactoring to patterns gradually restructures existing code toward well-known design patterns when they address specific design problems, improving structure and flexibility.

text: What is 'feature interaction' problem in software maintenance?
options: The interaction between features in the user interface animation system
Unexpected behavior arising when independently correct features interfere with each other
The social interaction between feature team members during sprint planning
The process of features interacting with the database to retrieve stored data
explanation: Feature interaction occurs when independently correct features produce unexpected behavior when combined, becoming increasingly problematic as systems accumulate features over time.

text: What is the 'software rejuvenation' technique?
options: Rejuvenating the development team by hiring younger programmers exclusively
Rebranding old software with a new name and logo for marketing purposes
Proactively restarting or refreshing software to prevent failures caused by aging effects
Converting old monochrome interfaces to colorful modern designs
explanation: Software rejuvenation proactively restarts or refreshes running software to counteract software aging effects like memory leaks and resource exhaustion, preventing failures.

text: What is the 'change proneness' metric in software maintenance?
options: A metric tracking how often the project manager changes the project scope
A measure of how prone developers are to making typographical errors
A measure of the proneness of hardware to physical damage from changes
A metric indicating how frequently a software component is modified over time
explanation: Change proneness measures how frequently a software component is modified, helping identify unstable areas that may benefit from refactoring or redesign.

text: How does 'clone detection' improve software maintenance?
options: It identifies duplicated code fragments that increase maintenance burden and inconsistency risk
It detects unauthorized copies of the software distributed without licenses
It detects when developers clone the project repository without authorization
It identifies duplicate records in the database that waste storage resources
explanation: Clone detection identifies duplicated code fragments in the codebase, which increase maintenance burden because changes may need to be applied consistently across all copies.

text: What is the 'architectural technical debt' concept?
options: The financial cost of hiring architects for building the office infrastructure
The accumulated cost of architectural shortcuts that compromise long-term system qualities
The total cost of purchasing architectural design software and related tools
The debt incurred by architecture students for their university education loans
explanation: Architectural technical debt is the accumulated cost of architectural shortcuts and compromises that degrade system qualities, requiring increasingly expensive future corrections.

text: What is 'software quality assurance' (SQA)?
options: A department responsible for assuring customers that the software is free
A process for purchasing high-quality computer hardware for development
A systematic process for ensuring software meets quality standards throughout development
A training program for teaching developers how to write beautiful code
explanation: Software quality assurance is a systematic process of monitoring and improving the software development process to ensure the final product meets defined quality standards.

text: What is a 'software defect'?
options: A design element that makes the user interface more visually appealing
A feature that works exactly as specified in the requirements document
A type of software license that allows free distribution of the product
A flaw in the software that causes it to produce incorrect or unexpected results
explanation: A software defect is a flaw, error, or fault in the software that causes it to produce incorrect results or behave in unintended ways.

text: What is the difference between 'quality assurance' and 'quality control'?
options: Quality assurance focuses on process improvement; quality control focuses on product inspection
Quality control focuses on process improvement; quality assurance focuses on product inspection
Quality assurance and quality control are identical activities with no differences
Quality assurance is performed by managers; quality control is performed by developers
explanation: Quality assurance is proactive, focusing on improving processes to prevent defects, while quality control is reactive, focused on inspecting products to identify defects.

text: What is a 'software inspection' or 'formal review'?
options: A review of the company's financial statements by external auditors
A structured process of examining software artifacts to find defects
An inspection of developer workstations to ensure they meet hardware standards
A government inspection of the office building for safety compliance
explanation: A software inspection is a formal, structured review process where software artifacts are systematically examined by peers to identify defects and improve quality.

text: What is 'ISO 9001' in the context of software quality?
options: A programming language standard for writing high-quality software code
A network protocol for transmitting quality data between testing servers
An international standard for quality management systems applicable to software organizations
A hardware specification for quality-certified computer components
explanation: ISO 9001 is an international standard for quality management systems that can be applied to software development organizations to ensure consistent quality processes.

text: What is a 'bug report' or 'defect report'?
options: A report on the number of debugging hours spent by each developer
A report about insect infestations in the office building
A financial report on the cost of fixing all issues in the project
A document describing a software defect including steps to reproduce and expected behavior
explanation: A bug report documents a software defect with details including steps to reproduce, expected behavior, actual behavior, and environment information.

text: What is 'peer review' in software quality?
options: An evaluation of work products by colleagues to identify defects and improvements
A review of the company by peer organizations in the same industry
A review of the peer-to-peer networking protocol used by the application
An annual performance review conducted between developers of equal seniority
explanation: Peer review is the examination of software work products by colleagues to identify defects, improve quality, and share knowledge among team members.

text: What is 'defect tracking' in software quality management?
options: Tracking the daily movements of team members who introduced the defects
Recording, monitoring, and managing software defects through their lifecycle
Monitoring the stock price of companies that produce defective software
Using GPS to track the location of defective hardware components
explanation: Defect tracking involves recording, monitoring, and managing software defects from discovery through resolution, ensuring all issues are properly addressed.

text: What is the purpose of a 'quality audit'?
options: To count the number of quality-certified employees in the organization
To audit the sound quality of the office communication system equipment
To systematically evaluate whether quality activities comply with planned arrangements
To audit the financial accounts of the quality assurance department
explanation: A quality audit systematically evaluates whether quality activities and results comply with planned arrangements and whether those arrangements are effectively implemented.

text: What is 'root cause analysis' in quality management?
options: Analyzing the growth of plant roots in agricultural management software
Analyzing the root directory structure of the application's file system
A technique for tracing the source code back to its original developer
A method for identifying the fundamental cause of a defect to prevent recurrence
explanation: Root cause analysis identifies the underlying fundamental cause of a defect, enabling corrective actions that prevent similar defects from occurring in the future.

text: What is the 'Pareto principle' (80/20 rule) as applied to software quality?
options: Approximately eighty percent of defects originate from twenty percent of the modules
Eighty percent of developers produce twenty percent of all code in the project
Twenty percent of test cases should be automated and eighty percent manual
Eighty percent of the budget should go to development and twenty to testing
explanation: The Pareto principle in software quality suggests that roughly 80% of defects come from 20% of the modules, helping focus quality improvement efforts on the most problematic areas.

text: What is the 'defect density' metric?
options: The density of comments in the source code that describe known defects
The number of confirmed defects per unit of software size such as per thousand lines of code
The number of developers per square meter of office space in the QA department
The physical density of the server hardware used for testing environments
explanation: Defect density measures the number of confirmed defects per unit of software size, such as per thousand lines of code (KLOC), indicating the quality level of the software.

text: What is 'Fagan inspection' and why is it considered effective?
options: An automated tool that inspects source code for formatting violations only
A casual code review performed while drinking coffee in the office break room
A structured review process with defined roles and steps that has been proven to find defects efficiently
An inspection named after a famous building inspector for checking office safety
explanation: Fagan inspection is a formal, structured review process with defined roles (moderator, author, reviewers, recorder) and steps that has been empirically proven to find defects efficiently.

text: What is 'process maturity' and how does it relate to software quality?
options: The age requirement for developers to be considered mature enough for the team
The age of the project measured in years since the initial planning phase
The maturity level of the programming language used for the project
The degree to which development processes are defined, managed, measured, and optimized
explanation: Process maturity indicates how well development processes are defined, managed, and optimized; higher maturity correlates with more consistent and higher quality software.

text: What is 'reliability' as a software quality attribute?
options: The probability that software performs without failure for a specified period under specified conditions
The software's ability to be easily modified for different use cases
The ability of the software to be deployed on multiple operating systems
The speed at which the software responds to user input actions
explanation: Reliability is the probability that software operates without failure for a specified time period under specified conditions, a key quality attribute for critical systems.

text: What is a 'quality gate' in software development?
options: A physical gate at the entrance to the quality assurance department office
A checkpoint where quality criteria must be met before proceeding to the next phase
A security gate that prevents unauthorized access to quality test results
A gate that controls the number of features added to each software release
explanation: A quality gate is a checkpoint in the development process where predefined quality criteria must be satisfied before work can proceed to the next phase or stage.

text: What is 'Six Sigma' and how does it apply to software quality?
options: A software development framework that requires exactly six development phases
A team of six developers who specialize in fixing the most critical bugs
A data-driven methodology for reducing defects to fewer than 3.4 per million opportunities
A testing tool that runs six different types of tests simultaneously
explanation: Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology that aims to reduce defects to fewer than 3.4 per million opportunities through systematic process improvement using DMAIC.

text: What is the 'mean time between failures' (MTBF) metric?
options: The average time it takes to hire a replacement for a developer who leaves
The average time developers take between coffee breaks during the work day
The time between the first and last failure discovered during testing
The average time between consecutive failures of a software system
explanation: MTBF measures the average time between consecutive failures of a system, indicating its reliability; higher MTBF values indicate more reliable software.

text: What is 'continuous quality improvement' in software development?
options: An ongoing effort to improve development processes and product quality incrementally
Improving the quality of internet connection continuously for better development
Improving the quality of office furniture continuously throughout the year
Continuously increasing the number of quality assurance staff each quarter
explanation: Continuous quality improvement is an ongoing effort to incrementally improve development processes and product quality through regular assessment, feedback, and adjustment.

text: What is the 'cost of poor quality' (COPQ) concept?
options: The cost of purchasing poor quality office supplies for the development team
The total cost resulting from producing defective software including rework, failures, and lost customers
The salary cost of hiring underqualified developers for the project team
The discount given to customers who purchase low-quality software products
explanation: COPQ includes all costs resulting from poor quality: internal failure costs (rework, scrap), external failure costs (warranty, lost customers), and opportunity costs.

text: What is the 'defect removal efficiency' (DRE) metric and how is it calculated?
options: The number of defects removed per hour of developer effort spent on fixes
The speed at which the janitor removes physical defects from office furniture
The ratio of defects found before release to total defects, measuring quality process effectiveness
The efficiency of the defect tracking tool's database query performance
explanation: DRE equals defects found before release divided by total defects (before and after release), measuring how effectively the quality process catches defects before customers do.

text: What is the 'Rayleigh model' for defect prediction?
options: A model for predicting the performance of Ray tracing algorithms in graphics software
A model for predicting the scattering of light through software interface elements
A model named after a quality assurance manager who invented defect tracking tools
A model that predicts defect discovery rates over time following a Rayleigh distribution curve
explanation: The Rayleigh model predicts that defect discovery rates follow a Rayleigh distribution, peaking during testing and declining afterward, helping plan testing resources.

text: What is 'statistical process control' (SPC) applied to software quality?
options: Using statistical methods to monitor and control software processes within defined limits
Using statistics to control which processes are installed on the production server
A process for statistically sampling users to determine their satisfaction levels
Controlling the number of statistical reports generated by the quality department
explanation: SPC uses statistical methods like control charts to monitor software processes, distinguishing between normal variation and assignable causes to maintain process stability.

text: What is the 'Orthogonal Defect Classification' (ODC) system?
options: A classification of developers based on the types of defects they typically introduce
A scheme that classifies defects along multiple independent dimensions to provide process feedback
A filing system for organizing defect reports in orthogonal office file cabinets
A method for detecting defects that occur at right angles in printed circuit boards
explanation: ODC classifies defects along independent dimensions like defect type, trigger, and impact, providing actionable feedback about the development process and its weaknesses.

text: What is the 'Cleanroom' software engineering approach to quality?
options: A method for cleaning up messy code through automated formatting tools only
Developing software in a physically clean room to prevent dust from affecting hardware
A rigorous approach using formal methods and statistical testing to achieve near-zero defect software
A testing approach that requires testers to wash their hands before touching keyboards
explanation: Cleanroom software engineering combines formal specification, incremental development with statistical testing, and team reviews to produce software with very low defect rates.

text: How does 'software reliability growth modeling' help in quality decisions?
options: It calculates the growth in team size needed to maintain quality standards
It models the physical growth of server hardware to determine upgrade schedules
It models the growth of software company revenue to plan quality budgets
It predicts remaining defects and time to achieve reliability targets based on testing data
explanation: Software reliability growth models use testing data to predict the number of remaining defects and the testing effort needed to reach a target reliability level.

text: What is 'total quality management' (TQM) as applied to software?
options: A management philosophy emphasizing continuous improvement with involvement from all employees
A quality approach that applies only to the final testing phase of development
A management style where the total number of quality staff equals the development staff
A system for managing the total inventory of quality testing tools and equipment
explanation: TQM is a management philosophy that emphasizes continuous improvement, customer focus, and involvement of all employees in quality improvement throughout the organization.

text: What is 'Kaizen' and how does it apply to software quality improvement?
options: A quality certification awarded only to Japanese software development companies
A philosophy of continuous incremental improvement through small daily changes by all team members
A martial arts technique applied metaphorically to aggressive defect elimination
A Japanese programming language designed specifically for quality assurance tools
explanation: Kaizen is a Japanese philosophy of continuous incremental improvement through small daily changes, applied to software through regular retrospectives and process refinements.

text: What is 'ISO/IEC 25010' and how does it define software quality?
options: A networking protocol for transmitting quality metrics between testing systems
A certification program for individual software quality engineers worldwide
A quality model defining eight characteristics including functional suitability and reliability
A standard for measuring the physical dimensions of software packaging materials
explanation: ISO/IEC 25010 defines a software product quality model with eight characteristics: functional suitability, performance efficiency, compatibility, usability, reliability, security, maintainability, and portability.

text: What is 'defect clustering' and what does it imply for quality assurance?
options: The practice of reporting multiple minor defects as a single clustered defect report
Clustering test servers together to improve the speed of automated test execution
Grouping quality assurance staff into clusters for maximum office space efficiency
The tendency for defects to concentrate in a small number of modules, guiding focused testing
explanation: Defect clustering is the observation that defects tend to concentrate in a small number of modules, implying that testing effort should focus on these defect-prone areas.

text: What is a 'software metric'?
options: A quantitative measure of some property of software or its development process
A unit of currency used to pay software developers globally
A type of software license that measures usage per user
A physical measurement of the computer hardware dimensions
explanation: A software metric is a quantitative measure of some attribute of software or its development process, used to assess quality, productivity, and progress.

text: What does 'lines of code' (LOC) measure?
options: The length of cables connecting servers in the data center rack
The size of a software program by counting the number of source code lines
The number of telephone lines installed in the development office
The number of lines in the project management Gantt chart timeline
explanation: Lines of code (LOC) is a simple size metric that counts the number of lines in the source code, used for estimation and productivity comparison.

text: What is 'code complexity' as a software metric?
options: The complexity of the hiring process for new software developers
A measure of how complex the project logo and branding design is
A measure of how difficult the source code is to understand and maintain
The complexity of the office building layout where developers work
explanation: Code complexity metrics measure how difficult source code is to understand, test, and maintain, helping identify areas that may need simplification or refactoring.

text: What is 'defect density' as a quality metric?
options: The physical density of documentation containing defect descriptions
The density of defect tracking tool installations across office locations
The weight per unit volume of defective hardware components
The number of defects found per unit of code size such as per thousand lines
explanation: Defect density measures the number of defects per unit of code size, such as defects per thousand lines of code, indicating the quality level of the software.

text: What is 'productivity' as a software metric?
options: The amount of output produced per unit of input in software development
The number of productive meetings held per week in the project
The productivity of the office air conditioning system in cooling the building
The physical product manufactured by a software development company
explanation: Productivity in software development measures the amount of output (such as function points or features) produced per unit of input (such as person-months of effort).

text: What is a 'process metric' in software engineering?
options: A metric that measures the temperature of the food processing plant
A measurement of a characteristic of the software development process
A measurement of the distance between the office and the nearest restaurant
A metric that counts the number of processes running on the server
explanation: Process metrics measure characteristics of the software development process, such as cycle time, defect injection rate, and process compliance.

text: What is a 'product metric' in software engineering?
options: A metric measuring the retail price of the software in stores
A measurement of the weight of the physical product packaging
A measurement of a characteristic of the software product itself
A metric tracking the number of products the company sells annually
explanation: Product metrics measure characteristics of the software product itself, such as size, complexity, reliability, and quality attributes.

text: What is 'mean time to repair' (MTTR)?
options: The duration of the lunch break that team members take for repair
The average time to deliver a repair person to the office building
The time to repair broken keyboards used by the development team
The average time required to fix a software failure and restore service
explanation: MTTR measures the average time required to diagnose and fix a software failure and restore the system to normal operation.

text: What is 'test coverage' as a metric?
options: The percentage of software elements exercised by the test suite
The physical area covered by test equipment cables in the testing lab
The geographical coverage of the testing team across different locations
The insurance coverage purchased for testing equipment and hardware
explanation: Test coverage measures the percentage of software elements such as statements, branches, or paths that are exercised by the test suite.

text: What is 'schedule variance' in project metrics?
options: The schedule of variance analysis meetings held by the quality team
The difference between the planned schedule and the actual project progress
The variation in work schedules between different team members
The variance in the daily starting time of team members arriving at work
explanation: Schedule variance measures the difference between planned and actual project progress, indicating whether the project is ahead of or behind schedule.

text: What are 'Halstead complexity measures' and what do they quantify?
options: Measures of the physical complexity of the office building floor plan
Measurements of the complexity of employee onboarding procedures
Metrics based on operators and operands in source code to measure program difficulty and effort
Calculations of network packet complexity in the organization's infrastructure
explanation: Halstead complexity measures use counts of operators and operands in source code to derive metrics for program vocabulary, length, volume, difficulty, and effort.

text: How does 'cyclomatic complexity' help in testing?
options: It calculates the number of test environments required for the project
It determines the number of testers needed for the quality assurance team
It measures the cycling speed of the hard drive during test execution
It indicates the minimum number of independent test paths needed to cover all branches
explanation: Cyclomatic complexity indicates the minimum number of linearly independent paths through the code, defining the minimum number of test cases needed for complete branch coverage.

text: What is the 'coupling between objects' (CBO) metric in object-oriented design?
options: The count of classes to which a given class is coupled through method calls or inheritance
The coupling between keyboard and monitor on a developer workstation
The number of objects stored in a single database table at any time
The number of physical cables connecting developer workstations together
explanation: CBO counts the number of classes to which a given class is coupled, measuring the extent of class interdependence; lower CBO values indicate better modularity.

text: What is 'fan-in' and 'fan-out' as software metrics?
options: Fan-in counts team members joining; fan-out counts team members leaving
Fan-in counts modules calling a module; fan-out counts modules called by a module
Fan-in measures incoming network traffic; fan-out measures outgoing traffic
Fan-in counts air intake fans; fan-out counts exhaust fans in the server room
explanation: Fan-in counts the number of modules that call a given module, while fan-out counts the number of modules called by a given module, measuring information flow complexity.

text: What is the 'depth of inheritance tree' (DIT) metric?
options: The depth of the physical foundation of the office building
The depth of the directory tree structure on the file system
The maximum length from a class to the root in the inheritance hierarchy
The depth of nested if-else statements in a method body
explanation: DIT measures the maximum length of the path from a class to the root of the inheritance hierarchy; deeper trees indicate greater design complexity.

text: What is the purpose of 'GQM' (Goal-Question-Metric) approach?
options: A game-based quiz methodology for testing developer knowledge skills
A method for generating, questioning, and modifying project requirements
A grading system used to evaluate developer interview performance
A structured approach for defining metrics by starting with goals and deriving questions and metrics
explanation: GQM is a top-down approach that starts with organizational goals, derives questions to assess goal achievement, and identifies metrics to answer those questions.

text: What is 'weighted methods per class' (WMC) metric?
options: The sum of complexity values of all methods in a class, indicating class complexity
The average weight of objects created by the class during runtime execution
The total weight of methods documentation measured in printed pages
The number of weighted decisions made by the class designer during design
explanation: WMC sums the complexity of all methods in a class; higher values indicate greater class complexity and potentially more fault-prone design.

text: What is 'response for a class' (RFC) in the Chidamber-Kemerer metrics?
options: The number of responses a class teacher receives from students during lectures
The count of methods that can be executed in response to a message received by the class
The average response time of customer support for class-related bug reports
The response speed of the garbage collector when deallocating class instances
explanation: RFC counts the total number of methods that can potentially be executed in response to a message sent to an object of the class, indicating testing complexity.

text: What is the 'lack of cohesion of methods' (LCOM) metric?
options: The lack of communication between development and operations teams
A measure of the absence of cohesive documentation for methods
A metric indicating how poorly the methods of a class relate to each other
A measure of team cohesion based on how well team members work together
explanation: LCOM measures the degree to which methods in a class use different instance variables; high LCOM suggests the class may need to be split into multiple classes.

text: What is 'technical debt ratio' and how is it used?
options: The ratio of technical staff to non-technical staff in the organization
The ratio of technical documentation to total project documentation
The ratio of debt payments to total company revenue each quarter
The ratio of remediation cost to development cost, indicating code health
explanation: Technical debt ratio compares the cost to fix all code quality issues (remediation cost) to the total development cost, providing a percentage indicator of code health.

text: What is 'function point analysis' and how does it differ from LOC as a size metric?
options: Function points measure functionality delivered to users independently of implementation language while LOC depends on language
Function point analysis can only be applied to functional programming languages
LOC measures functionality while function points measure physical code length
Both measure the same thing in exactly the same way with identical results
explanation: Function point analysis measures software size based on functionality delivered to users, independent of implementation language, while LOC varies significantly by language choice.

text: What is the 'Chidamber-Kemerer' metric suite and what does it measure?
options: A suite of financial metrics for measuring the profitability of software projects
A set of six object-oriented design metrics including WMC, DIT, NOC, CBO, RFC, and LCOM
A set of metrics for evaluating the usability of mobile application interfaces
A suite of metrics for measuring the performance of web server hardware
explanation: The Chidamber-Kemerer suite provides six OO design metrics: WMC, DIT, NOC, CBO, RFC, and LCOM, widely used to assess object-oriented design quality.

text: What is 'Goodhart's Law' and how does it apply to software metrics?
options: A principle that good metrics are always hart (difficult) to collect and analyze
A law stating that good software metrics always lead to good software products
When a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a useful measure as people optimize for it
A law requiring all metrics to be reported in a standardized format globally
explanation: Goodhart's Law warns that when a metric becomes a target, people optimize for the metric rather than the underlying goal, potentially gaming the system and distorting behavior.

text: What is 'COSMIC function points' and how does it improve on traditional FPA?
options: A method for measuring the cosmic radiation exposure of data center hardware
A measurement of the cosmic impact of software development on the environment
A function point method that only works for cosmetics industry software applications
A functional size measurement method that handles real-time and embedded systems better than IFPUG FPA
explanation: COSMIC function points extend functional size measurement to handle real-time, embedded, and multi-layered systems better than traditional IFPUG FPA by measuring data movements.

text: What is the 'number of children' (NOC) metric and what does a high value indicate?
options: The count of immediate subclasses of a class; high values suggest broad reuse but potential misuse of inheritance
The number of child tickets created from a parent issue in the bug tracker
The number of underage users accessing the software platform without parental consent
The number of child processes spawned by the application at runtime
explanation: NOC counts the immediate subclasses of a class; high values indicate broad reuse through inheritance but may also suggest improper abstraction or overuse of inheritance.

text: What is 'code churn' as a development metric and what does it indicate?
options: The rotational speed of the hard drive when accessing source code files
The amount of code added, modified, or deleted over time, indicating instability or rework
The butter-churning process applied to dairy industry management software
The rate at which developers churn through different programming languages
explanation: Code churn measures the amount of code added, modified, or deleted over time; high churn in specific areas may indicate instability, rework, or uncertain requirements.

text: How does the 'DORA metrics' framework measure software delivery performance?
options: It measures the physical delivery speed of software CDs to retail stores
It tracks the number of doors opened in the building by the delivery team daily
It uses deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, and time to restore service
It measures the performance of the Dora Explorer educational software application
explanation: DORA metrics measure software delivery performance through four key metrics: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, and time to restore service.

text: What is 'technical debt interest' and how should it be measured?
options: The interest rate charged by banks for loans to purchase technical equipment
The personal interest that developers have in learning about technical debt
The interest earned by investing unused project budget in financial instruments
The ongoing extra cost incurred in development activities due to the presence of technical debt
explanation: Technical debt interest is the ongoing extra cost incurred during development due to technical debt, measured through increased development time, defect rates, and maintenance effort.

text: What is 'cognitive complexity' metric and how does it improve on cyclomatic complexity?
options: A metric that weights control flow structures by nesting depth, better reflecting human comprehension difficulty
A metric identical to cyclomatic complexity with only a different name
A metric that measures cognitive load on users when interacting with the interface
A metric measuring the IQ requirements for developers to join the project
explanation: Cognitive complexity improves on cyclomatic complexity by considering nesting depth and structure types, better reflecting the actual difficulty humans face when understanding code.

text: What is the 'SQALE' (Software Quality Assessment based on Lifecycle Expectations) method?
options: A method for assessing the scale weight of software packaging for shipping
A method that maps code quality issues to remediation costs organized by quality characteristics
A quality assessment that only applies to software developed during certain seasons
A scale used to measure the quality of fish in aquaculture management software
explanation: SQALE maps code quality issues to remediation costs organized by software quality characteristics, providing a structured way to visualize and manage technical debt.

text: What is 'software configuration management' (SCM)?
options: Configuring the office network routers and switches for internet access
Managing the configuration of coffee machines in the office break room
A discipline for controlling and tracking changes to software artifacts
Configuring the display settings on each developer's computer monitor
explanation: Software configuration management is the discipline of identifying, organizing, and controlling changes to software artifacts throughout the development lifecycle.

text: What is a 'configuration item' (CI)?
options: An item on the agenda of the project configuration planning meeting
An item purchased from the company store with a configuration discount
A configuration setting in the computer's BIOS setup utility
Any artifact that is placed under configuration management control
explanation: A configuration item is any artifact placed under configuration management control, such as source code files, documents, test cases, and build scripts.

text: What is a 'baseline' in configuration management?
options: A formally reviewed and agreed-upon specification that serves as a reference point
The baseline salary for new software developers joining the team
The baseline color used in the company's branding and logo design
The bottom line of the project budget spreadsheet showing total costs
explanation: A baseline is a formally reviewed and agreed-upon specification or set of artifacts that serves as a basis for further development and can only be changed through formal procedures.

text: What is 'version control' in configuration management?
options: Managing the version of office furniture used in the development area
Tracking and managing different versions of software artifacts over time
Controlling the version number printed on software packaging boxes
Controlling which version of the operating system employees must use
explanation: Version control tracks and manages different versions of software artifacts, maintaining a history of changes and enabling collaboration among multiple developers.

text: What is a 'change control board' (CCB)?
options: A circuit board that controls changes to the server hardware configuration
A surfboard used during team-building events at the beach
A group responsible for evaluating, approving, or rejecting proposed changes
A physical whiteboard used to display the current project change requests
explanation: A change control board is a group of stakeholders responsible for evaluating, approving, deferring, or rejecting proposed changes to configuration items.

text: What is a 'software release' in configuration management?
options: The event where a developer is released from the project team assignment
The release of pressure from the server room air conditioning system
A press release announcing the company's quarterly financial results
A version of the software made available for distribution to users
explanation: A software release is a specific version of the software that has been tested, approved, and made available for distribution to end users or customers.

text: What is a 'branch' in version control?
options: An independent line of development diverging from the main codebase
A physical tree branch used as decoration in the office space
A branch of computer science that focuses on algorithm design
A regional office branch of the software development company
explanation: A branch is an independent line of development that diverges from the main codebase, allowing parallel work on features, fixes, or experiments.

text: What is 'merging' in version control?
options: The process of merging two companies into a single organization
Combining changes from different branches into a single unified branch
Combining the physical offices of two development teams into one
Merging multiple database tables into a single table for simplicity
explanation: Merging combines changes from different branches into a single branch, integrating parallel development work into a unified codebase.

text: What is a 'commit' in version control?
options: A prison commitment order for developers who introduce critical bugs
A verbal commitment to complete a task by the end of the sprint
A recorded snapshot of changes saved to the version control repository
A commitment letter signed by stakeholders approving the project budget
explanation: A commit is a snapshot of changes that is saved to the version control repository, creating a permanent record of the modifications made.

text: What is the purpose of a 'build pipeline' in configuration management?
options: A pipeline for transporting construction materials to the office building site
A management pipeline showing the career progression path for developers
A physical pipeline that carries cooling water to the server room
An automated sequence of steps that builds, tests, and deploys software
explanation: A build pipeline is an automated sequence of steps that compiles code, runs tests, and prepares software for deployment, ensuring consistent and reliable builds.

text: What is 'configuration auditing' and why is it important?
options: Verifying that configuration items conform to their specifications and change records are accurate
Auditing the financial configuration of the project accounting system
Auditing the configuration of office printers and scanner devices
Checking that all developer computers have the same desktop wallpaper
explanation: Configuration auditing verifies that configuration items match their documented specifications and that all changes have been properly recorded and approved.

text: What is 'continuous integration' in the context of configuration management?
options: Continuously integrating customer feedback into marketing materials only
Automatically building and testing code changes when they are committed to the shared repository
Integrating the development office with the marketing office continuously
Continuously integrating new employees into the existing development team
explanation: Continuous integration automatically builds and tests code changes when committed to the shared repository, detecting integration issues early and maintaining code quality.

text: What is a 'merge conflict' and how should it be resolved?
options: A conflict between two companies trying to merge their business operations
A disagreement between team members about which branch naming convention to use
A situation where changes from different branches affect the same code and must be manually reconciled
A conflict between the merge sort algorithm and other sorting algorithms
explanation: A merge conflict occurs when changes from different branches modify the same lines of code, requiring a developer to manually review and reconcile the conflicting changes.

text: What is 'release management' in configuration management?
options: Controlling the release of project information to the press and media
Managing the release of employees from the company through layoffs
Managing the release of office keys to new employees on their first day
Planning, scheduling, and controlling the movement of releases through different environments
explanation: Release management plans, schedules, and controls the deployment of software releases through environments such as development, testing, staging, and production.

text: What is the purpose of 'tagging' in version control?
options: Marking specific points in the repository history as important, typically for releases
Tagging social media posts about the software product for marketing
Adding metadata tags to images used in the user interface design
Adding name tags to developer workstations for identification purposes
explanation: Tagging marks specific points in the version control history as important, typically used to identify release versions for easy reference and retrieval.

text: What is 'environment configuration management'?
options: Managing the configuration of environmental sensors in smart building software
Managing and controlling the configuration of development, testing, and production environments
Managing the environmental impact of software development on climate change
Configuring the temperature and humidity of the office environment for comfort
explanation: Environment configuration management ensures that development, testing, staging, and production environments are properly configured and consistent with each other.

text: What is the 'change management process' in SCM?
options: The process of changing the management team of the software company
A process for changing the color scheme of the project management dashboard
A formal process for requesting, evaluating, approving, implementing, and verifying changes
The process of managing spare change in the office vending machine
explanation: The change management process provides a formal procedure for requesting, evaluating, approving, implementing, and verifying changes to controlled configuration items.

text: What is 'artifact versioning' and why is it important for build reproducibility?
options: Tracking the version of artificial intelligence tools used in code generation
Creating multiple versions of office art installations for the development area
Versioning ancient artifacts in museum database management software
Assigning unique versions to build outputs so any build can be exactly reproduced later
explanation: Artifact versioning assigns unique version identifiers to build outputs, ensuring that any specific build can be exactly reproduced, which is critical for debugging and auditing.

text: What is 'dependency management' in build configuration?
options: Tracking and resolving external libraries and packages that the software depends on
Managing the dependency of the office building on external power suppliers
Tracking which developers depend on others for code reviews and approvals
Managing the emotional dependencies between team members in the project
explanation: Dependency management tracks, resolves, and maintains external libraries and packages that the software depends on, ensuring consistent and secure builds.

text: What is a 'configuration management plan' (CMP)?
options: A plan for configuring the air conditioning system in the server room
A document describing how configuration management activities will be performed on the project
A floor plan showing the configuration of office desks and equipment
A planning document for the configuration of developer training schedules
explanation: A CMP documents how configuration management will be performed, including identification, control, auditing, and status accounting of configuration items.

text: What is 'GitOps' and how does it extend traditional configuration management?
options: An operations team that exclusively uses Git for internal communication
A set of Git commands specifically designed for operations staff to use
An approach using Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and application delivery
An operations methodology that prohibits the use of any version control system
explanation: GitOps uses Git repositories as the single source of truth for both application code and infrastructure configuration, with automated reconciliation to desired state.

text: What is a 'monorepo' vs 'polyrepo' strategy and what are the trade-offs?
