options: Restructuring legacy software to improve maintainability without changing functionality
Building entirely new software with different requirements
Deploying the same software to a different region
Training new users on the existing interface
explanation: Re-engineering restructures existing software to improve maintainability.

text: How does reverse engineering support maintenance?
options: It analyzes existing code to recover lost design information
It writes new code without examining the existing system
It reverses all changes made during the last cycle
It converts high-level designs into source code
explanation: Reverse engineering analyzes code to extract design information.

text: What is the difference between restructuring and refactoring?
options: Restructuring transforms code at any level; refactoring preserves external behavior
Both describe identical activities with no differences
Restructuring changes behavior; refactoring changes requirements
Refactoring is broader and may change functionality
explanation: Restructuring is broad code transformation; refactoring specifically preserves behavior.

text: What challenges do undocumented changes create?
options: They make it difficult to understand behavior and trace future impacts
Undocumented changes improve quality by reducing documentation
They have no effect on maintenance or understanding
Documentation of changes is never necessary
explanation: Undocumented changes obscure understanding and complicate future modifications.

text: How does configuration management support maintenance?
options: It tracks and controls changes ensuring consistency and traceability
It only manages physical hardware configuration
It eliminates the need for version control
It manages employee schedules and office configurations
explanation: Configuration management tracks all changes ensuring consistency.

text: What is a maintenance metrics program?
options: A systematic approach to measuring maintenance effort and effectiveness
A fitness program for developers working in maintenance
A method for counting maintenance staff members
A financial program for investing bonus payments
explanation: A metrics program systematically measures effort, defect rates, and effectiveness.

text: How does code aging affect maintenance costs?
options: Aging code accumulates complexity that progressively increases effort
Code quality naturally improves with age without intervention
Aging has no measurable effect on maintenance cost
Older code is always less expensive to maintain
explanation: Code aging leads to accumulated complexity, increasing maintenance costs.

text: What is the role of regression testing in maintenance?
options: Ensuring changes do not break previously working functionality
Testing only newly added features without checking existing
Removing old test cases no longer relevant
Regressing software to an earlier version
explanation: Regression testing ensures modifications do not introduce new defects.

text: How does the Belady-Lehman model describe software evolution?
options: It models growth of complexity and size through successive releases
It describes only initial development and ignores maintenance
The model states complexity decreases with each release
It applies only to hardware and not to software
explanation: The Belady-Lehman model describes how systems grow in size and complexity.

text: What is software rejuvenation?
options: Proactively restarting software to prevent age-related failures like memory leaks
A cosmetic redesign of the UI with no functional changes
Permanently shutting down software at end of life
Hiring younger developers to replace experienced staff
explanation: Software rejuvenation proactively counteracts aging effects like memory leaks.

text: How does software entropy relate to maintenance effort?
options: Disorder increases over time without deliberate effort to maintain structure
Software naturally becomes more organized with each change
Entropy only affects physical hardware, not software
Maintenance effort decreases as entropy increases
explanation: Software entropy describes the tendency to become increasingly disordered.

text: What is the wrapping approach to legacy migration?
options: Providing a new interface around the legacy system while keeping its functionality
Completely rewriting from scratch without preserving anything
Physically wrapping server hardware in packaging
Deleting the legacy system without any migration
explanation: Wrapping provides a new interface around a legacy system.

text: How does architectural decay impact maintenance?
options: Eroding architecture increases coupling making changes harder and riskier
Architectural decay has no measurable impact on maintenance
Decaying architecture makes maintenance easier
Architecture only affects initial development, never maintenance
explanation: Architectural decay increases coupling and complexity, making changes harder.

text: What is maintenance debt and how does it compound?
options: Deferred maintenance creates obligations that grow increasingly costly
Maintenance debt decreases naturally without intervention
It only applies to financial aspects of budgeting
Maintenance debt does not compound and remains constant
explanation: Maintenance debt accumulates when maintenance is deferred, compounding over time.

text: How does automated program repair work?
options: Using search algorithms or ML to generate patches passing existing tests
Manually rewriting the entire codebase each time
Deleting all code containing any defect
Preventing developers from writing new code until bugs are fixed
explanation: Automated repair uses algorithms or ML to generate candidate patches.

text: What are challenges of maintaining software product lines?
options: Managing variability while maintaining a shared core asset base
Product lines require no maintenance since they share components
Each product is maintained independently with no shared concerns
Product line maintenance is always simpler than single product
explanation: Product lines require managing variability and coordinating changes.

text: How does the strangler pattern apply to modernization?
options: New functionality gradually replaces legacy components until retired
The legacy system is immediately deleted and replaced
All legacy features are preserved with no changes
The pattern only works for systems built within the last year
explanation: The strangler pattern gradually replaces legacy components.

text: What is software immortality and its maintenance implications?
options: Critical systems must be maintained indefinitely requiring sustainable strategies
All software has a fixed lifespan and stops after ten years
Software immortality means code never needs maintenance
Only operating systems need indefinite maintenance
explanation: Software immortality refers to critical systems requiring long-term strategies.

text: What does QA stand for?
options: Quality Assurance
Quick Assessment
Quantitative Analysis
Queue Allocation
explanation: QA stands for Quality Assurance.

text: What is the primary goal of QA?
options: To ensure the development process produces a quality product
To write source code as quickly as possible
To design the physical hardware architecture
To manage the financial budget of the project
explanation: QA ensures the development process and outputs meet quality standards.

text: What is a software quality standard?
options: A documented set of criteria that processes and products must meet
A ranking of programming languages from best to worst
A measurement of how fast the team writes code
A list of preferred hardware vendors
explanation: A quality standard defines expected quality attributes.

text: What is a software audit?
options: An independent examination of a product or process against standards
A financial review of the project budget
A physical inspection of the office building
A marketing survey of potential customers
explanation: An audit independently verifies compliance with standards.

text: What is the difference between QA and QC?
options: QA prevents defects in processes; QC detects defects in products
QA and QC are two names for the same activity
QA is performed by developers; QC by managers
QA deals with hardware; QC with software
explanation: QA is process-oriented and preventive; QC is product-oriented and detective.

text: What is a review in QA?
options: A formal or informal examination of a work product to identify defects
A customer review posted on a shopping site
A salary review by human resources
A movie review in an entertainment magazine
explanation: A review systematically examines a software artifact for defects.

text: What is the purpose of a checklist in QA?
options: To ensure all required quality steps are verified consistently
To list grocery items for the office kitchen
To track attendance at social events
To record personal preferences of team members
explanation: A checklist ensures all quality verification steps are performed.

text: What is a defect in quality terminology?
options: A flaw causing software to deviate from expected behavior
A feature working exactly as described in requirements
A planned limitation in the product specifications
A suggestion for future enhancement from marketing
explanation: A defect is a flaw causing deviation from expected behavior.

text: What is process improvement in QA?
options: Systematically enhancing development processes to improve quality
Improving the physical speed of the office internet
Upgrading monitors and keyboards used by the team
Increasing parking spaces available at the office
explanation: Process improvement systematically enhances development processes.

text: What is a walkthrough?
options: An informal review where the author leads the team through a work product
A physical tour of the data center
An exercise routine performed each morning
A guided tour of the neighborhood
explanation: A walkthrough is an informal author-led review.

text: How does CMMI define process maturity levels?
options: Five levels from Initial to Optimizing representing increasing capability
Two levels representing only good and bad quality
Ten levels each representing a programming language
Three levels based solely on team size
explanation: CMMI defines five maturity levels: Initial through Optimizing.

text: What is the difference between formal inspection and informal review?
options: Formal inspections follow defined procedures with roles; informal reviews are flexible
Both follow identical procedures with no differences
Formal inspections are less effective than informal reviews
Informal reviews require more documentation than inspections
explanation: Formal inspections are structured with defined roles; informal reviews are flexible.

text: How does root cause analysis improve quality?
options: It identifies underlying causes of defects to prevent recurrence
It only addresses surface-level symptoms
It replaces all testing with a single document
It assigns blame to individual developers
explanation: Root cause analysis investigates fundamental causes to prevent future occurrences.

text: What is cost of quality?
options: The total cost of prevention, appraisal, internal and external failure
Only the cost of fixing bugs during testing
The salary of the QA team members
The cost of purchasing QA tools only
explanation: Cost of quality includes prevention, appraisal, and failure costs.

text: How does statistical process control apply?
options: It uses statistical methods to monitor and control process performance
It applies statistics only to financial aspects
It replaces all testing with probability calculations
It requires no data and relies on developer intuition
explanation: SPC uses statistical methods to monitor and improve process performance.

text: What is a Fagan inspection?
options: A highly structured formal review with defined phases and roles
A casual conversation between two developers
A financial audit of the QA budget
A physical inspection of hardware components
explanation: A Fagan inspection is a rigorous review with defined phases.

text: How does ISO 9001 relate to software quality?
options: It provides a quality management framework applicable to software development
It is a programming language standard
It only applies to manufacturing, not software
It defines specific programming techniques for quality code
explanation: ISO 9001 provides a quality management framework for software development.

text: What is defect density?
options: Number of defects per unit of software size, used to assess quality
Physical density of hardware in the data center
Density of comments per line of code
Number of developers per square meter of office
explanation: Defect density measures defects per unit of software size.

text: How does pair programming contribute to QA?
options: It provides continuous code review catching defects in real time
It doubles defects because two developers make more mistakes
It eliminates the need for any formal QA processes
It only works for projects using Waterfall
explanation: Pair programming provides continuous real-time review.

text: What is Six Sigma in software quality?
options: A data-driven methodology reducing defects to fewer than 3.4 per million
A methodology allowing exactly six defects per release
A team structure requiring exactly six QA engineers
A testing approach running each test exactly six times
explanation: Six Sigma aims for near-perfect quality with fewer than 3.4 defects per million.

text: How does the Rayleigh model predict defect discovery?
options: It models discovery as a curve rising to a peak then declining
It predicts constant discovery throughout the project
It assumes all defects are found at a single point
It only applies to hardware, not software
explanation: The Rayleigh model predicts defect discovery rates rise to a peak then decline.

text: What is the difference between process and product quality?
options: Process quality concerns how development is done; product quality concerns the software
Both terms describe the exact same dimension
Process quality is always more important than product quality
Product quality is independent of the process used
explanation: Process quality focuses on development practices; product quality on delivered software.

text: How does defect causal analysis drive improvement?
options: It analyzes patterns in causes to identify systemic process problems
It only analyzes individual defects without looking for patterns
It focuses on punishing developers who introduce defects
It replaces all other quality improvement activities
explanation: Defect causal analysis examines patterns to identify systemic weaknesses.

text: What is the cleanroom software engineering approach?
options: It emphasizes defect prevention through formal methods and statistical certification
It requires development in a physically clean room
It focuses only on defect detection through testing
It eliminates formal methods and relies on experience
explanation: Cleanroom emphasizes prevention using formal specification and statistical testing.

text: How does technical debt quantification help QA decisions?
options: It provides objective data for prioritizing quality improvement investments
Technical debt cannot be quantified and is purely subjective
Quantifying debt always shows it should be ignored
Technical debt quantification replaces all other metrics
explanation: Quantifying technical debt provides data for prioritizing quality investments.

text: What is the concept of quality gates?
options: Checkpoints where predefined criteria must be met before proceeding
Physical gates at the entrance of the development office
A game where teams compete for highest quality scores
Financial thresholds for the maximum quality budget
explanation: Quality gates are checkpoints where criteria must be satisfied before proceeding.

text: How does reliability growth modeling predict maturity?
options: It tracks defect discovery and fix rates to project target reliability
It measures physical growth of server infrastructure
It predicts how quickly the team will grow in size
It only models hardware reliability, not software
explanation: Reliability growth models track defect trends to predict when target reliability is reached.

text: What are challenges of continuous quality in DevOps?
options: Balancing speed of delivery with thorough quality verification
DevOps eliminates all quality challenges by automating everything
Quality is irrelevant in DevOps since all code is deployed immediately
Only manual testing can be used in DevOps
explanation: DevOps challenges include balancing rapid delivery with quality gates.

text: How does quality attributes trade-off affect architecture?
options: Improving one attribute often degrades another requiring deliberate decisions
All attributes can be simultaneously maximized without trade-offs
Attributes are independent and changing one never affects others
Architecture has no influence on quality attributes
explanation: Quality attributes often conflict, requiring deliberate architectural trade-offs.

text: What is the role of process modeling in quality improvement?
options: It creates formal representations to analyze, optimize, and standardize processes
It physically models the office layout for the QA team
It builds 3D models of the software architecture
It generates automatic documentation without human involvement
explanation: Process modeling creates formal representations for analysis and optimization.

text: What is a software metric?
options: A quantitative measure of some property of software or its process
A physical ruler for measuring printed documents
A musical beat pattern in presentation videos
A financial currency for purchasing tools
explanation: A software metric is a quantitative measure of some attribute of software.

text: What does LOC stand for?
options: Lines of Code
Level of Complexity
Length of Compilation
Logic of Components
explanation: LOC stands for Lines of Code, a basic measure of software size.

text: What is cyclomatic complexity?
options: A measure of independent paths through source code
The total number of developers assigned to the project
The physical size of the compiled binary on disk
The time needed to write a single function
explanation: Cyclomatic complexity measures linearly independent paths through code.

text: What is code coverage?
options: The percentage of code executed during testing
The number of code comments per function
The total cost of writing source code
The speed of code compilation in seconds
explanation: Code coverage measures the percentage of code exercised by tests.

text: What is a function point?
options: A measure of software size based on functionality delivered
A point in code where a function is defined
A physical location where the team meets
A score based on coding speed
explanation: Function points measure size based on functionality provided to users.

text: What does MTBF stand for?
options: Mean Time Between Failures
Maximum Testing Before Finalization
Minimum Time Before Features
Multiple Testing By Framework
explanation: MTBF measures the average time a system operates before failing.

text: What is defect density?
options: The number of defects per unit size of the software
The physical density of bugs in a tracking database
The weight of documentation per square meter
The speed at which defects are introduced
explanation: Defect density is defects per unit of software size.

text: What is throughput in software metrics?
options: The amount of work completed in a given time period
The physical speed of data through network cables
The number of monitors connected to a workstation
The weight of server hardware shipped
explanation: Throughput measures work completed within a specific time period.

text: What is a baseline metric?
options: A reference measurement for comparing future performance
A metric measured at the bottom floor of the building
The minimum salary offered to developers
A score from a jumping competition
explanation: A baseline is a reference measurement for comparison with future measurements.

text: What is the purpose of measuring software metrics?
options: To objectively assess quality, productivity, and progress
To determine the physical appearance of the UI
To decide the color scheme for office decoration
To calculate the number of chairs in meetings
explanation: Metrics provide objective data for assessing quality and making decisions.

text: How is Halstead complexity calculated?
options: Using distinct and total operators and operands in the code
By counting only blank lines in the source files
By measuring execution time of the compiled program
By counting files in the project repository only
explanation: Halstead uses counts of operators and operands to calculate volume and effort.

text: What is the difference between leading and lagging metrics?
options: Leading predict future outcomes; lagging measure past results
Both measure only events that have already occurred
Leading are less useful than lagging in every situation
Lagging predict future events; leading measure past ones
explanation: Leading metrics are predictive indicators; lagging reflect past outcomes.

text: How does the maintainability index work?
options: It combines Halstead volume, cyclomatic complexity, and LOC into a score
It only measures the number of comments in files
It counts maintenance requests submitted per month
It measures the physical age of hardware
explanation: The maintainability index combines Halstead, cyclomatic complexity, and LOC.

text: What is fan-in and fan-out?
options: Fan-in is modules calling a module; fan-out is modules it calls
Fan-in measures cooling capacity; fan-out measures heat output
Both metrics measure the same thing from different views
They are hardware ventilation measurements only
explanation: Fan-in counts incoming dependencies; fan-out counts outgoing dependencies.

text: How do velocity metrics help agile teams?
options: They track work completed per iteration to forecast capacity
They measure the physical speed of typing
They calculate internet connection speed for remote work
They determine how fast software loads on devices
explanation: Velocity tracks work completed per sprint for capacity planning.

text: What is the difference between process and product metrics?
options: Process metrics measure activities; product metrics measure software characteristics
Both measure identical properties of the product only
Process metrics are less important than product metrics always
Product metrics measure team behavior; process metrics measure code
explanation: Process metrics measure development activities; product metrics measure software.

text: How does technical debt ratio quantify quality?
options: It compares remediation cost to development cost as a percentage
It measures the financial debt of the organization
It only applies to projects using a single language
It calculates the ratio of developers to managers
explanation: Technical debt ratio compares fix cost to development cost as a percentage.

text: What is the GQM approach?
options: Define metrics by starting with goals, deriving questions, then measures
Generate random questions for developer interviews
Measure the quality of meeting rooms and facilities
Calculate distances between geographic office locations
explanation: GQM starts with goals, derives questions, and identifies metrics.

text: How does coupling metrics assess design quality?
options: High coupling indicates poor design and higher maintenance risk
High coupling always indicates excellent design
Coupling metrics are irrelevant to design quality
Low coupling indicates modules are too independent
explanation: High coupling indicates tight dependencies making the system harder to maintain.

text: What is MTTR?
options: Mean Time To Repair, measuring recovery speed for availability
Maximum Time To Restart measuring longest shutdown
Minimum Testing Time Required for shortest testing cycle
Mean Temperature To Run measuring ideal operating temp
explanation: MTTR measures average time to restore a system after failure.

text: How does the MOOD suite evaluate OO design?
options: It measures hiding, inheritance, coupling, polymorphism, and reuse factors
It only measures team mood and satisfaction
It evaluates the color scheme and visual design
It measures only the number of classes
explanation: MOOD measures method hiding, attribute hiding, inheritance, coupling, and reuse.

text: How do CK and MOOD metrics differ?
options: CK focuses on individual class properties; MOOD assesses system-wide percentages
Both suites measure identical properties
CK is simpler and less comprehensive than MOOD
MOOD focuses on individual classes while CK assesses the whole system
explanation: CK evaluates class characteristics; MOOD assesses system-wide design properties.

text: How does measurement validity affect metric usefulness?
options: A metric is valid only if it truly measures the claimed attribute
All metrics are automatically valid regardless of what they measure
Validity only matters for hardware measurements
A metric with high validity always has low reliability
explanation: Validity ensures a metric actually measures the intended attribute.

text: What is Goodhart's Law in software metrics?
options: When a metric becomes a target it ceases to be a good metric
Metrics always remain reliable regardless of use
Goodhart's Law only applies to financial metrics
Setting metrics as targets always improves quality
explanation: Goodhart's Law warns that when a metric becomes a target, people optimize for it.

text: How do network metrics measure structural complexity?
options: They analyze the graph structure of dependencies between components
They only measure physical network bandwidth
Network metrics are identical to LOC measurements
They count network cables in the data center
explanation: Network metrics analyze dependency graph structure.

text: What is the difference between absolute and relative metrics?
options: Absolute are raw values; relative are ratios normalized for comparison
Both produce identical values for all measurements
Absolute are always more useful than relative
Relative cannot be used for comparing projects
explanation: Absolute provide raw values; relative normalize as ratios for comparison.

text: How does metric entropy help assess code evolution?
options: It measures randomness of changes across the codebase over time
It measures physical temperature of servers during compilation
It only applies to thermodynamic systems
It counts entropy-related comments in source code
explanation: Metric entropy quantifies the randomness of changes, indicating maintenance hotspots.

text: What is the role of benchmarking?
options: Comparing metrics against industry standards or similar organizations
Physically testing the strength of workbenches
Marking bench seats in the park near the office
Measuring the height of benches in the break room
explanation: Benchmarking compares metrics against standards to identify improvements.

text: How do information theory metrics apply to complexity?
options: They use entropy concepts to measure information content of code structures
They only measure bandwidth of information transfer between people
Information theory has no application to software
They calculate the cost of storing information per gigabyte
explanation: Information theory metrics apply entropy concepts to measure code complexity.

text: What challenges arise from metrics-based developer evaluation?
options: It can lead to gaming, reduced collaboration, and focus on measured over actual quality
Using metrics always improves developer performance
Developers welcome metrics-based evaluation
Metrics perfectly capture all aspects of contribution
explanation: Metrics-based evaluation can lead to gaming and reduced teamwork.

text: What is configuration management?
options: Tracking and controlling changes to software artifacts
Configuring physical hardware in a server room
Arranging office furniture for the team
Configuring email accounts for new employees
explanation: Configuration management tracks and controls changes to software artifacts.

text: What is a version control system?
options: A tool tracking file changes and enabling collaboration
A system for controlling hardware firmware versions only
A voting system for deciding feature priorities
A grading system for ranking developer performance
explanation: Version control tracks file changes and enables collaboration.

text: What is a software baseline?
options: A formally approved version of a configuration item used as reference
The first line of source code written in the project
The lowest acceptable quality level for the product
The minimum number of developers needed
explanation: A baseline is an approved version serving as a reference for changes.

text: What is a commit in version control?
options: A saved snapshot of changes to the repository at a point in time
A promise to deliver the project on schedule
A financial commitment to fund development
A decision to assign a developer to a task
explanation: A commit is a snapshot of changes saved to the repository.

text: What is a branch in version control?
options: An independent line of development diverging from the main codebase
A physical tree branch used as decoration
A department in the management structure
A type of conditional statement in code
explanation: A branch is an independent development line that does not affect main.

text: What is a merge in version control?
options: Combining changes from one branch into another
Combining two software products into a single company
Physically joining two hardware pieces with a cable
Merging two teams into a single larger team
explanation: A merge combines changes from one branch into another.

text: What is a configuration item?
options: Any artifact placed under configuration management control
A physical item on a shopping list for supplies
A menu item in the application settings page
A line item in the financial budget spreadsheet
explanation: A configuration item is any artifact under configuration management control.

text: What is the purpose of a change log?
options: To record all changes made to the software over time
To log daily activities of each team member
To track changing weather near the office
To record changes in company stock price
explanation: A change log documents all modifications to the software.

text: What is a repository?
options: A central storage location for version-controlled files
A physical warehouse for storing hardware equipment
A bookshelf in the office library
A filing cabinet for printed employee records
explanation: A repository is central storage for version-controlled files and history.

text: What is a tag in version control?
options: A label assigned to a specific version of the code
A physical price tag on software packaging
A name badge worn during team meetings
A keyword for searching documents in an editor
explanation: A tag labels a specific commit, typically marking releases.

text: What is a change control board?
options: It evaluates, approves, or rejects proposed changes
It physically controls server room temperature
It selects colors and fonts for the UI
It controls maximum hours developers work
explanation: A CCB evaluates proposed changes and decides whether to approve or reject.

text: How does branching strategy affect collaboration?
options: It determines how parallel work is organized and integrated
Branching has no effect on collaboration
All teams must use exactly the same strategy
Branching only applies to solo developers
explanation: Branching strategy affects how teams organize and integrate parallel work.

text: What is the difference between centralized and distributed VCS?
options: Centralized uses a single server; distributed gives each developer a full copy
Both work identically with no architectural differences
Centralized gives each developer a full copy
Distributed requires constant network connection
explanation: Centralized VCS uses a single server; distributed gives each developer full history.

text: What is a merge conflict?
options: Overlapping changes in the same code area requiring manual resolution
A disagreement between team members about deadlines
Conflicts resolve automatically without any intervention
It only occurs when merging files in different languages
explanation: A merge conflict occurs when overlapping changes must be manually resolved.

text: What is release management?
options: Planning, scheduling, and controlling the deployment of releases
Managing press statements to the media
Releasing developers from contractual obligations
Controlling air conditioning release in the building
explanation: Release management plans and controls the movement of releases to deployment.

text: How does Git Flow organize development?
options: It uses main, develop, feature, release, and hotfix branches
It uses only a single branch for all work
It requires a new repository for each feature
It prohibits branches and requires direct commits to main
explanation: Git Flow uses long-lived main and develop branches with feature, release, and hotfix branches.

text: What is the difference between a build and a release?
options: A build is compiled code; a release is a build approved for distribution
A build and release are identical with no difference
A release is created before the build
A build is for customers; a release is for internal testing
explanation: A build is compiled software; a release is an approved build for distribution.

text: What is continuous delivery?
options: Keeping software always in a deployable state through automated pipelines
Continuously delivering printed documentation to the customer
Delivering new hardware to developers every day
A postal service for shipping physical media
explanation: Continuous delivery ensures software is always deployable through automation.

text: What is environment configuration management?
options: Ensuring consistency across development, testing, and production environments
Managing temperature and humidity of the physical office
Configuring decorative plants in the workspace
Controlling the lighting schedule in meeting rooms
explanation: Environment configuration management ensures consistent environments.

text: How does semantic versioning communicate change?
options: Major.Minor.Patch indicates breaking changes, features, and fixes respectively
Version numbers are assigned randomly
All changes increment only the first number
Semantic versioning uses letters instead of numbers
explanation: Semantic versioning uses Major.Minor.Patch to communicate change significance.

text: How does trunk-based development differ from Git Flow?
options: Trunk-based uses short-lived branches merged frequently to main for faster CI/CD
Trunk-based uses many long-lived branches like Git Flow
Both produce identical CI/CD speeds
Git Flow enables faster CI/CD than trunk-based always
explanation: Trunk-based development uses short-lived branches merged frequently to main.

text: What is the diamond dependency problem?
options: Two modules depend on different versions of the same shared dependency
Modules arranged in a diamond shape on the org chart
Four developers work on the same file at the same time
A problem only in projects using diamond-shaped logos
explanation: The diamond dependency problem involves incompatible versions of a shared dependency.

text: How does GitOps extend configuration management?
options: It uses Git repos as the single source of truth for infrastructure
It eliminates the need for version control in infrastructure
It only applies to application code, not infrastructure
It requires manual infrastructure changes without automation
explanation: GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for infrastructure configuration.

text: What challenges arise in microservices configuration?
options: Each service has independent config that must be coordinated across the system
Microservices require no configuration management
All microservices share a single config file with no conflicts
Configuration is simpler in microservices than monoliths
explanation: Microservices require coordinating independent configurations across many services.

text: How does immutable infrastructure relate to configuration?
options: Infrastructure is never modified after deployment; changes create new instances
Infrastructure must be constantly modified in place
Immutable infrastructure eliminates configuration tracking
All changes are applied to running servers without redeployment
explanation: Immutable infrastructure treats instances as unchangeable; changes require new instances.

text: What is a software bill of materials?
options: A list of all components and dependencies for vulnerability tracking
A financial invoice for purchasing equipment
It only lists hardware components
It replaces all source code documentation
explanation: An SBOM inventories all components for vulnerability tracking and supply chain security.

text: How does feature branching at scale create challenges?
options: Long-lived branches diverge from main causing complex merges
Feature branches always merge cleanly regardless of age
Feature branching eliminates all integration problems
Scaling branches reduces the total number of conflicts
explanation: Long-lived feature branches diverge significantly, creating complex merges.

text: What is configuration drift?
options: Unplanned changes causing environments to diverge, detected by comparison tools
Drift only occurs in documentation, not actual systems
It is a natural improvement making environments more reliable
Drift cannot be detected by any automated tools
explanation: Configuration drift occurs when environments diverge from intended state.

text: How does infrastructure as code improve configuration?
options: It enables version-controlled, repeatable, and testable configurations
It replaces all software development with infrastructure projects
IaC eliminates the need for version control
It requires manual configuration through GUI interfaces
explanation: IaC manages infrastructure through version-controlled configuration files.

text: What is the monorepo versus polyrepo trade-off?
options: Monorepos simplify dependencies but increase build complexity; polyrepos isolate but complicate cross-cutting changes
Monorepos and polyrepos produce identical outcomes
Monorepos are always superior in every context
Polyrepos eliminate all dependency challenges
explanation: Monorepos simplify dependencies; polyrepos provide isolation at the cost of cross-cutting changes.

text: What is a risk in project management?
options: An uncertain event that could negatively impact the project
A guaranteed successful outcome of project activities
A type of programming language for secure coding
A physical hazard in the office building
explanation: A risk is an uncertain event that could negatively affect objectives.

text: What is risk identification?
options: Finding and documenting potential risks to the project
Writing the final project status report
Identifying the most skilled developers on the team
Selecting hardware vendors for procurement
explanation: Risk identification systematically finds and documents potential risks.

text: What is risk mitigation?
options: Taking actions to reduce the probability or impact of a risk
Ignoring all identified risks and hoping they do not occur
Increasing risk probability to test team preparedness
Delaying risk analysis until after project completion
explanation: Mitigation takes proactive steps to reduce likelihood or impact.

text: What is a risk register?
options: A document listing identified risks with assessment and response plans
A government registration form for software companies
A physical register book for signing into the building
A list of all registered domain names
explanation: A risk register records all identified risks and their response plans.

text: What is risk probability?
options: The likelihood that a specific risk event will occur
The certainty that the project will finish on schedule
The probability that all members attend every meeting
The likelihood of zero defects at launch
explanation: Risk probability is the estimated likelihood a risk event will occur.

text: What is risk impact?
options: The potential consequence if the risk event occurs
The physical force of dropping equipment from a desk
The financial cost of purchasing risk management tools
The emotional impact of overtime on morale
explanation: Risk impact is the potential consequence on objectives if the risk materializes.

text: What is risk avoidance?
options: Changing the project plan to eliminate the risk entirely
Accepting the risk and preparing a response
Transferring the risk to a third party
Monitoring the risk without any action
explanation: Risk avoidance changes the plan to completely eliminate a risk.

text: What is a contingency plan?
options: A predefined plan to execute if a specific risk occurs
The primary plan used during normal activities
A plan for celebrating project completion
A financial plan for investing profits
explanation: A contingency plan is a predefined response for a specific risk event.

text: What is risk monitoring?
options: Continuously tracking identified risks and watching for new ones
Monitoring only at the beginning and never again
Watching financial markets for investments
Monitoring physical security cameras
explanation: Risk monitoring continuously tracks risks and watches for new ones.

text: What is a risk owner?
options: The person responsible for managing a specific risk
The person who introduced the risk through a mistake
The person who owns the company
The person who discovered the risk but has no further role
explanation: A risk owner is assigned responsibility for monitoring and responding to a risk.

text: How does a probability-impact matrix work?
options: It plots risks on a grid of probability versus impact to prioritize
It multiplies all scores together for a single number
It only tracks risks that have already occurred
It eliminates the need for qualitative assessment
explanation: A probability-impact matrix plots risks for prioritization.

text: What is the difference between known and unknown risks?
options: Known risks can be identified and planned for; unknown cannot be anticipated
Known risks are more dangerous than unknown ones
Unknown risks can always be identified with enough effort
Both categories are managed with identical strategies
explanation: Known risks can be planned for; unknown risks are unforeseen.

text: How does risk transference work?
options: Shifting impact to a third party through insurance, contracts, or outsourcing
Physically moving risk from one office to another
Transferring all staff to a different department
Ignoring the risk and hoping someone else handles it
explanation: Risk transference shifts impact to a third party through contracts or insurance.

text: What is expected monetary value in risk analysis?
options: The product of probability and monetary impact for decision-making
The expected salary of the risk manager
The total budget for risk management software
The actual cost of risks that have occurred
explanation: EMV multiplies probability by monetary impact to quantify risk exposure.

text: How does risk exposure help prioritization?
options: It combines probability and impact into a single ranking value
It measures exposure to office fluorescent lighting
It only considers probability without regard for impact
It assigns identical priority to all risks
explanation: Risk exposure combines probability and impact for ranking and prioritization.

text: What is risk-driven development?
options: Development decisions guided by addressing highest priority risks first
Risks are ignored to focus only on features
All development stops when any risk is identified
Risk analysis is performed only after project completion
explanation: Risk-driven development addresses the most critical risks earliest.

text: What is the difference between a risk and an issue?
options: A risk is a potential future event; an issue has already materialized
A risk and an issue are identical concepts
An issue is less severe than a risk always
Risks only occur in waterfall; issues only in agile
explanation: A risk may happen in the future; an issue has already occurred.

text: How does sensitivity analysis help?
options: It identifies which risks have the greatest impact on outcomes
It measures emotional sensitivity of team members
It tests sensitivity of hardware sensors
It analyzes sensitivity of feedback survey questions
explanation: Sensitivity analysis determines which variables most influence outcomes.

text: What is risk categorization?
options: Grouping risks by source or area to identify patterns and allocate responses
Assigning letter grades based on alphabetical order
Categorizing by the color of the document
Sorting by date last updated in the register
explanation: Categorization groups risks by type or area to identify patterns.

text: What is risk auditing?
options: Examining effectiveness of risk responses and the management process
Auditing financial accounts of the risk team
Physically inspecting the building for hazards
Reviewing personal performance of each member
explanation: Risk auditing examines effectiveness of responses and the overall process.

text: How does Boehm's framework approach software risks?
options: It identifies top risk items, resolves them, and tracks results
It ignores risks until they become critical issues
It assigns all risks to a single person
It only addresses financial risks
explanation: Boehm's framework identifies top risks, applies resolution techniques, and monitors.

text: What is risk burndown in agile?
options: Tracking reduction of identified risks over time through sprints
Burning physical documents containing risk information
Increasing risk exposure to test team resilience
Gradual increase of risk until launch
explanation: Risk burndown tracks progressive reduction of risks in agile.

text: How do decision trees support risk analysis?
options: They model sequences of decisions and chance events to calculate outcomes
They are physical tree diagrams for the war room
They only support qualitative analysis
They require eliminating all uncertainty first
explanation: Decision trees model decisions and probabilistic events to support decision-making.

text: What is a secondary risk?
options: A new risk arising as a direct result of implementing a risk response
The second risk identified in the initial session
A risk considered less important than primary risks
A backup plan identical to the primary response
explanation: A secondary risk emerges as a consequence of implementing a response.

text: How does the OCTAVE framework work?
options: It enables organizations to self-assess security risks through asset-based profiling
It outsources all risk assessment to consultants
It focuses only on technical vulnerabilities
It is a musical training framework for team building
explanation: OCTAVE enables self-directed security risk assessment through asset-based profiling.

text: What is residual risk?
options: Risk remaining after responses are applied, managed through monitoring or contingency
Risk that has been completely eliminated
The first risk identified during planning
A risk only affecting people in residential areas
explanation: Residual risk remains after responses, managed through monitoring or contingency.

text: How does portfolio-level risk management differ from project-level?
options: It considers risk interactions across multiple projects and aligns with organizational goals
It uses the same techniques as project-level
Portfolio management focuses on task-level risks only
It eliminates the need for project-level management
explanation: Portfolio-level considers interactions across projects and organizational risk tolerance.

text: What is risk appetite versus risk tolerance?
options: Appetite is the broad level of risk accepted; tolerance is specific acceptable variation
Both terms describe the exact same concept
Appetite is always lower than tolerance
Tolerance is willingness; appetite is the threshold
explanation: Appetite defines broad acceptable risk; tolerance specifies acceptable variation.

text: How do Monte Carlo simulations enhance risk quantification?
options: They run thousands of scenarios with random variables to produce distributions
They produce a single deterministic estimate
They require no input data and generate assessments from nothing
They only work for financial risks
explanation: Monte Carlo runs thousands of iterations to produce probability distributions.

text: What is emerging risk in long-term projects?
options: Risks developing gradually due to changing technology, market, or environment
Risks identified at the beginning that never change
Risks only emerging during the final week
A type of risk only affecting emergency response software
explanation: Emerging risks develop gradually as technology and environment change.

text: What is authentication?
options: Verifying the identity of a user or system
Encrypting data stored in a database
Backing up files to an external drive
Testing software for performance issues
explanation: Authentication verifies that a user or system is who they claim to be.

text: What is authorization?
options: Determining what actions an authenticated user can perform
Verifying a user identity through credentials
Encrypting all network communications
Creating a new user account in the system
explanation: Authorization determines what resources and actions a user is allowed.

text: What is encryption?
options: Converting data into coded form to prevent unauthorized access
Compressing files to reduce storage space
Creating backup copies on external devices
Formatting a hard drive for new data
explanation: Encryption transforms data into an unreadable form decoded only with the correct key.

text: What is a firewall?
options: A security system monitoring and controlling network traffic
A physical wall preventing fire damage in the data center
A tool for creating graphical interface designs
A device increasing internet connection speed
explanation: A firewall monitors and controls traffic based on security rules.

text: What is a vulnerability?
options: A weakness in the system that can be exploited by threats
A new feature added during maintenance
A backup copy of the production database
A performance optimization applied to code
explanation: A vulnerability is a weakness that could be exploited to compromise security.

text: What is a security patch?
options: An update fixing a security vulnerability in software
A decorative element added to the interface
A new feature enhancing the user experience
A tool measuring application performance
explanation: A security patch fixes a known security vulnerability.

text: What is the principle of least privilege?
options: Users should have only the minimum permissions for their tasks
Users should have access to all resources at all times
Administrators should share passwords with all members
All users should have identical full privileges
explanation: Least privilege grants only the minimum permissions necessary.

text: What is a password hash?
options: A one-way transformation into a fixed-length string for secure storage
A method for displaying passwords in clear text
A technique for sharing passwords between accounts
A way to make passwords shorter for memorization
explanation: A password hash is a one-way cryptographic transformation for secure storage.

text: What is malware?
options: Malicious software designed to harm or exploit systems
A legitimate tool for managing email
A hardware component improving network performance
A programming language for mobile applications
explanation: Malware is software intentionally designed to damage or gain unauthorized access.

text: What is a security audit?
options: A systematic evaluation of security measures and compliance
A financial review of project budget
A performance test measuring application speed
A user satisfaction survey about the interface
explanation: A security audit evaluates the effectiveness of security measures.

text: What is SQL injection?
options: Injecting malicious SQL through user input, prevented by parameterized queries
A technique for optimizing database query performance
A method for migrating data between platforms
A tool for generating database schemas
explanation: SQL injection inserts malicious SQL; prevented by parameterized queries.

text: What is cross-site scripting?
options: Injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
A technique for sharing CSS across websites
A method for transferring files between servers
A framework for cross-platform mobile apps
explanation: XSS injects malicious scripts that execute in other users browsers.

text: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
options: Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric uses a public-private key pair
Both types use identical key management approaches
Symmetric is always stronger than asymmetric
Asymmetric uses a single shared key for everything
explanation: Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric uses a public-private key pair.

text: What is a man-in-the-middle attack?
options: An attacker intercepts and potentially alters communication between two parties
A developer sitting between two others in the office
A project manager mediating disputes between members
A component sitting between the UI and database
explanation: A MITM attack secretly intercepts and may modify communication.

text: What is the OWASP Top Ten?
options: A regularly updated list of the most critical web application security risks
A ranking of the ten best programming languages
A list of the ten largest software companies
A collection of the ten most popular frameworks
explanation: The OWASP Top Ten lists the most critical web application security risks.

text: What is defense in depth?
options: Multiple layers of security controls throughout the system
Using only a single strong firewall as the sole measure
Concentrating all security at the network perimeter only
Removing security controls to simplify architecture
explanation: Defense in depth implements multiple overlapping security layers.

text: What is the purpose of input validation?
options: Ensuring user input conforms to expected formats before processing
Validating the team has enough input devices
Confirming requirements have been properly documented
Verifying the database has sufficient input capacity
explanation: Input validation ensures data conforms to expected formats before processing.

text: What is session management in web security?
options: Controlling the lifecycle of user sessions to prevent hijacking
Managing the schedule of team meetings
Organizing training sessions for new employees
Controlling coding sessions per week for developers
explanation: Session management controls session lifecycle to prevent hijacking.

text: What is defense in depth for data protection?
options: Multiple layers including encryption, access control, and monitoring
Storing all data in a single unencrypted file
Relying solely on building security for digital data
Giving all employees full access to all data
explanation: Defense in depth for data uses encryption, access controls, and monitoring.

text: What is a denial-of-service attack?
options: Overwhelming a system with traffic to make it unavailable
Denying access to unauthorized users through authentication
A policy denying service to unpaid customers
A feature denying requests not meeting quality standards
explanation: A DoS attack floods a system, exhausting resources and blocking legitimate users.

text: How does STRIDE categorize threats?
options: Into Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, and Elevation of Privilege
Into only internal and external threat categories
Based solely on the programming language used
By the geographic location of the attacker
explanation: STRIDE categorizes threats across six dimensions of security concern.

text: What is the difference between SAST and DAST?
options: SAST analyzes source code without execution; DAST tests the running application
Both analyze the application in exactly the same way
SAST tests the running app; DAST analyzes source code
Neither can find vulnerabilities in modern applications
explanation: SAST analyzes code without execution; DAST tests the running application externally.

text: What is zero trust architecture?
options: Never trust any entity by default and verify every access request
Trust all users on the internal network without verification
Only verify external users while trusting internal ones
A network with zero security controls for maximum performance
explanation: Zero trust requires verification of every access request regardless of location.

text: How does homomorphic encryption work?
options: It allows computation on ciphertext producing encrypted results matching plaintext operations
It decrypts data before computation and re-encrypts after
It only works for simple addition operations
It requires sharing the key with all computing parties
explanation: Homomorphic encryption enables computation on encrypted data.

text: What is a security champion?
options: A team member promoting security practices and bridging to the security team
A developer writing all security code without input
An external auditor reviewing every line of code
A manager deciding which vulnerabilities to ignore
explanation: A security champion advocates for security and liaises with the security team.

text: How does certificate pinning enhance security?
options: It associates a host with its expected certificate to prevent MITM attacks
It pins physical certificates to the bulletin board
It permanently locks the app to a single connection
It removes all certificate validation to simplify connections
explanation: Certificate pinning prevents MITM attacks by constraining expected certificates.

text: What is security as code in DevSecOps?
options: Defining and enforcing security policies through version-controlled code
Writing security documentation in a word processor after deployment
Manually configuring security through graphical consoles
Eliminating all security to speed up deployment
explanation: Security as code defines policies as version-controlled code for automation.

text: How does attack surface analysis reduce risk?
options: By systematically identifying and minimizing all interaction points
By increasing entry points to distribute attack load
By ignoring external interfaces and focusing on internal code
By removing all user-facing features
explanation: Attack surface analysis identifies and minimizes points where attackers could interact.

text: How does penetration testing differ from red team exercises?
options: Pen testing targets specific vulnerabilities; red teaming simulates full adversary campaigns
Both follow identical procedures with the same scope
Pen testing is broader than red team exercises always
Red teaming only tests physical security, never digital
explanation: Pen testing finds specific vulnerabilities; red teaming simulates comprehensive campaigns.

text: How does RASP differ from a traditional WAF?
